This image shows a dermatologist discussing the removal of a keloid, a type of skin disease characterized by a raised, rubbery, and sometimes painful growth. The keloid in the image is located on the labia and is challenging to remove due to its midline position. The dermatologist explains that the ideal time for treatment is within 24 hours, and they prefer to use superficial radiation, which focuses the energy at the skin level rather than passing through it completely. The radiation helps to target the core of the skin and the fibroblasts that produce the scar tissue. The dermatologist recommends radiation as part of the standard protocol for treating keloids, and it is often used in combination with surgery.

This image shows pyogenic granulomas, a type of skin disease characterized by red, raised, and sometimes painful lesions, commonly found on the hands, face, and mouth. The cause of pyogenic granulomas is not fully understood, but they may be related to trauma, infection, or certain medical conditions. Treatment options include surgical excision, radiation therapy, or laser therapy, depending on the size and location of the lesion.

This image discusses cellulitis, an unsightly skin condition that causes red, swollen, and painful areas on the skin, often accompanied by fever and chills. It is typically caused by bacterial infection and can be treated with antibiotics.

This image depicts red, dilated veins on the face, which can be a sign of rosacea, a skin condition characterized by redness and inflammation of the face, often accompanied by pimples and a burning sensation. The exact cause of rosacea is unknown, but it is believed to be related to an overactive immune response and blood vessels that dilate easily. Treatment options include topical creams, oral medications, and lifestyle changes to manage symptoms and prevent flare-ups.

This image shows a globular pattern of nevus cells in the dermis, which are collections of immature cells in nests. These can be found in both congenital and acquired nevi, with the dermal component usually only present in acquired nevi. The architecture of the nevus cells is crucial for diagnosis, and dysplastic nevus should no longer be reported, instead using the term "atypical" and discussing architecture and cytology.

This image shows a skin disease called lichen planus, which appears as polygonal, reticulate, and lacy white lesions on the skin. It can cause itching and is often distributed on the flexion or aspect of the body. Lichen planus can also affect hair and nails, leading to hair loss and a blue-gray hue. Histopathology reveals hyperkeratosis, wedge-shaped hypergranulosis, and basal cell degeneration as key findings in the disease.</s> This image shows the infiltration of lymphocytes against the epidermis in keratinocytes, leading to the formation of seeded bodies in lichen planus. The treatment for lichen planus includes corticosteroids, either in oral or topical form.

This image shows a plaque on the skin, which may indicate an inflammatory skin disease such as psoriasis or eczema, characterized by red, scaly patches covered with thick, dry skin. This image shows a viral illness called Pteriasis Rosea, which causes a non-specific rash that can later develop into a Christmas tree-like pattern. The rash starts as a large, well-demarcated herald patch and is followed by a diffuse, patchy rash throughout the body. The illness is usually self-limiting and takes two to three months to resolve. Treatment options include topical steroids, hydrocortisone, or Benadryl, but caution is advised for young adults as Benadryl may have sedating effects.

This image shows a 70-year-old man with large, dotted vessel-filled nodules on his back, which is a challenging case of nevoid melanoma. Dermoscopy reveals a vascular pattern with corkscrew, dotted, and linear irregular vessels, as well as asymmetrically located bruish areas. Another case presents as a papule on the leg with a typical vascular pattern, and dermoscopy diagnoses it as a multicomponent nevoid melanoma. Combining clinical and dermoscopy examinations can help in accurately diagnosing nevoid melanoma, which may be more difficult for pathologists to distinguish from other types of melanoma.

This image shows lymphoid tissue prolaborations in the germinal center, which are characterized by proliferations of lymphoid cysts with a centrally hyperchromatic nucleus. These prolaborations can also be found in Warthin tumors, which are the most common papillary foam tumors of the oral mucosa, arising at the site where minor salivary gland ducts merge with the surface of the epithelium. The tumors can present with exophytic growth and clinical features similar to squamous cell carcinoma or sequamus papilloma. Inverted ductal papilloma is another tumor that has been described in the minor salivary gland of adults, characterized by inverted growth inside the tissue.

This image discusses the issue of syringoma recurrence, a skin disease characterized by the appearance of flesh-colored bumps. The cause of recurrence is often due to incomplete removal of the syringoma during treatment, as not many clinics understand the anatomy of the disease. The recurrence rate is reported to be 70-50% after removal. To minimize recurrence, it is important to remove the syringoma completely and understand its anatomy. Proper treatment methods, such as those taught in videos, can help effectively remove the syringoma.

This image shows grouped vesicles on the perioral area, which are characteristic of herpes labialis, a viral infection. The diagnosis can be easily made in an image-based question. Additional characteristic points include a history of recurrences and fever as a prodrome. In herpes infections, acantholytic cells or zang cells, which are keratinocytes that have lost their intercellular connections, can be seen along with multinucleate gen cells, which are a result of ballooning degeneration of keratinocytes. These cells form a single mass with multiple nuclei.

This image shows a 42-year-old male with painful, gradually enlarged subcutaneous nodules in his elbows, which restricted joint movement. The diagnosis is tendon xanthoma, a classical feature of type 2a hyperlipoproteinemia. These nodules form due to abnormal LDL receptors, leading to increased circulating levels of LDL cholesterol that deposit over the tendons, causing tendon xanthoma. Treatment involves oral steroids to manage the life-threatening condition for both the patient and the newborn.

This image shows the appearance of mycosis fungoides, a type of skin disease characterized by flat, scaly patches that can be found in a "bathing trunk" distribution on the buttocks. The patches may improve in sunlight or summertime, but can also thicken and progress over time.

This image shows characteristic lesions of lichen planus on the volar aspects of the wrist, including erythematous plaques and papules. Lichen planus is often associated with antihypertensive medication use and has no systemic associations. It can be differentiated from other dermatosis such as psoriasis and severe dermatitis based on clinical characteristics. Drug-induced lichen planus can improve with drug cessation, while idiopathic cases have a course of remission and relapse. Sub-angual hyperkeratosis is an increase in nail tissue height, causing the nail bed to lift.

This image displays three skin lesions: trichofolliculoma, trichodinoma, and trichopithelioma. Trichofolliculoma features a large central cyst filled with keratin and branching epithelial cells, while trichodinoma consists of numerous cysts surrounded by eosinophilic cells. Trichopithelioma is characterized by basaloid cells with peripheral palisading and occasional cysts.

This image depicts deep seated folliculitis, a skin disease characterized by the involvement of the lower part of the hair follicle, which is filled with pus and undergoes subparation and abscess formation, leading to peripyretis, Staphylogenes, and scarring. This image shows a dermatologist explaining the differences between lichen planus, psoriasis, and tinea corporis, including their appearance, causes, and treatment options. Lichen planus presents as pronatic purplish papules or plaques with extreme itching, while psoriasis manifests as well-defined erythematous micaceous scaly plaques over extensor surfaces. Tinea corporis presents as annular lesions that are extremely itchy and can be ruled out using a negative KOH test. The dermatologist also mentions that pterosis rosa can be differentiated from these conditions based on their eruption being moderately pruritic and lasting 6 to 12 weeks, which tends to resolve spontaneously.

This image displays a royal family with a famous sex-linked disease, Mophilia, which is carried out by the X chromosome. Sex-linked traits are traits whose alleles are located on sex chromosomes, and they can be either X-linked or Y-linked, depending on the sex of the individual. Most Y-linked mutations lead to sterility and cannot be inherited, while X-linked traits can be passed down from one generation to another.

This image shows a patient with immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP), a quantitative platelet disorder characterized by a decrease in platelet count. In ITP, there is an increase in platelet aggregation against autoantibodies, leading to clot formation in the spleen. The condition can be classified as acute or chronic based on the duration of symptoms. Acute ITP is more severe and can affect both adults and children, while chronic ITP is less severe and may be secondary to other disorders like systemic lupus erythematosus or lymphoma. Clinical symptoms of ITP include hemorrhagic bullae, spleen enlargement, and shortened bleeding time. A pathologist can diagnose ITP by checking the platelet count and prothrombin time in a blood sample.</s> This image depicts a skin disease called immune thrombocytopenic purpura, which is characterized by the presence of autoantibodies against RBCs and a normal prothrombin time. Treatment options include splenectomy, which is the best and permanent option for patients with this condition, especially in children. The spleen plays a role in the formation of autoantibodies and their interaction with antigens, and removing the spleen can prevent the destruction of RBCs by macrophages.

Anthrax is caused by the bacterium Bacillus anthracis, which was used in biological warfare. The disease can be diagnosed through lab tests such as blood agar and selective media with penicillin. Treatment options include antibiotics such as Xibact and Vacumab, as well as monoclonal antibodies like raxibacumab and amoxicillin-clavulanate.

Sidofavir is an antiviral drug that can be injected intralesionally, while Avastin is a monoclonal antibody that can be administered intralesionally or intravenously. However, both drugs have potential side effects and may not be suitable for all patients. The dermatologist also mentions other treatment options such as interferon alpha 2A, vitamin A, acyclovir, photodynamic therapy, Thuja, and Japanese mushroom extract AHCC compound.

This image shows a dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP), a type of tumor characterized by spindle cells with thin, wavy collagen fibers and a cellular appearance. It tends to be more uniform in appearance than other cellular tumors, and diagnosis can be made based on cellularity and growth pattern. DFSP can infiltrate the fat and epidermis, but does not trap significant amounts of collagen. It is often difficult to distinguish from other tumors with wavy cells, but can be distinguished by its uniform, stellate cells and the absence of keloidal collagen trapping.

This image describes sarcoidosis as a potential skin disease characterized by the formation of small, non-cancerous lumps called granulomas, which can affect various organs including the eyes, causing uveitis. The exact cause of sarcoidosis is unknown, but it is thought to be an autoimmune disorder where the body's immune system attacks healthy tissue. Treatment options may include medications to reduce inflammation and suppress the immune system.

Treatment options include topical creams, ointments, or corticosteroids, as well as phototherapy or immunosuppressive medications in severe cases.

This image shows an indurated skin disease characterized by thickened, hardened skin. The cause of this condition is not entirely clear, but it may be related to an underlying medical condition or an allergic reaction. Treatment options may include topical creams, ointments, or steroid injections, as well as lifestyle changes to manage the underlying cause.

This image shows a close-up of a person's skin with a focus on the deposition of IgG and CD3 molecules, which indicate the activity of a disease not correlated with the activity of the disease. The treatment involves using a high dose of a vigorous or gaseous agent to control the disease.

This image discusses various methods to manage odor and exudate in patients with skin diseases, including the use of saline dressings, deodorant absorption, room deodorizers, odor masking, aromatherapy oils, puritas, cool hydrogel sheets, and capsicin ointments. It also mentions the importance of prevention and maintaining intact skin to avoid stressing adherence and provoking bleeding.

This image shows areas affected by hyperinsulinism, a condition characterized by excessive insulin production, which can cause skin thickening and darkening. The causes of hyperinsulinism are not fully understood, but it can be related to obesity, genetics, and certain medical conditions. Treatment options may include lifestyle changes, medications, and in some cases, surgery.

This image shows a person with a skin disease characterized by a red, raised, and itchy rash on their face. The cause of the disease is not clear, but it may be an allergic reaction to a certain substance. Treatment options include topical creams and ointments to reduce inflammation and itching, as well as avoiding the triggering substance.

This image describes candidiasis, a common fungal infection caused by Candida species such as Candida albicans, tropicalis, and glabrata, characterized by its white, yeast-like appearance on the skin, mucous membranes, and nails.

This image shows an excessive amount of blood flowing through the skin, which could be a sign of an underlying skin disease such as rosacea or lupus, characterized by redness and inflammation. The exact cause of this condition is not yet fully understood, but it may be triggered by factors such as genetics, environmental exposures, or certain medications. Treatment options may include topical creams or oral medications to reduce inflammation and redness, as well as lifestyle changes to avoid triggers.
This image depicts a skin disease characterized by white, starry patches on the skin.
This image depicts a dermatologist discussing the use of lasers and angiograms in treating vascularized retina, with unclear outcomes and the importance of informed decision-making.

This image shows a pyogenic granuloma, a red papule or nodule commonly found on the skin of young patients, usually affecting children on the face. It is also known as teleangiectatic granuloma or reactive lobular angioma and is considered a form of angioma development.

This image depicts a 52-year-old Caucasian male with a new, firm, asymptomatic lesion on his left third toe. The lesion appeared six months ago and was initially mistaken for Nevi, but upon closer examination, it was determined to be a separate entity. The patient has no history of skin cancer and is undergoing a routine skin check.

This image shows a basal cell carcinoma, also known as a rodent ulcer, characterized by a pearly papule with a central ulceration and dilated blood vessels (telangiectasia) on the face above the angle of the mouth. The tumor is caused by a sonic hedgehog gene mutation and is commonly found on the face, particularly above the line from the earlobe to the angle of the mouth. Microscopically, there is a proliferation of basaloid cells and peripheral tumor cells in islands, with a radial arrangement and parallel long axis, which is diagnostic for basal cell carcinoma.

This image shows a viral illness called Pteriasis Rosea, which typically affects older kids, teenagers, and young adults. It starts with a fever, headache, and generalized weakness, followed by the development of a large, well-demarcated herald patch. The rash spreads throughout the body, resembling a Christmas tree pattern, and is usually itchy. The illness is self-limiting and usually takes two to three months to resolve. Treatment options include topical steroids, hydrocortisone, or trimicillin ointment, and antihistamines like Benadryl. However, if the rash is accompanied by palm or sole rashes, an RPR test may be necessary to rule out syphilis.
This image describes patients with skin diseases that can be treated with immunomodulation.

This image shows the results of fractional radio frequency micro needling, a non-ablative treatment that improves static wrinkles, enhances epidermal barrier function, and can be used on the lower eyelid and around the eyes. The treatment is effective in improving skin texture and reducing stretch marks and acne, with minimal downtime. It can be combined with other treatments like PRP injection for better results.

This image shows a scaly lesion, which may indicate a skin disease such as psoriasis or eczema, characterized by red, dry, and flaky patches covered in scales. The cause of this condition can be genetic or environmental factors, and treatment options include topical creams, light therapy, and medications.
This image shows an example of erythema annularis centrifugum, a skin disease characterized by a ring-like red rash with a central clearing, often caused by allergic reactions or irritants.

This image shows mast cells, which are a type of immune system cell that can be associated with skin diseases such as allergic reactions or mastocytosis, appearing as round or oval-shaped cells with a darker color compared to normal skin cells.

This image shows a viral illness called pteriasis rosea, which causes a non-specific rash that can later develop into a Christmas tree-like pattern. The rash typically starts with a herald patch and spreads throughout the body, followed by fever, headache, and generalized weakness. The treatment involves using topical steroids, hydrocortisone, or Benadryl for itching and inflammation, and monitoring for any secondary syphilis.

This image describes actinic keratosis, also known as solar keratosis, as a skin condition characterized by raised, scaly patches typically found on sun-exposed areas, caused by prolonged UV exposure, and can be treated with topical creams or surgical removal.
This image depicts a skin disease characterized by the presence of vessels that do not cauterize like other vessels, requiring patience and a different approach compared to aneurysm surgery.
This image shows the characteristic features of neurofibromatosis type 1, including cafe au lait spots, axillary or inguinal freckling, and cutaneous neurofibromas.
This image depicts a patient with a skin disease characterized by a red, raised, and itchy rash. The cause of the disease is unknown, but it can be treated with topical creams and antihistamines.

This image depicts epithelioid sarcomas, a type of skin disease characterized by their appearance as round or oval-shaped cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm, commonly found in the hand. These sarcomas may metastasize to lymph nodes and are often misdiagnosed, requiring wide resection and radiation treatment.
This image shows an actinic keratosis, a precancerous skin lesion that may disappear but often returns with sun exposure. It is usually removed as a precaution to prevent skin cancer.

This image displays various capillary malformations in CMAVM, which can range from small, pink, and circular to larger, darker, and oval in shape, highlighting the diverse presentation of this skin condition.

This image shows a person with a skin disease characterized by red, itchy patches on their skin. The cause of the disease is unknown, but it may be related to an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical creams and oral medications to reduce inflammation and suppress the immune system.

This image discusses various types of acanthosis nigricans, including obesity-related, drug-induced, and malignancy-associated variants. Treatment options for obesity-related acanthosis nigricans include weight loss, while drug-induced acanthosis nigricans may resolve when the drug is ceased. Hereditary variants may or may not fade with age, and malignancy-associated variants may or may not fade after a malignancy is removed.

This image depicts necrobiotic xanthogranuloma, a skin disease characterized by cholesterol clefts, which can appear as yellow or brown nodules on the skin. The cause of this condition is unknown, but treatment options may include surgical excision and topical steroids.

This image describes keloids as raised, thick, and rubbery scars that form over a wound and can be caused by skin injury, burns, or surgery. Treatment options include steroid injections, laser therapy, and surgical excision.

This image describes Bechet's disease, characterized by mobile, transient, and cold hyperpion occurring in a relatively white eye with a flat surface, contrasting with HLA B27-associated uveitis with immobile hyperpion due to high fibrin content and intense AC reaction, causing a severely painful acute red eye.

This image shows a person with cheeks and fins that appear to be made of petroleum products, which can cause a skin disease known as seborrheic dermatitis, characterized by red, greasy, and itchy patches. The cause of this condition is not entirely clear, but it may be related to an overproduction of oil by the sebaceous glands or an immune response to the oil and dead skin cells. Treatment options include topical creams, shampoos, and dietary changes to manage symptoms and prevent flare-ups.

This image shows a subepidermal blistering with eosinophils, a skin condition characterized by raised, red, and inflamed patches, often caused by allergic reactions or skin irritants, and can be treated with topical steroids or immunosuppressive medications.
This image describes carpal tunnel syndrome, a condition where the median nerve is compressed within the carpal tunnel, leading to loss of innervation to the thenar muscles and subsequent atrophy.
This image shows a multilobular tumor of bone, characterized by small, round areas seen under a microscope.

This image shows a case of intraligamentous dermatitis, a skin condition characterized by red, raised, and itchy patches on the skin, often caused by an autoimmune response and treated with topical steroids and immunosuppressants.
This image shows inflammatory bumps and pustules, which may indicate an skin disease such as acne or psoriasis, and can be treated with topical medications or light therapy.
This image shows a patient with numerous large, exophytic papular nodules on their legs, which are characteristic of a certain skin disease.

This image shows a patient with a skin cancer excision and advancement and rotation flaps. The patient has done well post-op and radiation was considered but ultimately not used due to concerns about skin changes caused by radiation.

This image depicts the Millions Ears Sign, a skin condition characterized by the presence of numerous, small, raised bumps, which commonly affects immunocompromised individuals or those with a history of trauma.
This image describes cat scratch disease, a bacterial infection that can cause skin symptoms such as redness, swelling, and bumps, and is typically treated with antibiotics.

This image depicts a patient with psoriasis who was treated with systemic steroids. After stopping the treatment, the patient developed universally red, scaly skin with plaques losing their margins, indicating a likely case of erythrodermic psoriasis. It is important to note that steroid-induced psoriasis can cause either erythrodermic or pustular forms, but in this case, it is clear that it is the erythrodermic form. The correct answer is option number 4.

This image shows an area of the skin with a red, raised, and inflamed appearance, possibly indicating an infection or skin disease such as eczema or psoriasis, and may require treatment with topical creams or oral medications.

This image shows a patient with a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash that appears as small, raised bumps. The cause of this condition is not yet clear, but it may be related to an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical creams and oral medications to reduce inflammation and itching.

This image shows a scar resulting from a pentagon excision, which is a surgical procedure used to treat skin diseases such as basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma. The scar is typically characterized by a triangular or pentagonal shape and may be pink, red, or brown in color. The cause of the defect can be attributed to the removal of cancerous cells or excess skin during the excision, and treatment options may include further surgery, radiation therapy, or topical medications, depending on the severity and location of the scar.

This image shows a patient with pteriasis versicolor, a skin disease characterized by patches of light and dark skin, caused by overproduction of melanin. Treatment options include topical creams and laser therapy.
This image shows a patient with a rash consisting of multiple small, annular lesions with minimal scaling, indicating a likely diagnosis of pityriasis rosea.

This image shows a surgical procedure to remove a skin tumor, along with any surrounding tissue, while preserving the surrounding structures. The tumor appears as a raised, potentially discolored mass, and is typically caused by exposure to harmful UV rays or other environmental factors. Treatment options may include surgery, radiation therapy, or topical creams, depending on the severity and location of the tumor.
This image shows a person with a nose that is no longer as bumpy as it was before, likely due to successful treatment of a skin condition.

This image discusses congenital syphilis, a type of skin disease caused by the bacteria Tryponema pallidum. It can present with various symptoms such as rash, vesicular bullous rash, rhinitis, snuffles, hepatosplenomegaly, osteochondritis, periostitis, lymphadenopathy, anemia, and thrombocytopenia. In late congenital syphilis, Hutchison's triad may be present, which includes Hutchison's teeth, deafness, and interstitial keratitis. Management typically involves treatment with benzathine penicillin, with procaine penicillin used for neurosyphilis.
This image describes actinic keratosis, a precancerous skin condition characterized by flat, scaly lesions, typically found on sun-exposed areas, which can progress to skin cancer if left untreated.

This image shows fluid-filled lesions that may bleed into them, leading to hemocytin deposits. These deposits could indicate aneurysmal bone cysts, which are distinguished from other types of bone cysts by their radiographic features and abundance of granulation tissue and giant cells. The cystic spaces in aneurysmal bone cysts have lots of new bone formation and robust reactive osteoid production by reactive osteoblasts, making them appear busier and more active compared to other types of bone cysts.
This image shows a lesion of discoid lupus with depigmentation in the central area and hyperpigmentation at the periphery, accompanied by scarring.
This image shows two examples of melanomas with distinct shiny white structures, which are not visible in non-polarized light mode.

This image shows the results of a study that collected specimens before and after surgical debridement of a skin disease, analyzing them through microarrays, growing primary cells, and conducting histology and immunocytochemistry tests to understand the disease's causes and potential treatments.

This image shows an upper and lower split of the skin, which can be a sign of certain skin diseases such as dermatitis or psoriasis, characterized by red, itchy, and scaly patches, and can be treated with topical creams or oral medications.

This image discusses Bashet's syndrome, a mixed autoimmune and auto-anti-inflammatory disorder characterized by skin manifestations such as erythroderma, papules, and plaques, along with various systemic manifestations. The lecture covers the history, etiology, pathogenesis, clinical features, and different presentations of the syndrome, as well as the ocular manifestations and their severity.

This image provides information on Mepilex, a treatment option for patients with skin diseases such as diabetic foot ulcers and venous leg ulcers, characterized by a hydrocolloid dressing that promotes healing and reduces the risk of infection.

This image describes dystrophic calcification, a type of pathological calcification where calcium deposition occurs in dead and degenerated tissue, such as in Cassius necrosis and liquefaction necrosis. Dystrophic calcification can be seen in tuberculosis of the lung and chronic abscess formation, and can be distinguished from metastatic calcification, which occurs in different organs of the body.

This image describes three types of Congenital Tosses: skin eyelid crease absent, tautic eyelid retraction with jaw opening, and myogenic tosses. The first type is characterized by an absence of the skin eyelid crease, while the second type involves retraction of the tautic eyelid with jaw opening due to misdirected cross innervation between the oculomotor nerve and the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve. The third type, myogenic tosses, is usually associated with diplopia and restricted ocular movement. Surgical procedures such as corneal transplantation, tarsal plate axion, levator resection, and eyelid sling procedures may be indicated for different degrees of tosses.

This image shows a dermatologist discussing different types of dermoscopes, their pros and cons, and their uses. They mention Joula's loops, which have a lower price point but can be difficult to take pictures with, and optical or hiney scopes, which are more robust but can be expensive. The dermatologist also recommends the derm light DL four as their favorite dermoscope, praising its quality and ease of use.
This image shows dilated vessels and solar elastosis, which are signs of sun damage and aging in the skin.

This image shows a case of psoriasis, a skin disease characterized by red, scaly patches, and is caused by an overactive immune system. Treatment options include topical creams, light therapy, and medications.

This image shows a dog with numerous skin lesions, which may indicate an underlying skin disease such as eczema or dermatitis. The dog's fractious behavior and the presence of these lesions have prompted the owner to consider blocking them.

This image shows that topical isotretinoin can help reduce wrinkles, but it did not have a significant effect on the appearance of sunspots (solar lentigos). Topical tretinoin may be helpful in lightening sunspots.

This image shows a dermatologist explaining the differences between lichen planus, psoriasis, and tinea corporis, and how to differentiate them based on their appearance, causes, and symptoms. Lichen planus presents as pronatic purplish papules or plaques with extreme itching, while psoriasis manifests as well-defined erythematous micaceous scaly plaques over the extensor surfaces. Tinea corporis presents as annular lesions that are extremely itchy and can be ruled out using a negative potassium hydroxide (KOH) test. The dermatologist also mentions that pterosis rosa can be differentiated from these conditions and provides links to previous videos for further explanation.

This image describes various types of cutaneous drug reactions, including Stevens-Johnson, TEN, DRESS, morbilliform drug eruption, fixed drug eruption, and acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP), with different time frames for onset of symptoms ranging from hours to weeks.

This image shows a girl with a PIK3CA mutation, which can cause lymphatic abnormalities and overgrowth. The skin disease may not be visible at birth but can develop later on. The girl has hypotrophy, which is characterized by a thinner leg, and may also have capillary malformations. The workup for this condition is different depending on whether there is overgrowth or normal growth.

This image depicts a common skin condition often seen in individuals who have visited tropical regions, characterized by a red, itchy rash with small blisters. The cause of this condition is typically exposure to certain irritants or allergens found in the environment, and treatment options include topical creams or ointments, antihistamines, and avoiding triggers.

This image shows a cat with a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash. The cause of the disease is insect bites, and treatment options include topical creams and preventive measures to avoid further bites.

This image shows a person with a red, itchy rash covering their entire body, indicating a possible case of eczema, an inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry, scaly patches and a burning sensation. The cause of eczema is often unknown, but it can be triggered by allergens, irritants, or stress. Treatment options include topical creams, ointments, and phototherapy, as well as lifestyle changes to avoid triggers.

This image shows a patient with mycosis fungoides, a type of skin disease characterized by patches and plaques surrounding a tumor, which can mimic anaplastic large cell lymphoma but is easily diagnosed due to its distinct appearance.

This image shows various treatments for skin diseases, including topical creams, oral medications, and light therapy, which can help manage symptoms and improve the appearance of conditions such as acne, eczema, and psoriasis.

This image shows skin manifestations of TSC, a genetic disorder characterized by the presence of benign tumors, which can be difficult to distinguish from other skin lesions not associated with the disease.

This image shows a keloid, a type of scar, on the sternum. Keloids are raised, thick, and pink or brown in appearance and can form after wound healing. The exact cause is unknown, but it may be related to an overactive immune response or genetic predisposition. Treatment options include steroid injections, laser therapy, and surgical excision.
This image shows hemociderotic dermatofibromas, which are aneurysmal dermatofibromas with a brownish, blueish, or rainbow appearance, often lacking peripheral reticulation.

This image describes skin diseases such as atopic dermatitis, eczema, and psoriasis, with their appearances, causes, and treatment options. Atopic dermatitis appears in early childhood and often subsides in adulthood, while eczema can develop at any age and often affects symmetrical areas of the body. Psoriasis can also develop at any age and often appears in areas with skin-on-skin contact, such as the buttocks and groin. Both conditions can cause scaly, flaky skin.

This image describes forehead plaque, also known as a chagrin patch, which appears as a raised, red, and sometimes scaly lesion on the forehead, and can be caused by skin irritation or inflammation. Treatment options may include topical creams or ointments, or in severe cases, oral medications or light therapy.

This image depicts generalized pustular psoriasis, a rare and extreme form of the disease characterized by sterile pustules, with some patients transitioning from plaque psoriasis before entering the pustular stage, while others enter the pustular stage without prior plaque psoriasis.

This image depicts aggressive lesions often seen in the nasopharynx area of Asians in South America, previously known as lethal midline granuloma. These lesions are often Epstein-Barr virus positive and stain positive for CD2, CD56, and CD43, but not for CD3. They contain cytotoxic proteins and are effectively treated.

This image refers to a treatment that has been successfully used for the treatment of Boyle's disease, a rare skin condition characterized by the appearance of reddish-brown, scaly patches on the skin. The exact cause of the disease is unknown, but it is believed to be an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options may include topical creams, phototherapy, and immunosuppressive medications.
This image shows a skin lesion with a hemorrhagic crust, indicating an ulcer and possibly epidermal loss, along with subtle alopecia in a short-coat breed.

This image shows a close-up view of a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with a raised, scaly appearance, caused by an allergic reaction to certain foods, and can be treated with over-the-counter creams and avoiding the trigger foods.

This image shows the life cycle of Strongyloides stercoralis, a parasitic worm that can cause strongyloidiasis, a type of skin disease characterized by itching and rash. The disease is mainly spread through contact with infected feces, and the parasite can penetrate the skin, causing lesions. The life cycle involves three stages: the larval stage, which hatches from eggs in the feces and transforms into the adult form; the adult form, which can be parasitic or free-living; and the female, which lives in the small intestine and releases eggs that are passed in the stool. The disease is predominantly found in tropical and subtropical regions and can cause a range of symptoms, including abdominal pain, diarrhea, and weight loss. Treatment options include antiparasitic medications and supportive care.

This image describes pseudocopoxus, a common infectious disease caused by the para pox virus, which can cause skin lesions such as erythema, edema, and painful teeth. It is transmitted through skin abrasions and can affect both animals and humans. Treatment typically involves antiseptic and antibiotic applications, as well as the use of ointments containing kurkuma longa in butter. Another skin disease mentioned is bovine papular dermatitis, which is also caused by the para pox virus and can cause red or brownish lesions on the skin, especially in muscle nostrils and lips. The treatment for this disease is symptomatic and there is no known cure.
This image describes the four stages of a skin disease, which include erythematous papules and vesicles, verruca stage, hyperpigmentation stage, and hypopigmentation stage.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by redness, roughness, and darker skin tone. The cause of this condition is not clear, but it may be related to inflammation or an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical creams, light therapy, and immunosuppressive medications, depending on the severity and underlying cause of the disease.

This image depicts a young gentleman from Naples, Italy, who has had a skin lesion since childhood. The lesion is described as being brown and shaped like a dome. The cause of the lesion is unknown, and there is no available treatment option.
This image depicts a skin cancer, not on the face, located in the upper layer of the epidermis and treated through electrodesiccation and curettage.

This image shows a woman with a skin disease characterized by red, inflamed patches on her skin. The cause of the disease is unknown, but it may be an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical creams and corticosteroids to reduce inflammation and prevent flare-ups.

This image shows skin lesions typically found in sun-exposed areas of the face, such as folds and periorbital areas, which can be indicative of dermatomyositis. These lesions may also involve the ears, neck, knees, and elbows, and can present as telangiectatic erythema or macular patches. The exact cause of these lesions is not always clear, but sun exposure and secondary processes like seborrheic dermatitis may play a role.

This image shows a patient with a severe case of cellulitis, characterized by intense erythema on the left leg up to the knee, peripheral vision loss, and perifollicular purpura on the abdomen and thighs. The patient is also anemic due to scurvy, with vitamin C levels measured as non-existent.

This image shows a close-up view of a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with a scaly texture, caused by an allergic reaction to a particular substance. Treatment options include topical creams and antihistamines to reduce inflammation and itching.

This image shows an ulcerated nodule on the scalp with superficial crusting and scale, accompanied by two pinkish papules. A biopsy of one of the papules reveals metastatic squamous cell carcinoma on the scalp with the primary tumor and two metastases. The absence of epidermal connection is a clue to the metastatic nature of the disease.

This image describes wart removal using laser treatment at a clinic, involving cautery, radio frequency, or CO2 laser. The treatment removes visible warts, leaving a shallow pit that heals in two to three weeks. Post-treatment, use antibiotic cream, mild cleansing lotion, and sunscreen to prevent pigmentation and infection. The treated area may be raw for four to five days, causing mild pain or discomfort, which can be alleviated with antibiotic cream application.

This image describes various benign skin growths, including moles (nevi), skin tags, solar lentigines (liver spots), and seborrheic keratoses. Moles are generally symmetrical, uniformly colored, and smaller than a pencil eraser, while skin tags are fleshy flaps of skin that can be easily removed if desired. Solar lentigines are brown spots caused by sun exposure, and while they are benign, they can be treated if necessary. Seborrheic keratoses appear as dry, raised, and sometimes wart-like growths.

This image depicts the treatment of Actinomyces israelii infections, which involve a combination of antibiotics and surgery for several months to eradicate the infection and potentially remove affected tissue.
This image describes rosacea, a skin disease characterized by redness, sensitivity, burning, stinging, bloodshot eyes, crusty eyelids, and a feeling of something in the eye.
This image shows freckles or epidermal growths with a reticular dark brown to light brown pattern, often with moth-eaten edges, and can sometimes have a homogenous pattern.

This image shows hyperkeratosis, a skin condition also known as lichen simplex chronicus, affecting adults aged 30-50. It is characterized by thickened, scaly skin and is often associated with leg problems and muscle pain. The inflammation is not as severe as in other cases, but treatment options may include topical creams or ointments to reduce the thickness and discomfort.

This image shows a close-up view of an atypical fibroxanthoma, a type of skin disease characterized by an ulcerative growth with an atypical appearance. The image highlights the broad nodular shape and the presence of an ulceration on top of the growth. Atypical fibroxanthomas are often misdiagnosed as other skin diseases, such as melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, or leiomyosarcoma. To accurately diagnose atypical fibroxanthomas, a series of immunohistochemistry tests are performed to rule out other potential skin diseases. The image also mentions pleomorphic dermal sarcomas, which are a type of soft tissue sarcoma that can be difficult to distinguish from atypical fibroxanthomas.

This image shows a keloid on a woman's labia that was successfully removed by a dermatologist. The treatment involved using superficial radiation, which targets the core of the skin where the keloid cells are located, rather than passing through the entire skin. The dermatologist explained that they typically recommend radiation as part of their protocol for treating keloids, although the strength and voltage may vary depending on the individual case.

This image demonstrates the importance of dermoscopy in distinguishing benign and malignant pigmented regions, with typical dermoscopic features of seborrheic warts or keratosis appearing as light brown fingerprint-like parallel structures.

This image shows inflammation by mononuclear cells with specific features such as increased gene cells, histocytic infiltration, and multinucleotide cells, which can be associated with skin diseases like basal cell carcinoma. The treatment options for this condition may include immunotherapy using CD68 and CD1A.

This image describes anti-M1 receptor antagonists, which are used to treat skin diseases such as atopic dermatitis, characterized by red, itchy, and inflamed skin, and can be caused by an overactive immune response to allergens like dust mites and pet dander. Treatment options include topical steroids, immunosuppressants, and antihistamines.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a large, raised, red flap of skin, potentially extending to the scalp, with causes such as an autoimmune disorder or skin injury, and treatment options including surgical removal and topical steroids.

This image shows a grenzone, a type of skin disease characterized by a red, scaly rash that typically appears on the shins or lower legs. The cause of grenzone is unknown, but it can be treated with topical creams or ointments.

This image shows a person with a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash on their face. The cause of the disease is unknown, but treatment options include topical creams and avoiding irritants.

This image shows a dog with hair loss around its right eye, likely due to mange mites. The mites are typically acquired from the mother dog, but can cause problems when the immune system is weak and allows the mites to overrun. The vet diagnosed the dog's mange and treated it with oral medication for two months.
This image depicts a severe skin disease characterized by the gradual shedding of skin with even the slightest contact, indicating an underlying cause that requires proper treatment.
This image shows a patient with extensive, violaceous plaques and ulcers on their skin, which were treated with sclerotherapy.

This image shows an infant with impetigo, a common skin infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus, characterized by red, itchy lesions commonly found on the face, which can be transmitted through close physical contact or fomites.

This image shows a patient with low hemoglobin and anemia due to bleeding within a tumor, which was initially treated with medical therapy. The patient's platelet count stabilized and normalized, and fibrinogen levels increased to normal. Additionally, the D dimer decreased to normal levels, indicating improvement in the patient's condition. The patient also had cases of Kaposi sarcoma on the cheek, which is a type of skin cancer.
This image discusses Jeevantiyaadi Yamakam, a prescription for external application in the Kevalavatha stage, as the best choice for treating skin diseases.
This image shows a red, swollen, and painful patch on the skin, which may indicate an infection.

This image shows the clinical presentation of psoriatic lesions, which can include large numbers of different types of lesions such as psoriatic, C-line, and large arithmetus psoriatic lesions. Psoriasis is characterized by increased epidermal turnover, causing hypogranulosis due to the rapid turnover rate. Triggers for psoriatic lesions can be trauma, infection, or drugs such as beta blockers, ACE inhibitors, lithium, and interferons. Histopathology reveals changes in the epidermis, including acanthosis, elongation of reti ridges, neutrophilic abscesses, loss of granular layer, and thinning of suprapapillary plates.

This image shows genital molluscum, which appears as pearly shiny papules with central umbilication. It is important to differentiate between genital molluscum and other skin diseases like condyloma lata and acuminata, which are caused by the human papilloma virus (HPV). HPV is the commonest viral skin disease and can cause two types of warts, non-genital and genital. The genital wart is called condyloma acuminata, while the non-genital warts can be of four types, including Veruca vulgaris, plain wart, plantar wart, and filiform wart. It is crucial to know the strain of HPV that causes common wart, which is HPV2, and the predisposing factors for plantar wart, such as hyperhidrosis and mosaic infection.

This image shows that skin diseases caused by Vata predominantly have a dry, rough, and scaly appearance, and can be treated with measures to balance Vata in the body such as warmth, oil, and hydration.

This image shows a vine charcoal treatment for skin, which starts off dark and even, but becomes lighter as it is brushed out. The goal is to continue brushing until the treatment appears to stop releasing.

This image shows a classic presentation of levodermatous vasculitis, characterized by persistent venous ulcerations in the lower leg, primarily affecting women and more frequent in summer months. It is often associated with Renaud's phenomenon and acrocyanosis, and is caused by venous insufficiency. An ultrasound is the easiest and least invasive diagnostic tool, and further investigation may be necessary if ultrasound does not provide a clear answer.
This image depicts a heavyset individual with a loop around their neck, potentially indicating a pre-existing skin condition or medical concern that requires monitoring and management.

This image shows a kidney with a unique shape and color, which may indicate a skin disease such as neurofibromatosis, causing symptoms like disfiguration and pain. Treatment options may include surgery, medication, or laser therapy, depending on the severity of the condition.

This image shows a patient with a gray pseudomembrane in their throat, which is characteristic of diphtheria. Diphtheria is a bacterial infection caused by Corynebacterium diphtheriae and can lead to a range of symptoms, including sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and breathing problems. The infection can also cause a gray or white coating on the throat and tonsils, as seen in this image. This image showcases the work of Willen, considered the founding father of dermatology, who published the first textbook of dermatology with hand-painted plates. However, it is interesting to note that the lesions depicted in these illustrations are not the modern understanding of lupus, and there was a long-standing confusion between tuberculosis and syphilis.

This image shows a patient with immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP), a quantitative platelet disorder characterized by a decrease in platelet count. In ITP, there is hyperactivity and abnormality in the production of antibodies, leading to a decrease in platelet aggregation. The GP2B3A antibody is often involved in the interaction between antibodies and platelets, causing them to go to the spleen and be destroyed. ITP can be classified as acute or chronic, with acute ITP having a short duration history and being more severe than chronic ITP, which has a long duration history and is less severe. Chronic ITP can be secondary to other disorders such as systemic lupus erythematosus or lymphoma, and may affect offspring due to the placental barrier. Clinical symptoms of ITP include hemorrhagic bullae and an increased spleen size. A pathologist can diagnose ITP by checking the platelet count and prothrombin time in a blood sample.</s> This image shows a patient with type 2 hypersensitivity, which is a condition where the immune system attacks tissues. In this case, the patient has immune thrombocytopenic auto antibodies against RBCs, causing a decrease in platelet count. The spleen plays a crucial role in this condition, as it can cause autoantibodies to form and lead to the destruction of platelets. Splenectomy is the best option for treating patients with immune thrombocytopenic purpura, as it removes the spleen and prevents further autoantibody formation and destruction of platelets.

This image discusses various skin diseases and their potential connections to diabetes. Male pattern baldness is not directly related to diabetes, but there is a slight connection in women due to insulin resistance. Hydradenitis suppurativa is a condition characterized by sweat glands inflammation and oozing, often seen in diabetic patients. Blisters, also known as bullosis diabeticorum, can occur with diabetes and are often a result of poorly managed type 2 diabetes. Skin tags are also associated with insulin resistance and can be removed with lifestyle changes. Rosacea is a redness condition that can be mistaken for eczema or psoriasis. Xanthelasma are yellowish, scaly patches caused by cholesterol deposits, often found in people with diabetes. Acne vulgaris is a common skin condition that can be linked to diabetes, especially in those who consume high amounts of sugar and processed foods. Diabetic ulcers are non-healing open wounds that can be a sign of diabetes. To prevent and manage these skin diseases, it is important to maintain proper blood sugar control through diet and lifestyle changes.</s> This image shows various skin diseases, including dark patches (acanthosis nigricans), skin patches (necrobiosis lipoidica), male pattern baldness, hydradenitis suppurativa, blisters (bolosis diabeticorum), skin tags, rosacea, acne vulgaris, and ulcers (non-healing open wounds). To prevent or manage these conditions, it is important to maintain a healthy blood sugar level through diet and lifestyle choices, such as regular exercise, a low-carbohydrate diet, and managing stress.

This image discusses syringoma removal and recurrence, a common issue faced by patients who have had the skin growth removed with lasers or other methods. To effectively treat and prevent recurrence, it is important to remove the entire syringoma and understand its anatomy.

It is important to differentiate between these two conditions as solar lentigo is not a malignant tumor, while some seborrheic keratoses may require excision to rule out malignancy.

This image shows a multi-locular radiolucency in the mandible, which is a characteristic feature of an adult genic mixoma. It is a benign odontogenic tumor that can cause displacement and resorption of teeth, and is often associated with a lesion in the bone that extends into the soft tissues. The lesion appears as a soapy bubble on radiographs and is composed of loose, mixoid tissue with bony septae and thicker collagen bundles. The main issue in diagnosis is the wide clinical and histological differential diagnosis, which includes mixoid degeneration in other tumors, mixoid neurofibroma, and soft tissue myxoma.

This image depicts scabies, a highly contagious skin disease affecting people of all ages, socioeconomic statuses, and genders. Treatment involves applying permethrin cream from the neck down, the entire body, and using oral ivermectin, along with washing and vacuuming affected areas.

This image discusses a study in JAMA dermatology on the use of low-dose total skin electron beam therapy as part of a combination regimen for cesarean syndrome. The addition of low-dose total skin electron beam to systemic regimens, including extracorporeal photophoresis, bexarotene, and interferon, achieved long-term remissions in three patients with cesarean, who otherwise had an unusual response to treatment. Another paper showed that total skin electron beam combined with mogamalizumab resulted in a complete response in two patients with tolerable side effects.

This image describes solar elastosis, a skin condition that typically occurs in older individuals, characterized by the appearance of thick, leathery skin with a darker color. It is important to consult a professional for accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment options.

This image depicts asparagelosis, an invasive fungal infection characterized by pink and umbilicated papules and nodules, vesiculo-pustular lesions, and non-healing ulcers, which typically arises from direct inoculation or intravenous catheter use. It is often seen in advanced HIV disease with low CD4 count and is associated with weight loss, anemia, and lymphadenopathy. Treatment options include amphotericin or itraconazole, although relapse is common, and secondary prophylaxis with itraconazole is indicated until immune reconstitution with ART. Molluscum contagiosum is another cutaneous marker of advanced HIV disease, present as large, pearly, obligate, and confluent lesions predominantly on the face and trunk.

This image describes diffuse choroidal dystrophy, an autosomal dominant inherited condition that affects the retina and choroid, causing RPE modeling, hypopigmentation, and atrophy. It spares the center part of the retina initially but progresses to affect the entire retina, including the RPE and choreocapillaries. Visual fields show progressive concentric peripheral constriction, and ERG becomes non-detectable as the condition worsens.

This image describes infantile hemangioma, the most common tumor of infancy, characterized by a perinatal incidence of 1-2.6%, affecting approximately 4% of Caucasian infants in the first year of life with a lower incidence in darker-skinned infants. It has a female to male incidence of 1% and varies in male ratio. There is an increased incidence in preterm infants weighing less than 1,000 grams, and no clear genetic predisposition but several risk factors such as advanced maternal age, placental abnormalities, and multiple gestations. Infantile hemangiomas can be cutaneous or subcutaneous, and may be present at birth in 30-50% of cases.

The image also mentions mechanical tosses caused by increased weight of the upper eyelid or inflammation, and the importance of investigations, including corneal sensitivity and ocular motility, before surgery.

It is a member of the pox family and can cause skin surfaces to appear as if they are kissing. This image shows a close-up view of a skin disease characterized by the presence of elevated, domed-shaped lesions on the skin, commonly referred to as "chilo papules." This image shows a skin disease characterized by a broad, chunky stalk with a short appearance, potentially an ESD candidate, which was successfully exposed and treated by the dermatologist.

The causes of melanoma are largely unknown, but exposure to ultraviolet radiation from the sun and tanning beds is a significant risk factor. Treatment options include surgery, radiation therapy, and immunotherapy.

This image depicts a patient with a skin disease characterized by a painful, red, and swollen area on their trunk, caused by group A strep bacteria. The treatment involves removing dead tissue and administering antibiotics to eradicate the infection.

This image describes pre-septal and post-septal cellulitis, which are skin diseases that can cause swelling, pain, and vision loss. Pre-septal cellulitis is an infection of the soft tissue behind the orbital septum, while post-septal cellulitis involves an extension of the infection into the cranium. Treatment options include antibiotics, warm compresses, and surgical intervention if the infection is severe or does not respond to treatment. It is important to diagnose and treat these conditions early to prevent complications such as blindness, exposure keratopathy, and septicemia.</s> This image discusses the treatment options for preceptal and orbital cellulitis, which are skin diseases characterized by inflammation and swelling in the preceptal and orbital regions, respectively. Treatment typically involves antibiotics and corticosteroids to manage symptoms and prevent complications.
This image discusses mpox, a skin disease characterized by its distinctive rash and blisters, which can be caused by the monkeypox virus and is now known as non-offensive to certain populations.

This image shows a person with a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash on their face. The cause of the rash is an allergic reaction to a specific food. This image shows a case of a subtle skin disease characterized by a gradual change in skin color and texture, likely caused by prolonged sun exposure. Treatment options include sun protection, topical creams, and in severe cases, surgery.

This image describes C. ulcerans, a skin disease characterized by painful, ulcer-like lesions, typically appearing as circular or oval-shaped patches with a central necrotic area. Its cause is not fully understood, but it is believed to be transmitted through contact with infected animals or contaminated environments. Treatment options include topical and systemic antibiotics, as well as surgical excision of affected areas. Early detection and preventive measures, such as wearing protective clothing and avoiding contact with infected animals, are crucial to reduce the risk of infection.
This image shows a close-up view of a skin disease characterized by a red, raised, and inflamed appearance, likely caused by an infection. Treatment options include antibiotics and topical creams.

This image shows rosettes and diffuse, structureless pigmentation, which were verified as Bowen's disease, a skin condition characterized by the appearance of reddish or brownish patches on the skin. The cause of Bowen's disease is typically sun exposure, and treatment options include topical creams, phototherapy, and surgery in severe cases.

This image depicts a 31-year-old man with a symmetrical, flat-bottomed lesion on his skin, which may indicate an underlying skin disease such as psoriasis or eczema, and requires proper diagnosis and treatment.

This image depicts a person suffering from a severe skin disease characterized by a discolored, inflamed, and painful rash that covers a significant portion of their body. Treatment options include topical creams, oral medications, and in some cases, phototherapy or light therapy to help manage symptoms and promote healing.

The lesion was excised by an oral surgeon and a biopsy confirmed the diagnosis. Tobacco and sun exposure are significant risk factors for cancer, including squamous cell carcinoma, and treatment options may include excision, chemo, radiation, or a combination of these.

This image shows a successful treatment for a type of skin cancer called Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), which is characterized by a round or oval shape with a pink or brown color, and is often caused by exposure to ultraviolet radiation. The treatment option involves removing the cancerous cells and surrounding tissue to prevent the spread of the disease.

This image discusses segmental overgrowth, a skin disease characterized by uncontrolled growth of skin cells, and the use of off-label drugs such as serolimus, an mTOR inhibitor, which has been shown to improve symptoms in some patients. However, it is important for physicians to carefully monitor patients for potential adverse side effects when using these drugs.

This image shows HHV8, a virus that can cause skin diseases such as cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, with symptoms including raised, red, and scaly patches on the skin. The exact cause of HHV8 infection is unknown, but it is thought to be transmitted through close contact with an infected person. Treatment options for HHV8-related skin diseases may include medications, such as immunosuppressants, and in some cases, surgery or radiation therapy.

This image describes CMV, a member of the herpes virus family, which can be primary or reactivate and present with symptoms such as mononucleosis-like illness, mild AST and ALT elevations, hepatosplenomegaly, and rare cases of granulomus, cholestatic, or massive hepatic necrosis. In immune-compromised individuals, disseminated CMV can occur, and in people with HIV, it can cause AIDS cholangiopathy, a chronic cholestatic condition that mimics primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) but is not treated by antiretrovirals.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with a burning sensation, caused by an allergic reaction to an unknown substance, and can be treated with antihistamines and topical creams.

This image shows penile fore-dye spots, which are larger, classically located on either side of the midline of the penis. They are not hair follicles but glorified sebaceous glands. Treatment involves using a laser system to ablate the outer surface and laser any stubborn areas. For granular areas, a gentle light-based approach is used to de-epithelialize the area and remove the underlying sebaceous material. Healing for these spots may take a few weeks.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by red, inflamed patches on the skin, often accompanied by itching and burning sensations. The cause of this condition is not yet fully understood, but it may be triggered by allergies, irritants, or infections. Treatment options include topical creams, ointments, or oral medications, depending on the severity of the disease and the underlying cause.

This image describes the life cycle of Nasostoma parasite, a skin disease caused by ingesting raw or undercooked protein food sources contaminated with the parasite. Symptoms include localized, intermittent migratory swellings in the skin and subcutaneous tissues that may be painful, itchy, or red. These swellings are a telltale sign of the disease, and eosinophilia (increase in eosinophils in the blood) can also be a helpful diagnostic tool. Clinical features may include malaise, fever, urticaria, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and epigastric pain, along with skin manifestations such as migratory nodular paniculitis, soft tissue eruptions, boils, and nodules.
This image describes a skin disease that affects both men and women equally, commonly occurring in young people, mainly in their 20s and 30s, and often affecting the oral mucosa.

This image shows a 20mm x 10mm patch of skin with a red, scaly appearance, indicating possible psoriasis, which is caused by an overactive immune system and can be treated with topical creams and light therapy.

This image describes a type of common skin disease presenting as an ulcer, nodule, or indurated plaque in the oral cavity, differing from usual ulcers in lacking a nodule or raised lesion. It commonly occurs on the floor of the mouth and lateral or ventral side of the tongue, and may arise from pre-malignancy sites such as erythroplakia or leukoplakia.

This image depicts an autoimmune skin condition called vitiligo, characterized by patches of white skin with a rounded or oval shape. It is not commonly associated with other health issues like thyroid, pernicious anemia, or diabetes. There are different types of vitiligo, including focal vitiligo, which can be treated in primary care with a short course of topical steroids and tacrolimus 0.1%.

This image shows a lesion on the skin that appears to be a potential melanoma, characterized by a black or brown color and an irregular shape. The cause of this lesion is not yet clear, but early diagnosis and treatment are crucial to prevent the spread of the disease. Biopsy is a common diagnostic tool used by dermatologists to examine the tissue and determine the appropriate course of treatment.

It is commonly associated with HCV or HIV and can be treated with systemic steroids, topical steroids, tacrolimus, cyclosporine, acetratin, methotrexate, or phototherapy, depending on the severity and location of the disease.

This image shows a close-up of Kaposi sarcoma, a skin disease characterized by purple, irregular, and sometimes ulcerated nodules. It is caused by human herpes virus and can be found in immunocompromised individuals, such as those with HIV/AIDS. The prognosis varies based on the type and stage of the disease, with the classic type having a good prognosis and the African and AIDS-associated types having a poor prognosis. Treatment options include chemotherapy and immunotherapy.

This image describes disseminated aspergillosis, a fungal infection that can spread to multiple organs, often presenting with a gradual clinical deterioration, rash, low-grade fever, and mild sepsis. Blood cultures are typically negative, but lab contamination is a common cause of positive results.

This image shows various skin diseases, including melanoma, with different patterns such as globules, bluish-gray, and cobalt stone, and shapes such as round and irregular. These diseases are caused by the presence of melanocytic nests in the dermis and epidermis, and can be treated with surgery and other medical interventions.

The cysts can occur in any tissue and are often visible as white bulging cysts on the skin surface. When mature cysts rupture, spores are released into the water, infecting the fish's superficial cutaneous surface.

This image depicts superficial basal cell carcinoma, which appears flat and pink or red in early stages and can be challenging to distinguish from inflammatory skin conditions. It typically grows gradually over months to years, remaining shallow but potentially developing deeper invasion areas. Superficial BCC may be less likely to develop into another BCC and can recur after treatment with topical steroids or non-surgical methods due to focal areas of deeper extension along hair follicles.
This image describes pathological calcification, a condition where calcium salts accumulate in tissues other than bone and teeth, with no specific details about skin diseases provided.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with a raised, scaly appearance. This image shows a neurofibroma, a benign tumor of the nervous system, which can appear as a firm, raised, skin-colored bump on the skin. It is often caused by a genetic mutation and can be treated with surgical removal or other medical options.

This image discusses the classification of fungal diseases, which are divided into three types: superficial, subcutaneous, and systemic. Superficial infections include surface and cutaneous infections, affecting the skin's surface layer, while subcutaneous infections penetrate just below the skin and can spread throughout the body. Systemic infections infect deep into organs and spread throughout the body.

This image describes the four clinical and dermoscopic stages of hollow nevi, which are skin lesions characterized by a depression or pit in the skin, often accompanied by a raised, red border. The causes of hollow nevi are not well understood, but they may be related to genetics or sun exposure. Treatment options include observation, cryotherapy, or surgical excision, depending on the size and location of the lesion.
This image shows a tiny bit of pigment, which is very difficult to photograph due to its small size and color.

This image shows a skin disease called phylometricoma, which is a tumor arising from matrix cells. It is characterized by the presence of ghost cells, which are terminally differentiated cells that do not have intercellular junctions and have well-preserved cytoplasm. The tumor can be observed in various areas of the skin, including the hair follicle, and is often associated with beta ketamine mutation. Phylometricoma can be found in odontogenic tumors, craniofacial masses, basal cell carcinomas, teratomas, endometroid adenocarcinomas, and endometrial carcinoma of the ovaries.

This image describes various treatment options for panpigroid, a type of skin disease characterized by inflammation and scarring. Topical steroids may be used as an adjunct, while cyclophosphamide has been used as a first line therapy in severe cases. The rituximab and IVIG combination is a newer approach with high remission rates, and can be administered in different schedules. Infliximab, adalimumab, and etanercept may also provide some benefit, but relapse is common and long courses of immunomodulation are generally required.

This image describes Molluscum contagiosum, a skin condition characterized by small, raised, skin-colored bumps with a central dimple, often appearing on the face, chest, armpit, and upper legs. The spots can be clustered together and typically clear on their own within six to 18 months, although new spots may develop during this time. Squeezing or cutting the spots can lead to scarring and spreading, and it's important to avoid passing the infection to others. Treatment may involve topical creams or laser therapy, and the skin may appear white, pink, or purplish after healing.

This image shows an invasive carcinoma, a type of skin cancer characterized by a growth or mass with an irregular shape and color, often appearing raised or nodular. The cause of invasive carcinoma is typically exposure to harmful UV radiation from the sun, and treatment options may include surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy.

This image shows a retinoblastoma tumor that has regressed, with four types of regression: type 1 is a white calciferous tumor with a cottage cheese pattern, type 2 is a translucent gray non-calcified mass with a fish flesh pattern, type 3 is a combination of types 1 and 2, and type 4 is a flat atrophic scar.

This image highlights the potential effects of stress on the skin, including unbalance, vitamin loss, malabsorption, mitochondrial fatigue, and adrenal fatigue, which may contribute to hyperpigmentation.

This image describes various skin diseases, including herpes zoster appearing in dermatomes, tinea capitis treated with oral griseofulvin, tinea corporis and tinea cruris treated with topical azoles, scabies with itching worse at night and little blisters and burrowing tracks, and actinic keratosis in elderly individuals with a history of working outside.
This image shows a person with a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash. The cause of the disease is not yet clear, but treatment options include topical creams and antihistamines.

This image shows a lady with a severe hypokinesis of the apex, which is a condition characterized by a lack of movement or mobility at the top of the spine. The cause of this condition is not entirely clear, but it can be related to various factors such as muscle weakness, nerve damage, or spinal cord injuries. Treatment options may include physical therapy, medications, and in some cases, surgery.

This image shows a person with a skin disease characterized by a red, bumpy rash on their face. The cause of the disease is unknown, but it appears to be an infection. Treatment options include antibiotics and topical creams to manage symptoms and promote healing.

This image features sebaceous hyperplasia, an overgrowth of oil glands causing little dimples on the skin. It can be mistaken for basal cell cancer and is often seen in individuals with high testosterone levels, acne-prone skin, or due to skin occlusions. Topical therapies with retinoids can help, but removal may require radiofrequency treatment.

This image describes scabies, a skin disease caused by mites that burrow into the skin and cause an itchy rash, typically found on the fingers, wrists, arms, and groin. It is contagious and can be transmitted through direct skin-to-skin contact or sharing of items like clothing and bedding. Treatment involves applying an over-the-counter cream like Lyclair 5% Dermal Cream, which contains the insecticide Permatrin to paralyze and kill the mites and their eggs. Some itching may persist after treatment, but Urax Cream can also be used to relieve symptoms.
This image shows a small, well-defined spot on the skin, which can be safely treated with a shallow depth of energy.

This image describes an 86-year-old gentleman with a fungating tumor on the neck, which causes skin irritation and discomfort. The treatment involves using a hydrofibre dressing to protect the peri-wound area and promote healing while also reducing dressing changes and improving patient satisfaction.

This image shows a close-up view of a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with a raised, scaly texture, caused by an allergic reaction to a specific substance. Treatment options include topical creams and ointments, as well as avoiding the allergen.

This image shows reactive lesions such as fibroepithelial polyps or pyogenic granulomas, commonly found in the oral cavity due to irritation or trauma. These lesions can develop papillary or nodular outgrowths and are characterized by a healing tissue with good vascular element, inflammation, and melanin incontinence. Another type of lesion is varicofam xanthoma, which is an exophytic lesion with varicose or flat-topped borders, showing good amount of parakeratinization and keratin plugging. The presence of pale-looking, foamy histiocytes in the lamina propria indicates a diagnosis of varicofam xanthomas. While human papillomavirus was once thought to be the etiological factor, it is now believed to be more of an association.

This image shows a patient with severe photorepressive and photoaging on the upper arms and thighs, who has been treated with Zeo Retamax. The treatment has resulted in significant improvements in the skin's color, homogeneity, and texture.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a raised, red, and inflamed patch with white buttons, caused by an autoimmune disorder, and can be treated with topical steroids and immunosuppressive medications.

This image shows congenital ectropion, a condition where the outer lamellae of the eyelid shorten, causing the eyelid to invert outwards. This can lead to epiphora, keratitis, and potential vision problems. Other congenital eyelid diseases include horizontal extension of the eyelid fissure (euriblepharon), vertical shortening (vertical ectropion), and fusion of the eyelid skin (congenital dystichiasis). Congenital hemangioma capillaries can also cause mechanical ptosis and may require urgent treatment if they interfere with vision.

This image depicts a patient with a skin disease characterized by the overexpression of a bad protein, which was being targeted for treatment using small interfering RNAs. The Pachynikia Congenitif Foundation supported research on using these RNAs to eliminate the protein from the patient's skin, with promising results.
This image shows cobblestone-like papillomatous nevi on the trunk, which can change from flat to papillomatous and may require excision in some cases.

This image highlights the significance of vascular lesions in a skin disorder, which can appear as red or purple marks on the skin. The cause of these lesions is not yet fully understood, but treatment options may include laser therapy or medications to reduce their appearance.
