Rosacea is another common skin condition characterized by episodes of flushing and blushing, which can progress to persistent redness over time.

This image shows a mucinous carcinoma characterized by epithelial tumor islands dispersed within bluish mucin lakes separated by fibrovascular septa. The tumor cells are bland, but the distinctive histopathologic features include holes of mucin with islands of neoplasm in the center, separated by fibrovascular cores.

This image shows pemphigoid gestation, a skin disease characterized by pruritic urticaria papules and blisters on the abdomen and trunk, which may worsen during pregnancy and flares postpartum. The lesions are typically annular and located in the upper left corner of the breast.

This image shows a patient with a skin disease characterized by a rash and a tumor. The diagnosis involves assessing the appearance of the rash and tumor by a dermatologist and analyzing a biopsy sample by a pathologist, who can determine if it is a T-cell lymphoma or B-cell lymphoma.

This image shows a case of cutaneous melanoma, an aggressive form of skin cancer that can cause sudden vision loss. The patient was given a short period of time to live and the option of aggressive cancer treatment, but chose to live her life with quality and not be sick every day. The dermatologist took HRT images to show the elevated optic disc edema, indicating the progression of the disease.

This image depicts GERD, a digestive disorder characterized by abnormal stomach acid reflux, which can cause symptoms such as heartburn and regurgitation. The causes of GERD include a weakened lower esophageal sphincter, hiatal hernia, and certain medications. Treatment options include lifestyle changes, medications, and surgery in severe cases.

This image depicts the facial appearance of Lotskill EDS, characterized by a thin pinched nose, thin lips, and large, appearing eyes due to adipose tissue atrophy in the ocular lobeless ears. Additionally, premature hair loss is a common feature, which may be severe and particularly affect young women.

This image depicts Hansen's leprosy, a type of skin disease characterized by nerve involvement and tracheal inflammation, which can lead to subclotoxetine infection, floating stenosis, and lung involvement such as cough, dyspnea, and massive pulmonary hemorrhage. It is important to differentiate this from tuberculosis, which presents with hemoptysis due to the rupture of bronchial vessels. Lab investigations, including perinuclear cytoplasmic antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (pANCA) and anti-protein antibodies (cANCA), can help diagnose and monitor the disease activity.

This image shows a large colloidal malignant melanoma measuring 16.5 by 11.5 by 4.1 millimeters, presenting in December of 2006 with echographic confirmation of the tumor and exudative retinal detachment.

This image depicts uveodermatologic syndrome, a rare depigmenting disease affecting Akitas and Chows, characterized by histiocytic endophthalmitis and pigmented cell attack in the skin, often leading to severe ophthalmitis and dermatitis, ultimately life-threatening in dogs.

This image depicts a common skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash that can appear on various parts of the body. The cause of this condition is often an allergic reaction to certain substances, and treatment options include avoiding the allergen, using over-the-counter creams, and seeing a dermatologist for prescription medication.

This image shows various types of psoriasis, including psoriasis-like pattern in established pustular psoriasis, interface or lichenoid dermatitis mimicking lichen planus, pustular folliculitis, and eosinophils from none to numerous. This image shows an irritated case of molluscum contagiosum, a skin disease characterized by small, raised, pearl-like bumps that can be itchy and painful. This image shows a dolphin with a distinctive black and white coloration, commonly associated with skin diseases such as leukoderma, which causes a loss of pigmentation in the skin. This image shows a patient with a melanoma, which can be difficult to detect due to its irregular shape and color. The dermatologist uses a dermatoscope and handheld lens to examine the skin closely and identify the melanoma.

This image shows stretch marks, narrow streak-like lines that can be pink, red, purple, or brown, commonly found in up to 80% of pregnant women. They are caused by hormonal changes and can be prevented through proper skin care and maintained by treatment options.

This image shows a case of granulation tissue, a type of skin disease characterized by the growth of thick, raised, and red or pink patches of skin, often accompanied by pus-like fluid. The cause of granulation tissue is typically an injury or infection, and treatment options may include debridement, antibiotics, and topical ointments to promote healing and prevent infection.

This image describes sebaceous filaments, which are permanent structures in the face that can become clogged with dead skin and oil, leading to difficult-to-treat bumps. While popping them may temporarily relieve the issue, it is not recommended and they often return. Dermatologists can try to remove them through cutting or using a plasma pen.

This image discusses the importance of staging in skin diseases, particularly lymphoma, to classify patients into subcategories based on the extent of involvement. Factors such as the number of lymph nodes, involvement of blood cells, and response to treatment are considered to determine the appropriate course of action and predict the patient's outcome.
This image shows a doom-shaped papule, along with multiple plain top papules and plain warts, illustrating common skin conditions seen in daily practice.

This image shows a target lesion with three zones of color: an outside rim of red, a central circle of pale or lighter color, and a violaceous or purple center due to cell death. In contrast, a target toy lesion has two zones of color, with an outside that is red or deep pink and an inside that is violaceous or purple. These differences are important in distinguishing between Stevens-Johnson Syndrome and atypical target toy lesions.

This image describes pulmonary avian, a condition that can cause brain infection, TIA, or brain abscess through paradoxical embolism or migraine. This image discusses syringoma, a skin disfigurement caused by unknown causes, with symptoms including small, raised, colorless or pink bumps on the skin. This image shows multiple yellowish-white firm nodules on the base of the thumb of the left hand, which may indicate an underlying skin disease such as granuloma annulare, and can be treated with topical steroids or other medications.

This image describes venous leg ulcers, which are caused by valve dysfunction of the veins due to factors such as increased intra-abdominal pressure, failure of calf pumps, congenital varicose veins, or previous DVTs. This image discusses plexiform neurofibromas, a variant of neurofibromatosis type 1, which affects less than 10% of children with the condition. Plexiform neurofibromas are composed of perineural cells, mast cells, and Schwann cells, and typically present with eyelid ptosis, S-shaped deformity, and possibly squint or strabismus. Growth usually plateaus after puberty.

This image shows a blister on the roof of a lesion, which is characteristic of Bullous Pemphigoid. The blister is typically round or oval in shape and may be clear or have a yellowish tint. This image shows melasma, a common skin condition during pregnancy, characterized by brown, clearly defined patches on the face, typically appearing in the forehead, malar distribution, and cheekbones. It persists postpartum and is challenging to treat due to contraindications in pregnancy. To avoid melasma, avoid excessive sunlight exposure and use sunscreens. Striae gravidarum are linear, red-purplish stretch marks that occur in the second trimester, resulting from the rupture of dermal elastic fibers and are not reversible. They often fade postpartum to thin atrophic hypopigmentation.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by the presence of an intact basement membrane, which can be caused by various factors such as genetics or exposure to certain chemicals, and can be treated with topical creams or other medications.

This image depicts a skin disease characterized by a persistent, unrelenting presence on the skin, with a shape and color that varies depending on the specific condition. This image depicts a case of basal cell carcinoma on an 83-year-old's right calf. The cancer appears as a raised, pink or brown lesion. The goal of surgery is to remove the entire cancer and close the wound without distortion or impairment. After surgery, the pathologist identified cancer cells on the lower edge, so an additional rim of tissue was removed to prevent further growth. The resulting wound cannot be closed directly, as demonstrated by the inability to pinch together the edges of the remaining wound.
This image shows a dermoscopy of a thrombose angioma, characterized by a homogeneous black area on the right side, surrounded by lacunae and septa, which are the remnants of the angioma.

This image highlights the relationship between diet and rosacea, a skin condition characterized by redness, swelling, and acne-like breakouts. A survey conducted by the National Rosacea Society found that nearly 80% of rosacea patients consume hot foods, alcoholic beverages, foods containing capsicum-like spices and peppers, and foods containing cinnamon and chocolate. These foods can activate certain channels in skin cells, leading to symptoms like redness, flushing, and burning sensation. While the impact of diet on rosacea symptoms varies, some foods may have protective effects, such as omega-3 fats found in oily fish, walnuts, flax seeds, which may help safeguard against ocular or eye rosacea. Gut health also plays a significant role in rosacea, and strategies targeting gut health could be promising areas for research. Probiotics like lactobacillus casei may have a positive impact on rosacea symptoms. To manage rosacea, it is recommended to keep a food diary, avoid processed foods, reduce foods with a high glycemic index, consider an elimination diet, and consume a wide variety of colorful whole foods and fibrous carbohydrates. Topical treatments such as moisturizers, azelaic acid, and niacin may also be helpful in managing rosacea symptoms.</s> This image discusses rosacea, a skin condition with anti-inflammatory properties, and Ivermectin, a medication used to kill parasites that may reduce dandruff and dandruff infections in the case of rosacea. Personal experience with Ivermectin shows that it can effectively treat flare-ups when used daily for four weeks.

The condition is commonly seen over the scalp and skull, and can be treated with drugs such as methamazole, carbamazole, and mesoprostol. It is important to remember that certain infections, like chickenpox and herpes viruses, can also cause this condition. Topical antifungal medications with anti-pruritic, anti-inflammatory, and anti-inflammatory properties, such as serotaconazole, can be used to treat fungal infections. Additionally, certain enzymes, like squalene epoxidase and 14-alpha-d methylase, play a crucial role in the production of ergosterol, which is important for fungal cell walls. Medications like alalamin and azole group of medications can inhibit the conversion of squalene to lanosterol, while the allocryl group of medications can help with the conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol.

The cause of ITP can be primary or secondary, with secondary causes including viral infection, after immunization, or as a secondary disorder. In some cases, ITP can be short-lived in children, while in others it can be chronic. During pregnancy, ITP may be more severe and can affect the offspring as well. As a pathologist, it is important to check the platelet count and bleeding time in patients with ITP to monitor their condition and treatment.</s> This image shows a patient with immune thrombocytopenic purpura, a type of blood disorder characterized by low levels of platelets and autoantibodies against the RBCs. This image describes three types of Congenital Tosses: 1) skin eyelid crease absent with higher than normal lid in down gaze position, associated with misdirected cross innervation between the oculomotor nerve and the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve; 2) tautic eyelid retraction with jaw opening, due to restriction of ocular movement and loss of levator function; and 3) absent sweating (decreased sweating) or mechanical tosses, which can occur due to increased weight of the upper eyelid or inflammation. Investigations before surgery, such as corneal sensitivity and ocular motility, are important for diagnosis and treatment. Surgical procedures indicated in mild to moderate tosses include tarsal plate axion, levator resection, and sling procedures, while severe tosses may require levator muscle resection.

Treatment options include antibiotics such as penicillin and monoclonal antibodies like Xigduo, which help neutralize the anthrax toxin. Prevention measures include vaccination and proper handling of infected animals.

This image discusses tinea corporis and tinea versicolor, two common skin diseases caused by dermatophytes (Microsporum, Trichophyton, and Epidermophyton) and yeast (Malassezia furfur), respectively. Tinea corporis is spread by skin-to-skin contact and presents as red, itchy, circular or oval lesions. Tinea versicolor appears as hypopigmented or hyperpigmented macules and is usually asymptomatic. Diagnosis involves a KOH prep, which shows short hyphae and spores. Treatment for both conditions involves topical antifungal medications.

Treatment options include medications to kill the parasites and alleviate symptoms.

This image describes articularia pigmentosa, a benign skin disease characterized by dormant, stable, and non-inflammatory lesions that can be mistaken for other conditions. The diagnosis can be confirmed through the "drier sign," which has a high sensitivity of over 90% and is consistent with articularia pigmentosa lesions. The disease is often triggered by various factors and can cause pruritus.

This image describes folliculitis, a skin condition caused by infections, mechanical trauma, or chemicals that involves the follicle. It can present as dome-shaped, pus-filled follicular pustules, and can be classified as superficial, deep, or furuncle, with deep folliculitis causing an erythematous plaque with multiple pus points. Pharynx is a deeper involvement of the air follicles and adjacent areas, causing tender, erythematous, indurated nodules with throbbing pain and fever. Carbuncle is a deeper involvement of contiguous air follicles, often seen over the back and neck, with a tender, lobulated appearance and first discharge from multiple openings. Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome is a rare but severe condition that causes widespread erythema and blistering, often following a staph infection.

This image describes Turner syndrome, a rare genetic disorder affecting 1 in 2,500 newborn female infants, characterized by short stature, absence of secondary sexual characteristics, and potential heart and kidney problems, with no known cure.

These lesions can be tender, itchy, or shiny and are usually flat, but may be elevated or have a thicker scale. They can range in size from 3 to 10 millimeters and may require biopsy for confirmation or treatment if liquid nitrogen therapy is unsuccessful.

This image shows a diagram explaining the differences between chickenpox and shingles, also known as zoster. Chickenpox is a contagious disease caused by the varicella-zoster virus, which results in an itchy rash that spreads all over the body. Shingles, on the other hand, is a reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus, causing a unilateral rash that never crosses the midline and can be accompanied by severe neurotic pain. The primary infection is chickenpox, while the secondary or latent infection is shingles. The diagnosis of shingles can be made through direct microscopy, TCR, virus isolation, or serology, and the treatment options include acyclovir, vidarabin, or other antiviral medications. To prevent shingles, immunoglobulin and the live attenuated vaccine can be used within 72 hours of exposure.

Genital warts are called condyloma acuminata, while non-genital warts can be of four types: Veruca vulgaris, plain wart, plantar wart, and filiform wart. It is crucial to know the strain of HPV that causes common wart, which is HPV2, and the predisposing factors for plantar wart, such as hyperhidrosis and mosaic infection.

This image shows a close-up of a person's skin with a noticeable loss of collagen, a protein that gives skin its elasticity and firmness. The condition, known as scleroderma, can cause the skin to become thick, tight, and discolored. The exact cause of scleroderma is unknown, but it is believed to be an autoimmune disorder in which the body's immune system attacks its own tissues. Treatment options for scleroderma include medications to reduce inflammation and slow down the progression of the disease, as well as skin care routines to manage symptoms and prevent complications.

This image describes monkeypox lesions as well-defined, deep-seated bumps with sharp borders, sometimes developing a umbilication in the middle. They are relatively subcutaneous, the same size and stage on the same body site, and usually start on the face before spreading outward. Fever always comes before a rash, and most people have swollen lymph nodes. Syphilis is another condition that can cause rashes on the palms and soles, while Rocky Mountain spotted fever may also present with rashes.

This image shows stretch marks, which are caused by the stretching of the skin, often appearing as long, narrow streaks with a slightly raised surface. This image shows a fibroma of tendon sheath, which is a hypocellular lesion with thin, compressed, elongated vessels and cleft-like vessels. It typically occurs in the distal extremities but can also be found closer to large joints. The lesion may become sclerotic and collagenized over time, developing cracking artifacts between the collagen.

This image depicts a skin disease known as drug eruption, which can present with a morbilliform or exanthematous rash, similar to a viral exanthem. These eruptions can occur with virtually any drug and are often accompanied by histologic findings such as spongiosis, interface changes, vacuoles, apoptotic keratinocytes, and lymphocyte exocytosis. Although eosinophils are often associated with drug reactions, their absence does not necessarily mean it is less likely to be a drug eruption. A mechanistic approach to determine if a drug eruption is allergic or not is not helpful in diagnosis and management.

This image describes keratoacanthoma, a skin disease characterized by a benign, solitary, dome-shaped lesion with a firm consistency. This image shows a blister caused by the removal of a worm using a small stick, with a range of 15-20 days for the worm to be completely removed. Ascaria lumbricoides is a gastrointestinal tract infection caused by Ascaris lumbricoides, which can also cause ascaris pneumonitis due to larval migration in the lungs. Hookworm infection, caused by Nekator americanos and Silo to maduro, can lead to clinical manifestations such as pneumonitis, allergic reactions, and anemia. Enterobiasis is a common helminth infection caused by Entamoeba histolytica, and trichoriasis is caused by Trichuris trichura.

This image shows a patient with immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP), a quantitative platelet disorder characterized by a decrease in platelet count. In ITP, there is hyperactivity and abnormality of platelets, which can lead to hemorrhagic bullae and an increased risk of bleeding. The cause of ITP can be primary or secondary, with secondary causes including viral infection, after immunization, or as a secondary disorder. In some cases, ITP can be more severe in children, particularly during pregnancy. As a pathologist, it is important to check the patient's blood sample for a decreased platelet count and increased bleeding time in the extrinsic pathway, which is not affected by coagulation factors.</s> This image depicts a patient with type 2 hypersensitivity, which is a condition where the immune system attacks tissues. In this case, the patient has immune thrombocytopenic autoantibodies against RBCs, as detected by the Coombs test. The spleen plays a crucial role in this condition, as it can cause autoantibodies to form and interact with RBCs, leading to the destruction of platelets. Splenectomy is the best and permanent option for treating patients with immune thrombocytopenic purpura, as it removes the spleen and prevents further autoantibody formation and destruction of platelets.

This image describes the surgical management of mucormycosis, a fungal infection that can spread to the sinuses and orbit. The pre-requisites for surgery include a thorough evaluation of the patient's medical history, lung status, cardiac clearance, pulmonary clearance, ophthalmology checkup, and renal function. The surgery aims to remove devitalizing tissue, debulk infected tissue, and perform tissue biopsy for diagnosis confirmation. The stage of the disease determines the specific surgical approach, which may include endoscopic sinus surgery, maxillectomies, and orbital decompression. Aggressive debitement of all sinuses is crucial for success, and repeated debitements may be necessary. The use of antifungal therapy alone is inadequate for controlling infection, and surgery should be considered early in the treatment course with complete removal of infected or necrotic tissue.

It can be caused by a secondary bacterial infection, and the condition can worsen if not treated at the right time. Lava migrants can be caused by roundworm infections from dogs and cats, which are not naturally a human parasite. The parasites are unable to complete their life cycle in humans, and the immune system forms a granuloma to arrest the larvae, leading to their death. The disease can cause various symptoms depending on the organs affected, such as fever, anorexia, vomiting, fatigue, hepatomegaly, asthma, and neurological disorders. Diagnosis is based on clinical features and identification of the parasites is not possible due to the migration taking place in visceral organs.</s> This image describes a parasitic infection that can cause skin diseases such as eosinophilia and elevated globulinemia. This image highlights the link between sugar consumption and inflammation in the skin, which can lead to the development of skin diseases such as acne, eczema, and psoriasis, characterized by redness, bumps, and scaling, respectively. The causes of these skin diseases can be genetic, environmental, and hormonal factors, and treatment options include lifestyle changes, topical creams, and in some cases, medications.

This image shows a device used for a painless treatment of skin diseases, such as psoriasis, which appears as red, scaly patches on the skin. The cause of psoriasis is not fully understood, but it is believed to be an autoimmune disorder that triggers the overproduction of skin cells. Treatment options include topical creams, light therapy, and medications.

This image features Michael Piacenza, a professional dermatologist from Advantage Pest Control in St. Petersburg, Florida, discussing the treatment of fleas. Fleas have four stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. Eggs can be easily killed with desiccants, while larvae can be targeted using a boric acid-based mixture with a botanical pesticide. The boron in the mixture disrupts the larvae's digestive enzymes, killing them off. However, the pupa stage fleas are practically impervious to treatment.

This image shows a 55-year-old male with skin type 2, presenting with a changing mole on his left arm that has been present for over 10 years. The mole was previously removed two years ago and there is no bleeding or itching. The patient has no family history of skin cancer.

This image explains how ultraviolet light causes skin diseases such as sunburn, which appears as red, painful, and peeling skin, by damaging the skin's DNA and increasing the risk of skin cancer. Treatment options include avoiding sun exposure, using sunscreen, and seeking medical advice for severe cases.

This image shows giant cell arthritis, a skin disease characterized by the appearance of red, raised, and painful nodules, which can be caused by autoimmune disorders and treated with medications such as corticosteroids and biologics.

This image shows cytoplasmic anchors, a type of protein found in Wagners or GPA, and Perry nuclear anchors, which are associated with eGPA and polyangiitis. ANCA testing can be positive in up to 5% of the normal population.

This image features Necrobiopsis, a condition characterized by the death of collagen, causing the skin to appear thin, fragile, and translucent. The exact cause of Necrobiopsis is unknown, and treatment options are limited.

This image shows a small, pigmented lesion in the caruncular area of an 18-year-old patient with a history of repeated bleeding. The lesion was initially thought to be a congenital nevus, but it was later diagnosed as a low-grade melanoma after excision.

This image depicts a person who is scheduled for a biopsy appointment on May 17th to have their stitches removed and receive the results of the biopsy. The person is currently wearing a bandage and is unable to scrub it off, but the exact type and appearance of the skin disease are not described.

This image shows urticaria, a skin condition characterized by the sudden appearance of wheels, angioedema, or both, which present as sharply circumscribed, variable-sized, and shaped swellings accompanied by itching or burning sensations that resolve within 1 to 24 hours.

This image depicts inflammation of blood vessels, a condition known as vasculitis, which can cause various symptoms such as skin lesions, pain, and swelling. The exact cause of vasculitis is not yet fully understood, but it can be triggered by certain medications, infections, or autoimmune disorders. Treatment options include medications to reduce inflammation and prevent further damage to blood vessels.
This image shows a combination of cephalate maggots with freckling, which is a new criterion for skin disease diagnosis.
This image shows a skin disease called post-Kala-Azar dermal leishmaniasis, caused by the parasite Leishmania donovani, with two major presentations: monomorphic and polymorphic.

This image shows scabies, a skin disease caused by mites that burrow into the skin and cause an itchy rash, commonly found on the fingers, wrists, arms, and groin. It is transmitted through direct skin-to-skin contact or sharing of items like clothing and bedding, and can take up to eight weeks to show symptoms. Treatment involves applying over-the-counter cream like Lyclair 5% Dermal Cream, which contains insecticide Permatrin to paralyze and kill the mites and their eggs. Some itching may persist after treatment, but Urax Cream can also be used for relief.

This image describes various patent medicine treatments for coughs, colds, asthma, croup, bronchitis, whooping cough, and consumption, which was tuberculosis and caused significant coughing. The treatments often contained opioids that decreased the cough reflex.

This image depicts acrodermatitis, a skin condition characterized by the appearance of small, flesh-colored bumps on the skin, often found in areas such as the neck, armpit, and groin. The cause of acrodermatitis is not fully understood, but it may be related to skin irritation, infections, or autoimmune disorders. Treatment options include topical creams, laser therapy, or surgical removal, depending on the severity and location of the lesions.
This image shows a cilindroma, a slowly growing adenexal tumor commonly found on the head, neck, and scalp with a dermoscopic pattern of arborizing vessels, white lines, and red lines.

This image depicts a patient with atypical sweet syndrome, a skin disease characterized by a sweet-smelling fluid that oozes from the skin, caused by a hematological malignancy and responding to corticosteroids and antibiotics. However, the patient has now developed a chest infection.

This image describes Bessette's syndrome, a rare skin disease characterized by the presence of multiple, benign, red or brown nodules on the skin, often found on the face, neck, and upper body. The exact cause of the disease is unknown, but it is thought to be related to exposure to certain chemicals or environmental factors. Treatment options include surgical removal of the nodules, topical creams, and avoiding known triggers.

This image displays various skin diseases, including vitiligo, lichen sclerosis, hemangioma, seborrheic keratosis, and neurofibromatosis. The lecture highlights the importance of assessing skin color, texture, and appearance to accurately diagnose and treat skin conditions.

This image shows a rash that is characteristic of Pteriasis Rosea, a viral illness commonly affecting older kids, teenagers, and young adults. It starts with a herald patch, followed by a diffuse, patchy rash throughout the body that resembles a Christmas tree pattern. The rash is usually itchy, and treatment includes topical steroids, hydrocortisone, or Benadryl, with the latter having sedating effects. Physical examination and a good history are key for diagnosis.

This image describes a patient with a skin disease characterized by the formation of blisters. The patient was treated with IV fluids, steroids, and antacids, and after nine days of admission, the blisters stopped forming. The patient was then followed up in the clinic, and after six weeks, her skin improved and became smooth.

This image shows Weldon hypomelanotic macules, which are white skin lesions that can appear in various shapes on the posterior trunk, extremities, head, or neck, and may be caused by Emp conversion. Treatment options include topical steroids and phototherapy.

This image shows scabies, a skin disease caused by tiny mites that burrow into the skin and cause an itchy rash, commonly found on the fingers, wrists, arms, and groin. The rash appears as tiny bumps and can take up to eight weeks to show. Scabies is highly contagious and can be transmitted through direct skin-to-skin contact, sharing of items such as clothing and bedding. Treatment involves applying over-the-counter cream like Lyclair 5% Dermal Cream, which contains insecticide Permatrin to paralyze and kill the mites and their eggs.

This image shows a person with a skin disease characterized by red, itchy patches on their skin. The cause of this condition is repeated exposure to mild irritants, and over time, some individuals may develop a tolerance to the substance. Treatment options include avoiding further exposure to the irritant, using over-the-counter creams, and seeing a dermatologist for prescription medications.
This image shows very benign looking trabeculae, which are made up of sphyngeal cells.

This image shows a close-up of a skin disease characterized by red, scaly patches on the affected area, caused by an overproduction of desmoglein 1 antibodies, and can be treated with topical creams and immunosuppressive medications.
This image shows an example of septic vasculitis, a type of vasculitis characterized by thrombi and inflammation, which may also include neutrophils and bacterial cocci.

This image describes Lewis-Dietz syndrome, a rare genetic disorder characterized by widely spaced eyes (hypertheliorism) and other facial and skeletal abnormalities. The exact cause is unknown, but it is thought to be related to mutations in the FGFR3 gene. Treatment options focus on managing symptoms and improving quality of life.

This image shows an enchondroma, a type of benign bone tumor that appears as a smooth, round or oval lesion with a white or yellowish color. It is caused by an overgrowth of cartilage cells and can be treated through surgical excision.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by the appearance of red, raised, and inflamed patches, potentially caused by granuloma formation. Treatment options may include topical steroids, immunosuppressants, or light therapy.
This image features a happy family with a healthy, well-groomed dog that enjoys playing fetch and snuggling with its human companions.

This image depicts a treatment for BCC, a skin disease characterized by a raised, pink or brown lesion with a smooth or slightly scaly surface, which can be caused by exposure to harmful UV rays and may require surgical excision or other treatments.
This image shows a high client satisfaction rate for a skin disease treatment, with an overall response of 85 to 90 percent.

This image shows a dermatologist assessing a patient's skin disease, which appears as a [describe the appearance of the skin disease, such as its shape, color, or texture]. The dermatologist is investigating the possible causes of the disease and discussing treatment options with the patient.

This image displays the incubation process for diagnosing skin diseases such as Actinomyces and Uricis, using antibiotic-containing media and maintaining temperatures between 25-37 degrees Celsius for 6-8 weeks. The diagnosis is made through both direct and indirect methods, and the appearance of the affected grains may show threads characteristic of Actinomycetes. In some cases, deformed legs may be a symptom of the disease. The lecture discusses the complexities of mycology and the importance of using simple names for genus and species.
This image shows gangrene, a skin disease characterized by the death of skin tissue, often caused by infection or ischemia, and can be treated with debridement, antibiotics, and surgery.

This image depicts a person in their 50s or 60s with a red, crusty skin condition caused by CAD (Chronic Actinic Dermatitis), which makes them sensitive to light and requires heavy protection from the sun. The condition was first reported about 15-20 years ago and can have a significant impact on a person's mental well-being.

This image shows a skin disease called monkeypox, which affects different animal species and has been detected in multiple organisms, including arboreal rodents like the kale of the squirrel. The disease causes a rash and can be transmitted to humans through contact with infected animals. Treatment options include antiviral medications and supportive care.

This image shows various skin diseases caused by Streptococci, including impetigo (yellow, crusty lesions), erysipelas (red, raised bumps), and strep throat (sore throat with pustules and red inflammation). Untreated, these infections can lead to more severe complications such as scarlet fever, sepsis, pneumonia, and toxic shock syndrome.

This image shows a solitary circumscribed plaque lesion with potential ulceration, characterized by hyperplasia, hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis, and scattered bizarre dyskeratotic cells in the epithelial cells of the epidermis.
This image provides instructions for using a product, including details and precautions.

This image shows a close-up of a person's skin, which appears to have a red and inflamed rash covering a large area. The rash is likely caused by an allergic reaction or skin irritation, and treatment options may include topical creams or ointments, avoiding known allergens, and using over-the-counter antihistamines.

This image shows a person with a rash on their skin, likely caused by an allergic reaction to the pajamas they are wearing. Treatment options may include avoiding the allergen and using over-the-counter creams to soothe the skin.
This image shows a patient with dry mucous membranes and dehydration, indicating potential skin disease such as eczema or psoriasis, which can be treated with topical creams or oral medications.
This image shows a skin disease characterized by the formation of a sac-like structure that causes pressure and painful sensations as the infection spreads.
This image describes Merkel cell carcinoma, a rare tumor characterized by vessels that are not in focus, arborizing, and with a cherry red background.

This image shows how celiac disease, an inflammatory condition in the intestine, can extend to the skin through the activation of the immune system. In patients with celiac disease, transglutaminase enzymes, present in the skin as epidermal transglutaminases, can trigger an autoimmune response against a previously sequestered antigen, leading to skin inflammation and blisters. This is known as epitope spreading, where the original tissue damage from the primary inflammatory process then exposes a previously sequestered antigen, causing a secondary autoimmune response.

This image shows a skin disease called tinea corporis, characterized by a central clearing surrounded by an elevated, round, and flat border, with a black color. The disease is associated with ringworm and can be treated with antifungal medications.

This image shows an example of a nail matrix melanoma, characterized by a brown coloration with irregular lines of varying thickness and spacing. Another example displays multiple irregular lines of different thickness, and a positive Hutchinson sign, which is pigmentation outside of the nail, typically seen at the proximal nail fold.

This image shows a patient with a skin disease characterized by red, itchy patches on their legs. The cause of the disease is believed to be an allergic reaction to a traint anticoagulant used in New Zealand. Treatment options include topical creams and avoiding exposure to the allergen.

This image shows a lack of epithelialization with a granulating area and mucopurulent discharge in the belly button of a newborn. Treatment typically involves wiping with alcohol pads a few times a day to dry up the discharge, and cauterization with a silver nitrate stick if necessary.

This image depicts a person with a skin disease characterized by a specific shape and color, which may be caused by genetics or environmental factors, and can be treated with various topical and systemic therapies.

This image shows an annular form of sarcoidosis, characterized by a chronic stage with peripheral evolution and central flaring, predominantly seen on the forehead, face, and neck. It is often observed in young women and tends to involve bilateral hyaluronophagy, with a tendency to resolve spontaneously within two years.

This image shows a skin disease known as lichen planus, which causes polygonal, itchy lesions on the skin. The lesions have a reticulate, lacy white pattern and can be found on the flexion or aspect of the body. The disease may also cause hair and nail changes, such as alopecia and a blue-gray hue. Histopathology reveals hyperkeratosis, wedge-shaped hypergranulosis, and basal cell degeneration in the affected skin. The disease can be treated with topical steroids and other medications, and it may cause hyperpigmentation when healing.</s> This image shows lymphocyte infiltration against the epidermis in keratinocytes, leading to the formation of seeded bodies in lichen planus. The treatment for lichen planus is corticosteroids, which can be given orally or topically.

This image shows rheumatoid nodules on the left hand and inflamed nodular papular lesions on the right hand, which are associated with skin diseases such as erythema nodosum and hydradenitis superativa.

This image discusses scabies, a skin disease characterized by itchy, red, and sometimes blistered skin. Treatment involves giving a start dose of 200 micrograms per kg of ivermectin, followed by a week later. It's important to treat all contacts in the household to prevent reinfection. For post-scabies, anti-histamines and sometimes steroids may be used for symptom management.
This image discusses the improvement of scars after skin cancer surgery through the use of lasers, which have enhanced aesthetic outcomes in two specific cases.

This image shows two middle-aged ladies with diffused pigmentation of the face, and the dermoscopy results reveal a case of epidermal varicose nevus on the left and a terra firma on the right. The left nevus comedonicus has open comedones, while the right one has keratin, scales, and dirt. The dermoscopy results also indicate that the nevus comedonicus on the left is congenital and the terra firma on the right is acquired.

This image shows various skin diseases, including perineuroma, fibrous papules (angiofibromas on the face), adenoma sebaceum (on the penis), vulvar or vestibular papillomatosis (on the vulva), conan tumors (in the periungual area), acquired digital fibrokeratoma (ADFK), and fibrous papules (on the central face or nose). These diseases can cause symptoms such as growths, discoloration, or changes in appearance, and may be treated with surgery, medication, or other therapies.

This image describes bacterial virudan strep, a common cause of upper respiratory tract infections, which can potentially lead to endocarditis. Antibiotic prophylaxis may be prescribed before dental procedures to prevent its spread.

This image shows the changes that occur in the retinal pigment epithelium with age, including a reduction in photoreceptor density, a decrease in melanin granules, and the accumulation of waste and residual bodies. Basal laminar and linear deposits, as well as choreocapillaries, undergo progressive involutional changes. Drusens, formed from basal laminar and linear deposits, are clinically visible as small yellow round lesions along the basal surface of the retinal pigment epithelium and are a key factor in the development of age-related macular degeneration.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash that has spread beyond the normal boundaries of the skin. The cause of this condition is unknown, but it may be related to an allergic reaction or an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical creams and ointments, as well as oral medications to suppress the immune system.

This image describes multiple endocrine neoplasias (MEN), which are tumors of endocrine glands such as the pancreas, parathyroid, pituitary, and thyroid. Types 2A and 2B are caused by mutations in the Rett gene.

This image depicts functional melanonychia, a common skin condition affecting people with darkly pigmented skin, particularly African Americans, where matrix melanocytes become activated, causing the nails to become discolored or develop white streaks.
This image is for BBL Forever Clear, a treatment for skin discoloration caused by sun damage, age spots, and acne scars, offering a non-invasive and effective solution with minimal downtime.

This image depicts a Hutchinson freckle, which is a type of melanoma in situ, characterized by a flat, brown or black spot with irregular borders and a rough surface. The cause of Hutchinson freckles is not fully understood, but they are associated with increased sun exposure and fair skin. Treatment options include surgical removal, radiation therapy, and immunotherapy.
This image shows examples of foods to avoid for healthy skin, such as those high in sugar and unhealthy fats, which can cause acne and other skin conditions.

This image shows a fibrous forehead plaque, similar to an angiofibroma, with solid, flesh-colored papules on the forehead. Angiofibromas can be mistaken for acne but lack comedones. Periungual fibromas and collagenomas are also present, characterized by firm, flesh-colored papules with varying degrees of erythema and telangiectasia.
This image shows purple, polygonal, angular, flat-topped papules on the skin that are pruritic or itchy, indicating lichen planus, a skin disease characterized by severe pruritus.
This image shows columnar epithelium with varying appearances depending on the section of cut, and in some cases, the mucin is washed out, providing a clearer view.

This image shows muscle inflammation, calcified lesions, and puffed rice appearance in the tissue from old incubation cysts, as well as developing cysts with the head of the tapeworm identified. Histological analysis may demonstrate necrosis with eosinophilic infiltrates in the acute phase of infection, and encapsulated cysts and associated pathology later on. Occasionally, the larvae or adult worm may also be identified.

This image shows a close-up view of a person's skin, possibly affected by a skin disease characterized by redness and inflammation, with causes such as allergies or irritants, and treatment options including topical creams or ointments.
This image shows a patient with severe ptosis, which has been successfully treated with crutch ocular processes.

This image shows thick red plaques with silvery scales, commonly found in the vulva and elsewhere, which is a characteristic appearance of psoriasis. Psoriasis versus eczema: while both conditions can cause skin discomfort, psoriasis lesions tend to persist, whereas eczema lesions have a waxing and waning process.

This image shows a pathological lesion of syphilitic granulation tissue around a blood vessel, infiltrated with lymphocytes and few giant cells. The characteristic feature is a prominent endarteritis obliterans, which is inflammation of the blood vessel walls. This is accompanied by generalized lymphadenitis, especially in the epitrochlear and cervical lymph nodes, which are enlarged. The generalized lymphadenitis can occur in most areas of the body or in the brain. The terminal stage of syphilis appears after two years of infection and is characterized by slow caseous necrosis in a localized area of syphilitic granulation tissue. This is followed by fibrosis and the healing process. Examples of gamma worm-eaten appearance of skull bones and worm-eaten appearance of the heart are also seen in syphilis. The liver can have multiple foci of necrosis surrounded by radiating fibrotic scars. The infection can also affect the aorta, leading to interventricular thoracic aorta involvement and arch of the aorta syphilitic infection. The lupulation of the surface stage of syphilis under enlargement is followed by fibrosis, which is more common and develops very slowly. Congenital syphilis can be transmitted from an infected mother to her fetus after the fourth month of pregnancy. Early manifestations of syphilis include scaly disquamation of the palms and soles, radiating scars at the angles of the mouth and anus, and gamma of the nasal septum. Syphilitic osteomyelitis can cause inflammation of bone and bone marrow, leading to retardation of growth. Syphilitic inflammation of internal organs, such as the liver, can also occur. Hudgson teeth, which are small, big, widely separated, and notched, can be a sign of infection. Immunity to syphilis is primarily TH1-mediated, with no production of interleukin 2 and interferon gamma.

This image discusses the study of prognostic markers and targets for future therapy in sebaceous Barcelona carcinoma, which may lead to the development of targeted treatments for more aggressive tumors. The sclerosing variant of BCC presents with secretory shell changes and can be managed with the use of a targeted treatment that inhibits the hedgehog signal pathway. Targeted therapy for squamous cell carcinoma using epidermal growth factor receptor inhibitors is also commercially available and shows promise for future treatment. It is important to rule out basal cell nervous syndrome in selected patients with multiple BCCs or God syndrome.

This image is about a skin disease called Montpelier, which caused an eruption in the early 1900s on the island of Martinique, destroying an entire town. The disease had a distinct blue color and left a lone survivor, a drunk naked man.

This image shows a patient with a varicose wound that was treated with vancomycin and variflow, and later transitioned to ceftriaxone and Cipro on discharge, with continued variflow and bedside debridements as needed.
This image shows a rare case of hypopion uveitis with extensive maculopapular lesions, which appear as erythema nodosum-like patches on the skin.

This image describes a potential treatment plan for melasma, a skin disease characterized by brown or gray patches, using the BBL Hero device with a lower energy setting of 10 millijoules and 10% coverage, followed by a series of treatments every four to six weeks and the possible addition of at-home hydroquinone for patients who can tolerate it.

This image depicts a skin disease characterized by red, inflamed, and itchy patches, caused by an overactive immune response to a harmless substance, and can be treated with topical steroids and immunosuppressive medications.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by tender, red, and inflamed skin with a raised, red, and scaly appearance, caused by an overactive immune response to a harmless substance, and can be treated with topical steroids and antihistamines.
This image depicts urticaria, a skin condition marked by the sudden emergence of raised, red, and itchy bumps on the skin, which can vary in size and shape and can be acute or chronic.
This image shows an aggregation of three or more similar structures clustered together, commonly used in dermoscopy to describe skin diseases such as melanoma, with distinct shapes and colors.

This image depicts a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash that may appear on the face and neck, often caused by an allergic reaction to a specific laxative. Treatment options include avoiding the offending laxative, applying over-the-counter creams, and seeing a dermatologist for prescription medication.

This image shows a close-up view of a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with small bumps, caused by an allergic reaction to a certain food or substance. Treatment options include avoiding the allergen, using over-the-counter creams, and seeing a dermatologist for prescription medication.
This image describes a common situation where women may undergo surgery to save their uterus, but complications can lead to the removal of the uterus, causing unexpected outcomes.

This image describes Jabs-Blau syndrome, a skin disease characterized by a gain-of-function mutation in part 15 of 2, which can be inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, and its early-onset sarcoidosis counterpart caused by a normal mutation.

This image shows an example of candida, a type of yeast infection that can cause skin rashes and irritation, often appearing as red, itchy patches. The cause of diaper rash, which is commonly seen on the skin of infants who wear diapers, can be attributed to a combination of factors such as moisture, irritants, and yeast overgrowth. Treatment options for diaper rash typically involve keeping the area clean and dry, applying over-the-counter creams or ointments, and avoiding tight clothing or diapers that trapped moisture.

This image shows capillary malformations, which are benign vascular tumors that appear as red or blue lesions, venous malformations, which are congenital venous anomalies that can be blue, purple, or red and cause pain and swelling, and lymphatic malformations, which are congenital lymphatic anomalies that can be blue, gray, or white and cause lymphedema.

This image describes Mepilex Transfer, a wound dressing that promotes moist wound healing and provides additional protection to the skin from round wounds, soft or round skin, and skin problems, with the option of using a secondary dressing for optimal healing.

This image shows a couple of skin lesions, possibly a type of skin disease, with a reddish-brown color and a raised, bumpy appearance. The exact cause of the lesions is unknown, but they may be related to sun exposure or an underlying skin condition. Treatment options may include topical creams or ointments, laser therapy, or surgical removal, depending on the severity and cause of the lesions.

This image highlights the negative impact of night shift work on health, including an increased risk of obesity, depression, cancer, and early death, primarily due to disrupted sleep and exposure to artificial light.

This image shows the sign of post-inflammatory hyper and hypopigmentation, signs of dysplasia, carcinoma in situ, or frank cancer in the genital wall, which is the most common part of the genital wall and a sign of proliferation of the genital wall. The main treatment for this condition is surgery to remove the affected tissue, but it may also cause scarring and fatigue.

This image shows a dog with a skin lesion that appears circular and may be confused with fungal dermatitis. The lesion is accompanied by crusts and a blood-tinged discharge. The dog's skin folds may also be affected, leading to skin fold dermatitis. To diagnose the underlying cause, a skin biopsy and cytology may be performed. The treatment for this condition involves finding and addressing any underlying causes, such as parasitic diseases, hypersensitivity, or endocrine disorders. Systemic antibiotics may be used for a minimum of six to eight weeks, with topical therapy using antiseptics like chlorhexidine or benzoyl peroxide also being effective. If the lesion does not resolve, a culture and sensitivity test may be performed to determine the most effective antibiotic.
This image shows the removal of subepithelial debridement on the face, followed by the removal of the cornea. The process was performed with careful control of bleeding using an edry soak swab stick.

This image depicts the case of hyperkeratinization, a skin condition characterized by an excessive build-up of keratin, resulting in thick, scaly patches on the skin. The causes of hyperkeratinization can vary, including genetic factors, skin injury, and certain medical conditions. Treatment options may include topical creams, medicated shampoos, and in some cases, surgical removal of the affected skin.
This image shows a close-up view of interrupted sutures used in skin surgery.
This image shows a neck vessel being used for microvascular reconstruction, with no major ligation of the vessels.

This image shows a choroidal hemorrhage, which is not a malignancy but can be caused by rare choroidal melanomas. It occurs when blood layers between the choroid and retina block choroidal fluorescence, highlighting the retinal circulation and any coincidental or causal CNV as hyperfluorescent.
This image shows a child with polyendometriosis, a skin disease characterized by erythematous, scaly patches on the elbows and knees, resembling psoriasis.

This image shows a plexiform neurofibroma, a type of skin disease characterized by a thick, rubbery, and discolored appearance, caused by excessive growth of nerve cells and fibers, and can be treated through surgical removal.
This image depicts a person with dermatitis caused by excessive hand washing, characterized by red, itchy patches on the skin.

This image describes intravenous immunoglobulin as a therapy for treating streptococcal toxic shock, a rare skin disease characterized by severe symptoms such as redness, blistering, and rapid breathing.

This image depicts a patient who self-treated interdigital maceration, leading to a swollen, red, and painful left foot. Lab tests identified staph aureus, a gram-negative bacteria, and candida as the cause of the infection, which was treated with appropriate antibiotics and topical creams.

This image shows a close-up of a capillary hemangioma, which is a benign skin tumor that appears as a reddish or purplish lesion. It typically starts shortly after birth and is more common in girls. The lesion is composed of vascular channels lined with a single layer of endothelium. The type of tissue must be mentioned in any pathology specimen to avoid confusion with other conditions like dermocystocel, which is an inflammation of the lacrimal sac. Capillary hemangiomas may increase in size during crying or straining and can cause ptosis (eyelid drooping) and amblyopia (lazy eye) due to the pressure on the eyelid and surrounding tissues.

This image shows tuberculosis cutis varicosa, a skin disease characterized by large, wart-like lesions on the extremities with marked hyperplasia, dermal infiltrate, neutrophilic abscess, and cascading necrosis. It is caused by direct inoculation of the pathogen and is not associated with lymphadenopathy.

This image depicts recurrent respiratory papillomatosis, a skin disease characterized by the growth of wart-like lesions in the throat and larynx, often caused by the human papillomavirus (HPV). Treatment options include surgery and laser therapy.

This image discusses autoantibodies, which can form antigen-antibody complexes and circulate in the body, potentially causing damage to various organs, including the kidneys. Abnormal cytokine production, such as interleukin 10 and alpha and interferon, can also contribute to inflammation. Immune complexes, which are sterile and not infective, can deposit on the corda tendini and cause non-bacterial endocarditis, also known as Lippmann sac endocarditis. Renal damage can result in extensive scarring and classifications of glomerulonephritis, such as minimal mesangial lupus nephritis and sclerosis. Understanding the immune system and its functions is crucial to understanding how these conditions form and their potential impact on the body.

This image shows a man with heart failure who is sitting with his legs in washing up bowls due to excess exudate levels that were not contained by his dressings. Despite the challenges of managing this condition, the patient's quality of life improved significantly once the exudate levels were brought under control. Clinicians need to assess the underlying factors causing the excess exudate and manage the patient holistically to prevent further issues and improve their quality of life.

This image depicts acanthosis nigricans, a condition characterized by velvety hyperpigmentation in folds such as the neck, axilla, groin, and popliteal fossa. It results from insulin resistance, which can be caused by diabetes, obesity, metabolic syndrome, nicotinic acid, and certain drugs like OCPs. A malignancy associated with acanthosis nigricans is gastric adenocarcinoma, which can present with sudden appearance or skin outpouchings called skin tags.

This image shows an example of a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with a burning sensation, caused by an allergic reaction to a particular substance, and can be treated with over-the-counter creams and avoiding the allergen.
This image describes the potential development of skin cancer in individuals with burn scars or scar tissue.

This image shows a thermoscopic picture of a skin disease characterized by a triangular pigmented structure at the top of the barrel, indicating the presence of the Delta glider sign. The image also features a white circle, which represents the jet with contrail sign, and a new sign located on the trunk, possibly corresponding to the fishes of the mite.

This image shows a classification system for a medical condition involving the tibia, with different stages ranging from total absence to a nubbin and a proximal epiphysis. The progression of the condition can have an impact on the patient's mobility and treatment options.

This image discusses various treatment options for leg veins, including sclerotherapy, pulse dye laser therapy, intravascular or endovascular laser therapy, and radiofrequency ablation, which can help improve circulation, appearance, and symptomatic relief. Wearing compression stockings is also recommended for preventing the formation of veins and facilitating blood return to the heart.

This image depicts lichen planus, a rare, inflammatory skin disorder characterized by flat, scaly patches that can affect various body parts such as hair, nails, and oral mucosa, with a higher incidence in males.

This image shows a Type V malignancy, which appears as a raised, red, and potentially ulcerated lesion on the skin. The cause of hyperkeratosis can be genetic or acquired, and treatment options may include topical creams, laser therapy, or surgical removal, depending on the severity and location of the condition.

This image shows a healthy, glowing complexion, free from skin diseases such as acne, eczema, or psoriasis. The skin appears smooth and even, with a natural color and texture. The causes of skin diseases can vary, but they often result from genetic predisposition, hormonal imbalances, allergies, or environmental factors such as pollution and UV radiation. Treatment options for skin diseases depend on the specific condition and can include topical creams, oral medications, light therapy, and lifestyle changes. Maintaining a healthy lifestyle, including a balanced diet, regular exercise, and adequate sleep, can also help prevent skin diseases and promote overall skin health.

This image shows a viral illness called pteriasis rosea, which causes a non-specific rash that can later develop into a Christmas tree-like pattern. The rash typically starts as a herald patch and spreads throughout the body, followed by fever, headache, and generalized weakness. The illness is self-limiting and usually takes two to three months to resolve. Treatment options include topical steroids, hydrocortisone, or trimicillin ointment, as well as Benadryl for itching and inflammation. However, it's important to rule out syphilis if the rash also appears on the palms of hands or soles of feet.

This image depicts pteriasis lichenoides, a chronic skin disease characterized by patches of thick, scaly, and discolored skin, often accompanied by itching and burning sensations. This image shows a skin disease characterized by painful, cracked sores, often caused by a deficiency in vitamin C. Treatment options include topical creams and supplementation with vitamin C.

This image shows a close-up of pustular psoriasis, a skin disease characterized by hundreds of sterile, non-follicular pustules that appear on major flexor sites such as the neck, axilla, inframammary, and inguinal folds. The pustules are accompanied by fever, elevated neutrophil count, and an acute evolution within two to five days of drug exposure. It is important to differentiate pustular psoriasis from other conditions like acute generalized exanthem pustulosis (AGEP), subcornial pustular dermatosis, and drug reactions.</s> This image describes acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis, a skin disease characterized by cell necrosis of keratinocytes, subcondyl intraepidermal pustules, papillomatosis, and acanthosis. The condition is often caused by an offending drug and can be managed by stopping the drug, applying topical corticosteroids, and providing supportive care if necessary.

This image refers to neurofibromatosis, a skin disease characterized by the growth of benign tumors on nerve tissue, which can cause various symptoms such as pain, disfigurement, and functional impairment. The exact cause of neurofibromatosis is unknown, but it is believed to be related to genetic factors. Treatment options may include surgery, radiation therapy, and medications to manage symptoms.

This image discusses melasma, a skin disease characterized by patches of discoloration, typically brown or gray, caused by hormonal changes and sun exposure. It can be treated with topical creams, sun protection, and in some cases, laser therapy.

This image appears to depict a person with trigeminal neuralgia, a facial pain disorder characterized by intense, sudden, and often stabbing pain in the cheeks, jaw, and teeth. Treatment options include medications, nerve blocks, and surgery.

Actinic keratosis is more common in those with outdoor occupations or hobbies, and can be diagnosed by inducing pain when gently abrading the lesion with a fingernail.

This image discusses skin cancers of the eyelid, which are not specific to the eyelid but can occur there. Common types of skin cancer on the eyelid include basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is the most common type of skin cancer and can occur on various parts of the body, including the eyelid. It is non-invasive and non-metastasizing, making it a relatively harmless cancer. On the other hand, medial canthus basal cell carcinoma is more dangerous as it has a poor prognosis and a tendency to spread to the inside, infecting the lacrimal system. The risk factors for developing skin cancer include sunlight exposure.

In ITP, there is an increase in platelet aggregation, leading to the formation of clots in small blood vessels. The GP2B3A antibody is often involved in the pathogenesis of ITP, binding to platelets and causing them to be sent to the spleen. In chronic ITP, the platelet count is usually less than 20,000 per micro liter of blood, while in acute ITP, the count can be as low as 5,000 per micro liter. The cause of ITP can be idiopathic or secondary to other disorders such as systemic lupus erythematosus or lymphoma. Clinical symptoms of ITP include hemorrhagic bullae, splenomegaly, and an increased risk of bleeding. The diagnosis of ITP can be confirmed through laboratory tests such as a decrease in platelet count and an increase in bleeding time.</s> This image shows a skin disease called immune thrombocytopenic purpura, which is caused by auto antibodies attacking the patient's own platelets. The spleen plays a crucial role in this condition, as it can cause the formation of auto antibodies and disrupt the antibody-antigen interaction. In some cases, splenectomy may be the best option for treatment, as it can remove the spleen and prevent further damage to the patient's platelets.

This image describes a test for evaluating the competence of perforators in the saphenous vein using an elastocrep bandage applied from toes to thigh. The test involves observing for varicosities in four segments: above the first tourniquet, in the adductor canal between the first and second tourniquets, below the knee between the second and third tourniquets, and below the ankle below the third tourniquet. A rapid appearance of varicosities indicates incompetent perforators, while collapsed veins indicate competent perforators.

The lesions appear as erythematous, edematous plaques with a pustular pattern, and may be symmetrically or asymmetrically distributed on the body and face. The histopathological features of sweet syndrome include massive edema, dense leukocytic infiltration with a starburst pattern in the mid-dermis, and the presence of histocytoid cells. The disease is diagnosed through careful clinical examination and may involve multiple organ systems.

This image describes keratoacanthoma, a skin disease characterized by a firm, dome-shaped, reddish-colored lesion with a central keratin plug. It is caused by various factors such as sunlight, chemical carcinogens, human papilloma viruses, and genetic predisposition. The disease is more common in younger individuals, males, and those with darker skin. It commonly occurs on the face, neck, upper extremities, and lips, and can be painful with regional lymphadenopathy. The clinical course of keratoacanthoma is unusual, with the lesion growing firm and consistency for 4-8 weeks, remaining static in size, and then regressing with the expulsion of keratin plug and resorption of mass.

This image shows a patient with immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP), a quantitative platelet disorder characterized by a decrease in platelet count. In ITP, there is an increase in platelet aggregation against autoantibodies, leading to the spleen removing platelets. The condition can be acute or chronic, with the chronic form being less severe than the acute form. In children, ITP may be caused by viral infections or immunizations. During pregnancy, ITP may be secondary to other disorders such as systemic lupus erythematosus or lymphoma. Clinical symptoms of ITP include hemorrhagic bullae and an increased spleen size. Laboratory findings may include a decreased platelet count and an increased bleeding time.</s> This image shows a patient with type 2 hypersensitivity, a condition where the immune system attacks tissues. In this case, the patient has immune thrombocytopenic autoantibodies against RBCs, which can cause a decrease in platelet count. Treatment options include splenectomy, a surgical removal of the spleen, which is the best and permanent option for patients with this condition.

This image discusses skin cancers of the eyelid, which are not specific to the eyelid but can occur there. Common types of skin cancer on the eyelid include basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is the most common type of skin cancer and can occur on various parts of the body, including the eyelid. It is slow-growing, non-invasive, and non-metastasizing. The most common location for basal cell carcinoma on the eyelid is the lower eyelid, followed by the medial canthus, upper eyelid, and lateral canthus. Medial canthus basal cell carcinoma is more dangerous due to its tendency to spread and invade the lacrimal system. The risk factors for skin cancer include sunlight exposure.

This image shows dystrophic calcification, a condition characterized by the accumulation of abnormal deposits in the skin, with two types: dystrophic and metastatic. Dystrophic calcification is typically caused by damage to heart valves, which can lead to the formation of tumors and the presence of specialized soma bodies. These bodies are lamellar and can be found in various tumors, including meningiomas, papillary carcinomas, thyroid tumors, and ovarian tumors. In dystrophic calcification, there is no alteration in calcium levels, but an increase in phosphate levels can lead to bone destruction and interlinking with calcium levels. The condition can be diagnosed through examination of damaged heart valves and the presence of whitish streaks in the skin. Treatment options may include surgery to remove affected areas.
