This image describes a surgical technique for treating skin diseases, specifically caruncular lesions. The procedure involves suturing posterior flaps for additional lining, or excision of the remaining mucosa on both sides and removal of a little tissue from the caruncle. The use of medpore coated tubes and mucosal alternatives are considered for biocompatibility and reduced clogging with secretions.
This image shows a nodular BCC, the most common type of basal cell carcinoma, which appears as a raised, firm, pink papule in the dermis and epidermis.
This image shows a thick melanoma with a blue or red color appearance, indicating a potentially serious skin disease that requires prompt medical attention and treatment.

This image describes acral lentiginous melanoma, a type of melanoma that occurs on the soles of the feet and is more common in individuals with darker skin. It tends to be diagnosed later and has a higher risk of spreading throughout the body, which is why it is important to check the bottom of feet during a full skin check.

This image shows the progression of diphtheria, a bacterial infection that causes a thick, grayish-brown pseudomembrane to form on the throat and tonsils. The membrane can lead to tissue necrosis, respiratory obstruction, and systemic complications such as cardiomyopathy and neuropathy. Diphtheria is typically treated with antitoxin and supportive care, and it is important to be aware of the potential complications and to differentiate it from other diseases with similar symptoms.</s> This image describes diphtheria as a bacterial infection caused by Corynebacterium diphtheriae, characterized by the production of toxin that can lead to skin lesions, airway obstruction, and potentially fatal complications. Treatment involves administering antitoxin based on clinical diagnosis, with penicillin and erythromycin used as antibiotics to halt bacterial production. Supportive care includes droplet precautions and vaccination according to the national immunization schedule.

This image shows a case of mixed infection with both mucormycosis and bacterial culture, highlighting the importance of extra vigilance by microbiologists to identify and diagnose such cases. Mucormycosis is a fungal infection that can be difficult to diagnose, as it can mimic other conditions and has low sensitivity in cultures. Identification of the fungi can be achieved through various methods such as KOH mount, culture on artificial media, and sequencing. In this case, the patient had a mucor infection, which was confirmed through the growth of the fungus on specialized media and identification using malgi top.

This image shows a skin disease in a dog with a circular, crusty appearance, which may be indicative of fungal dermatitis. The disease may be caused by underlying factors such as parasitic diseases, hypersensitivity, or endocrine disorders. To diagnose and treat the condition, a veterinarian may take a patient's fear, perform cytology, and use systemic antibiotics or topical therapy with antiseptics like chlorhexidine or benzoyl peroxide. The treatment protocol may involve changing antibiotics if resistance is detected, and combining bactericidal and bacteriostatic antibiotics for synergistic effects. It is important to follow proper treatment principles and avoid indiscriminate use of antibiotics to prevent antimicrobial resistance.

This image shows a contrast between two skin diseases, one with a red and inflamed appearance and the other with a more subtle, patchy pattern. The causes of these diseases can vary, but may include genetic factors, environmental exposures, or immune system dysfunction. Treatment options may include topical creams, light therapy, or immunosuppressive medications, depending on the specific disease and its severity.

This image shows a person with a severe skin disease characterized by a disfiguring rash and redness, caused by an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical creams and immunosuppressive medications.

This image shows a close-up of a skin disease characterized by a red, raised, and itchy patch on the skin, likely caused by an allergic reaction or irritation. Treatment options may include topical creams or ointments, avoiding triggers, and seeking medical advice.

This image depicts a person with a severe skin disease characterized by a disfiguring rash and redness, caused by an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical steroids and immunosuppressive medications.

This image describes the management of hereditary angioedema, a skin disease characterized by recurrent swelling of the face, lips, tongue, and throat. Treatment includes reassurance, antihistamines, and, in some cases, long-acting non-treatment treatments.
This image shows a neurofibroma, a benign skin growth consisting of fibrous tissue and nerve cells, which can appear as a raised, brown or black lesion on the skin.

This image discusses a person experiencing pain and discomfort in their wrist and shoulder, which they suspect may be due to carpal tunnel syndrome. They have also noticed the appearance of red freckles on their skin, some of which they believe may be moles, and are considering visiting a dermatologist to have them checked out.</s> This image is about a person who is concerned about their family history of skin cancer and is considering getting checked for it. They have an upcoming OBGYN appointment and are trying to schedule a dermatology appointment to address their concerns.
This image shows patchy hyperpigmentation in tanned or darker skin, which can be caused by sun exposure and may require treatment to even out skin tone.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a change in skin color, likely due to inflammation or an autoimmune response. Treatment options may include topical steroids, immunosuppressants, or light therapy, depending on the underlying cause.
This image shows leukocytic infiltration with intracytoplasmic inclusions in the skin, indicating a possible autoimmune skin disease.

This image shows a melanoma, a type of aggressive skin cancer, commonly occurring on the lower limbs of female individuals with fair skin. It is characterized by asymmetry, irregular borders, and a change in color.

This image depicts a Moorfield UCL glaucoma seminar, where experts discuss the causes and treatment options for this eye disease, which affects the optic nerve and can lead to vision loss if left untreated.
This image shows a skin disease characterized by a red, raised, and itchy patch on the skin, caused by an allergic reaction to an irritant, and can be treated with antihistamines and topical creams.

This image shows a close-up of a Verrucus epidermal nevus, a skin condition characterized by a raised, brown or pink bump with a smooth surface, commonly found on the face. The cause of this condition is not fully understood, but it is believed to be related to hormonal changes or genetic factors. Treatment options include topical creams or laser therapy, depending on the size and location of the nevus.

This image shows a red, bright plaque on the skin, which is a type of superficial basal cell carcinoma. It is characterized by its lack of an edge and the absence of vessels, and can often be multiple. These carcinomas are challenging to treat due to their close proximity to the epidermis, and are commonly treated with skin-directed therapies such as cryotherapy.

This image describes seborrheic dermatitis, an inflammatory skin condition characterized by papillosquamous lesions, commonly affecting areas rich in sebaceous glands and occurring in infants, middle-aged adults, and those with immunodeficiency or certain neurological conditions.
This image describes a potential skin disease characterized by type infections, which can be treated with a third generation Kepler spore.
This image shows spokewheel-like areas on the skin, which can be removed through a specific treatment.
This image depicts a newly emerging skin disease in previously asymptomatic children, characterized by an unspecified rash or lesion, with an unknown cause and treatment options.
This image depicts a theriasis palpebrum, a skin disease characterized by the infestation of theriasis palpebrum.
This image shows masses in the iris, which can be caused by various factors such as cysts and pigment lesions, and not just melanomas.

This image shows a close-up of a verrucous epidermal nevus, a skin condition characterized by a wart-like growth with a rough, scaly surface, typically found on the face. The cause of this condition is not fully understood, but it may be related to genetics or immune system dysfunction. Treatment options include topical creams, cryotherapy, and surgical removal, depending on the size and location of the nevus.

This image shows a port wine stain, a skin condition associated with Sturge-Weber syndrome and Klippert-Renouer syndrome, characterized by a reddish-purple birthmark along the distribution of the trigeminal nerve on the face.

This image shows a back massager roller from Amazon, designed to help relieve tension and improve circulation in hard-to-reach areas of the back, commonly affected by skin diseases such as acne and psoriasis.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash with a raised, scaly appearance. The cause of this condition is not yet fully understood, but it may be related to an autoimmune disorder. Treatment options include topical creams and ointments, as well as avoiding triggers such as certain foods and environmental irritants.

This image depicts a seborrheic keratosis, a common skin condition characterized by raised, brown, wart-like lesions that can appear on the face, chest, or back. The exact cause of seborrheic keratosis is unknown, but it is thought to be related to an overproduction of keratin, a protein that makes up the outer layer of the skin. Treatment options include topical creams, freezing the lesions with liquid nitrogen, or surgical removal.

This image depicts a patient with a vascular-based disease, possibly vaccine-associated ischemic dermatopathy, presenting with a mass at the shoulder caused by a vaccine injection site reaction. The patient has a history of breeds not typical of dermatomyositis, suggesting a possible combination of diseases. Biopsy may be necessary to determine the exact cause of the nodule and other lesions.

This image depicts a benign bony outgrowth called a terminal phalanx tumor, commonly found on the great toe or rarely on a finger, affecting individuals between 10 to 35 years of age. The exact cause is unclear, but trauma may be a contributing factor. Pain is the primary symptom, and treatment involves removing the tumor using a bone nibbler or chisel.
This image describes an ulcer that can erode the stomach wall and potentially cause bleeding or form a hole, though it is rare.

This image shows a close-up view of a person's nose, which appears to have a reddish tint and a bumpy texture, potentially indicating an inflammatory skin condition such as acne or eczema. The exact cause of the skin disease cannot be determined without further examination, but common treatments include topical creams, antibiotics, and lifestyle changes to manage symptoms and prevent future flare-ups.

This image describes a punch biopsy procedure, including the process of obtaining a tissue specimen, suturing the wound, and monitoring the patient for complications. The biopsy involves cutting the base of the specimen at the level of fat, placing it in a labeled bottle with formalin, and checking the punched instrument for any remaining tissue. After the biopsy, the wound is sutured with simple interrupted stitches, and the patient is monitored for bleeding, infection, and scarring. Complications are rare, but the suture may fail in some cases, and the surgeon should be aware of the patient's anatomy to avoid complications.
This image shows a thick plug of cartilage that is not being mineralized, which appears as radiolucency on a histologic prep and may indicate a skin disease.
This image shows two cases of syphilis with aortitis, featuring traditional syphilitic ascending aortic dilation or aneurysm accompanied by heavy calcification of the ascending aorta.
This image describes melanoma, a type of skin cancer, which can metastasize to the brain and may require surgery, especially if it affects a lung lobe.

This image shows small outpouchings of the mucosa in the bronchi, which contain Oslerus osleri larvae. These parasites can cause respiratory problems if the life cycle is not interrupted and large granulomas form.

This image discusses the importance of addressing deep, firm, and large lumps or masses in the body, which may not be benign and could be indicative of soft tissue sarcomas. Patients with neurofibromatosis and NF1 are at a higher risk of developing malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNSTs).

This image shows a child with Pteriasis alba, a skin disease characterized by white, scaly patches on the face. The cause of this condition is not fully understood, but it may be related to genetics or exposure to certain environmental factors. Treatment options include topical creams and ointments to help remove the scales and promote healing.

This image displays multifocal diffuse pigmentation, which can be caused by physiologic factors such as drugs or smoking, or by inflammation. Treatment options may include topical creams or laser therapy.

This image shows a skin disease called lichen planus, which presents as purplish papules or plaques with extreme itching. Psoriasis manifests as well-defined erythematous micaceous scaly plaques over extensor surfaces. Tinea corporis presents as annular lesions that are extremely itchy and can be ruled out using the koh mode test. Pterosis rosa is a skin condition that tends to resolve spontaneously within 6 to 12 weeks and can be differentiated from other skin diseases by its eruption's moderate pruritus.

This image describes three types of Congenital Tosses: skin eyelid crease absent, tautic eyelid retraction with jaw opening, and myogenic tosses. Skin eyelid crease absent is characterized by a higher than normal lid in the down gaze position. Tautic eyelid retraction with jaw opening is caused by misdirected cross innervation between the oculomotor nerve and the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve. Myogenic tosses are usually associated with diplopia, ocular thyropathy with restricted ocular movement, and mechanical tosses caused by increased weight of the upper eyelid or inflammation. Investigations, including corneal sensitivity, ocular motility, and Bell's phenomena, are important before surgery. Surgical procedures indicated in mild tosses include tarsal plate axion, levator resection, and slings procedure. Severe tosses require 4 mm or more pupillary area coverage and levator muscle resection.

This image describes a study of 56 patients with skin diseases caused by Nocaria brasiliensis, Actinomadure, and Madure. The treatment involved 3 weeks of amikacin followed by 5 weeks of amikacin and sulfamethoxazole. Eleven patients experienced alterations in audiometry, with moderate unilateral hypoacusia in 2 patients and a decrease in creatinine clearance in 1 patient. Four patients required 4 cycles of treatment, and all were cured except for 3 who experienced recurrence.

This image shows confetti-like athiasis, also known as congenital reticular ecthysiform erythroderma, which is a skin disease characterized by a red, scaly rash with a net-like pattern. The cause of this condition is unknown, and treatment options may include topical creams or ointments, phototherapy, or systemic medications.

This image discusses the harmful effects of the sun on the skin, including actinic keratosis and rhinophyma, and the importance of proper diagnosis and treatment by dermatologists. It highlights the role of agency in working with medical professionals to address skin conditions and emphasizes the need for a daily skincare routine to prevent or treat skin damage caused by the sun.

This image shows the importance of protecting the skin to prevent maceration, a condition characterized by soft, wrinkled, and potentially discolored skin due to prolonged exposure to moisture or irritants.

This image depicts a skin disease characterized by a red, itchy rash next to the heart, accompanied by a burning sensation and a feeling of warmth. The cause of this condition is not clear, but it may be related to an allergic reaction or an infection. Treatment options include topical creams and ointments, as well as oral medications to reduce inflammation and itching.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by inflammation and reduced blood flow. The cause of this condition is not provided, but potential treatment options may include topical steroids or immunosuppressants.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by a red, raised, and itchy rash, caused by an immune response to a foreign substance. Treatment options include topical creams and oral medications to reduce inflammation and suppress the immune response.

This image shows a skin disease characterized by red, itchy patches on the trunk and lower limbs, which can be caused by an allergic reaction or an infection and can be treated with topical creams or oral medications.

This image depicts a case of psoriasis, a chronic autoimmune skin disease characterized by thick, scaly patches covered with white scales, often found on the elbows, knees, and scalp. The exact cause of psoriasis is unknown, but it is believed to be triggered by a combination of genetic and environmental factors. Treatment options include topical creams, phototherapy, and systemic medications, depending on the severity and location of the disease.

Markings for an M-T rearrangement. An incision is marked perpendicular to the web to form a “T.” This incision is parallel to a crease in the anterolateral neck. The length of this incision is determined by the pinch test. Then, a triangular flap whose apex is at the intersection of the limbs of the “T” is marked. This is an equilateral triangle, with the limbs and base of the triangle measuring the same length as the releasing incision. The base of the triangular flap is then connected to the superior and inferior edges of the webbing to form an “M.” Note that the inferolateral displacement of hair-bearing skin is present within the superior triangle.

This manuscript describes a previously unreported clinical case of Salmonella enterica serovar infantis associated with ulcerative keratitis in a dog. Local immunosuppression of the corneal surface likely contributed to this opportunistic infection. Antimicrobial therapy with topical ofloxacin, tobramycin, and systemic enrofloxacin was successful in resolving the infection.

Granulosis rubra nasi (GRN) is a rare genodermatosis involving the eccrine glands clinically characterized by localized hyperhidrosis, erythema, papules, pustules, and vesicles over central region of face. GRN is asymptomatic, spontaneously resolves during puberty, and treatment options have inconsistent results. We hereby present a case of GRN in 38 years female with sites and dermoscopy findings not defined so far.

The device is placed gently against the skin with the wire ends protruding. When the handle of the platysmotome is turned, the metal loop cuts the muscle, and the wire emerges from the skin. It is important to realize that the circular plate, with the small center hole through which the two ends of the wire pass, is essential to keeping the wires held tightly together, preventing them from separating in the effort to cut the muscle, which could widen the puncture hole or cut the skin. Maintaining the puncture holes of the steel wire point-like is an indispensable condition of this technique because it guarantees rapid closure of the holes without scarring.

We isolated the gracilis, adductor magnus, and adductor longus muscle using electrocautery and then folded them in the dead space around the anus, achieving a padding effect. After restoring the subscrotal margin using the biceps femoris muscle with a high relaxation ability and arc of rotation, we used the semimembranosus and semitendinosus muscle for a partial resection of the fascia and adipose tissue, preserving the vascular pedicle, to match the shape of the recipient site, thus adjusting the thickness of the flap.

First steps of the wound dressing application. Anterolateral view of patient 1’s mixed etiology ulcer with multiple lymphatic fistulas on the right leg. When this photograph was taken, the previous conventional Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT) had already temporarily been stopped (over a period of 3 days) because the patient developed a local allergic reaction to the conventionally used adhesive films. Instead, various silver-coated dressings were applied, but unfortunately, we were unable to create a dry sterile medium for the wound healing. A window was cut around the wound contours on the inner side of the hydrocolloid dressing. The outer side deliberately covered not only the wound edges but also part of the surrounding skin. Double-sided adhesive hydrogel strips had already been placed around the hydrocolloid dressings.

Q-angle (quadriceps angle) is the angle between the quadriceps tendon and the patellar tendon. It provides useful information about the knee joint’s alignment. The Q-angle is formed in the frontal plane by two line segments—one drawn from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the center of the patella, and the other drawn from the center of the patella to the tibial tubercle. An increased Q-angle is a risk factor for patellar subluxation.

Malignant syphilid is an extreme and rare form, combining the ulcerative and pustular variants. Papulopustular lesions become necrotic and form deep ulcers with thick, dirty crusts. The walls of the ulcer are soft rather than indurated. There are usually relatively few lesions, and the head and neck are favored. There may be associated oral mucosal changes. The patients are quite ill with fever, chills, weight loss, and even hepatitis, but they do not have lymphadenopathy. Some may die if not treated promptly. Malignant syphilid appears more common in HIV-infected and other immunosuppressed patients, but usually there is no explanation. The patient simply never develops an appropriate immune response, while his or her sexual partner(s) handle the T. pallidum well. There is no proof that the organism is more virulent.

Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome represents the most severe form of meningococcal sepsis, with cutaneous necrosis and disseminated intravascular coagulation. caption: Developing raised circles quickly become dark and papular with marked central necrosis, umbilication, frequent crusting, and surrounding edema. There is local lymphangitis and regional lymphadenitis, often with secondary infection. Involution of nodules usually starts after 2 weeks, but larger nodules, particularly on the legs, break down to form itchy, painful ulcers up to 2 cm in diameter. Gradual healing occurs over 2 months to leave circular cicatrized scars. Differential diagnosis includes insect bites in the early stages, monkeypox, where the evolution of a large number of lesions is usually rapid, and tropical ulcers which are usually larger with foul, green slough and are slow to heal. Early tanapox and smallpox could be confused, so it is important to make the correct diagnosis. Crusted lesions could be mistaken for anthrax, but the evolution in tanapox is much slower.

image_path: validation_data/Alan D. Irvine- Albert C. Yan- Peter H. Hoeger - Textbook of pediatric dermatology-Wiley-Blackwell (2011)_00072_02977.png
caption: The length of the hair peg and the three zones of the developing follicle are exaggerated in some regions of the skin but more subtle in others. Changes occur in all three regions, with the first notable events taking place at the proximal and distal ends. Elongated core cells in the neck of the follicle continue into the epidermis, where they form a strand of cells that lies between the basal and intermediate cell layers. This is the hair tract that marks the position and pathway of the presumptive hair canal. The distal end of the hair peg flattens, and the epithelial cells along this basal border elongate to form a distinct layer that establishes the matrix. The flattened end of the follicle begins to invaginate into the cord, shaping the bulb with the matrix as the roof of the bulb. Mitotic figures are evident in matrix cells, and longitudinally orientated cells, presumably the progeny of the dividing matrix cells, move out of the matrix into the center of the cord, thereby establishing the first layers of the inner root sheath and the hair (the hair cone). Cells of the outer layer of the follicle located adjacent and lateral to the matrix appear to become more loosely associated with one another, perhaps permitting the inward migration of the cells derived from the matrix. Melanocytes aggregate in the matrix and produce melanin ahead of melanocytes in the general body skin, thus making the bulbs of developing hair follicles ideal sites to examine when evaluating skin biopsy samples from a fetus at risk of tyrosinase-negative oculocutaneous albinism. Such samples can be induced to synthesize melanin by the DOPA reaction if the fetus is normal. The fate of the original K1/K10-positive core cells after the matrix begins to function is not clear, but it is possible that they remain as the layer of cells that lies interior from and perpendicular to the columnar cells of the outer layer of the outer root sheath. Once the follicle is fully differentiated, however, only cells within the infundibulum stain with antibodies that recognize the K1 and K10 keratins of the intermediate layer of fetal epidermal cells. During these events, the cord is surrounded in its entirety by several layers of elongated mesenchymal cells that form a sheath. The connective tissue matrix is sparse within this cellular sheath and appears to be devoid of the fibrillar collagens that are present in the surrounding subepidermal and interstitial matrix. Differences in matrix molecules are observed at different levels of the hair peg and when comparing the dermal papilla with the follicle sheath. Between 15 and 17 weeks EGA, bulges of epithelial cells begin to grow out from the epithelial cord on the posterior surface of the follicle, and the adult layers of the follicle differentiate into the hair and internal root sheath. Once these bulges form, the follicle is called a bulbous

caption: This is a melanoma 0.9 mm in thickness. Notice that the blue-white veil at the center of the lesion is more conspicuous under NPD and is difficult to appreciate with PD. However, white shiny lines (known also as crystalline structures) can only be seen with PD. Since the whitish veil is due to orthokeratosis overlying the dermal melanin, it will be most conspicuous when viewed with NPD. In contrast, shiny white lines corresponding to altered stromal collagen will only be visible with PD, which captures the birefringence of collagen. Image taken with NPD.

caption: An increased awareness of the variable presentation of lesions is necessary on the part of the screening physician to identify involvement early on in the disease course. Although the incidence of melanoma is lower in patients with skin of color compared to Caucasians, mortality is significantly higher. Five-year survival rates of 78.2% in African Americans, 84.9% in American Indians, 80.7% in Asians, and 86.5% in Hispanics are compared to 90.2% in Caucasians. This disparity in survival rates may be attributed to poor access to medical care, delay in diagnosis, more aggressive nature of melanoma, and difficulty in diagnosing lesions, which are typically located in less visible body sites, such as the feet.

This healthy 8-year-old girl suffered from recurrent inflammatory, fissured, eczematous dermatitis on the face and perianal region. Patch testing revealed a methylisothiazolinone contact allergy. This preservative was contained in the wet wipes used to cleanse these regions. The eruption promptly cleared when the wipes were discontinued.

image_path: validation_data/Alan D. Irvine- Albert C. Yan- Peter H. Hoeger - Textbook of pediatric dermatology-Wiley-Blackwell (2011)_00587_06569.png
caption: Clinical features: Meningococcal meningitis is the most common form of the disease, is indistinguishable from other forms of childhood bacterial meningitis, usually responds rapidly to antibiotics, and is associated with little long-term morbidity. Meningococcal septicaemia with shock is more devastating, commonly presenting with nonspecific symptoms of fever, vomiting, headache, abdominal pain, and muscle aches. The rash of meningococcal septicaemia may be very subtle in the early stages and its recognition is imperative if this life-threatening condition is to be diagnosed early and treated effectively. In the early stages of meningococcal infection, the rash may resemble a viral exanthem such as rubella. This erythematous maculopapular rash is non-purpuric, non-pruritic, and often very transient. Following a diligent search of all areas of the body, the purpuric lesions of meningococcal septicaemia are seen in 80–90% of patients. The rash usually occurs within 12–36 hours of the onset of the disease and is characteristically petechial but may blanch early in the course of the infection. In the classic presentation of meningococcal septicaemia, the petechiae are irregular, small, and often have raised centers. Lesions commonly occur on the limbs and trunk but may be found on the head, palms, soles, and mucous membranes. They may also occur on areas subjected to friction or under pressure by clothing. Progression of the rash of meningococcal septicaemia has been shown to relate to the severity of the coagulopathy and prognosis. As the disease becomes more fulminant, lesions coalesce to form large ecchymoses and become hemorrhagic and bullous. These areas of extensive skin involvement may progress to gangrene of the digits or whole limbs. In such severe purpura fulminans, the gangrenous regions are gunmetal grey or blue-black and are well demarcated.

caption: Tar, dithranol, and retinoids are likely to make the skin sore in the flexures and on the genitalia, so they should not be used. The vitamin D3 analogues calcitriol and tacalcitol are non-irritant and can be used in flexures twice daily and should be tried first. If they do not help, then you can use a topical steroid. The weakest possible steroid to clear the skin is required, but 1% hydrocortisone is ineffective and not worth trying. Use a moderate topical steroid such as 0.05% clobetasone butyrate or 0.1% hydrocortisone 17-butyrate cream or ointment applied twice a day. Do not use topical steroids in the flexures for long periods of time, as steroid atrophy and striae are likely.

caption: Thiazide, and calcium channel blockers. The differential diagnosis of SCLE includes tinea corporis, psoriasis, erythema annulare centrifugum, dermatomyositis, and nummular eczema. CCLE contains several subtypes, with the most common subtype being discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE). DLE lesions initially present as erythematous, scaly papules that become plaques with central atrophy and hypopigmentation, surrounding hyperpigmentation, and firmly adherent scale that are painful if lifted manually (“carpet tack sign”). In hair-bearing areas, scarring alopecia is commonly seen. Localized DLE can be found in sites above the neck that are either in photo-exposed (e.g., face, scalp) or photoprotected areas (e.g., ears). These highly visible lesions can be cosmetically disfiguring in darker skin types. Generalized DLE can include the trunk (upper more than lower) and extensor surfaces of the extremities and represent a higher risk of systemic disease. Other skin diseases considered in the differential include lichen planus, lichen planopilaris, cicatricial pemphigoid, vitiligo, post-inflammatory hypopigmentation, and squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinomas arising from CCLE have been reported in African American patients. Lupus erythematosus panniculitis is a rare subtype of CCLE characterized by subcutaneous tender nodules and plaques that may adhere to overlying skin. The lesions typically appear on the upper thighs, buttocks, breast, and face and resolve with deep atrophy. Lupus erythematosus tumidus (LET) presents in photosensitive areas with urticarial-like annular plaques with little epidermal involvement. Lupus-nonspecific lesions, some of which are listed, are more often seen in SLE patients. Diffuse and linear nail dyschromia has been detected in 52% of African Americans with SLE. Oral ulcers and photosensitivity, the latter of which is less commonly seen in blacks, are included in the American College of Rheumatology diagnostic criteria for SLE.

Erythema of 10-20 mm indicates a positive lepromin test. It signifies hypersensitivity but is scarcely of clinical relevance. This reaction is known as the early reaction or Fernandez reaction. The early reaction may transition into a chronic inflammatory node with a diameter greater than 5 mm. The tissue is excised and histologically examined. In the late reaction (Mitsuda reaction, lepromin reaction), there is either a tuberculoid or sarcoid granuloma. A positive lepromin test indicates tuberculoid leprosy with good resistance and favorable prognosis, while a negative test shows lepromatous or dimorphic leprosy with poor resistance. Individuals in contact with leprosy patients in endemic areas who have negative lepromin tests should be prophylactically treated or evacuated due to the risk of infection. In leprosy-free areas, the lepromin test is similar to the tuberculin test; it is positive in over 80% of the population and suggests an immunological response to M. tuberculosis. In the human body, Schwann cells become the host cells for M. leprae, which reach these cells either directly through the skin or via the bloodstream.

image_path: validation_data/(James, Andrew-s Disease of the Skin) William D. James MD, Timothy Berger MD, Dirk Elston MD - Andrews- Diseases of the Skin- Clinical Dermatology -Saunders (2005)-OCR1_00229_03446_0.png
caption: Classic lesions are uncommon in children with lichen planus, who often have affected family members and are more frequently seen in children from the Indian subcontinent. Nail changes are found in 5% to 10% of patients, with higher proportionality in children. Lichen planus often affects only the nail apparatus in children, presenting as longitudinal ridging, splitting, onycholysis, and redness of the lunula. The entire nail matrix may be involved, leading to obliteration of the nail plate due to idiopathic atrophy. Lichen planus can mimic Yellow Nail Syndrome and is characterized by pterygium formation, with fibrotic replacement of the nail matrix and fusion of the proximal nail fold with the nailbed. Additionally, lichen planus is linked to twenty-nail dystrophy of childhood, usually resolving spontaneously. The mucous membranes, particularly the oral mucosa, are also commonly affected, with 15% of patients with oral lichen planus also exhibiting skin lesions. Women comprise 75% of oral lichen planus cases, typically beginning 10 years later than in men. Oral lesions can be reticulate, erythematous, or ulcerative, often presenting multiple patterns with one predominating; the ulcerative form is the most common. Adjacent reticulate and erythematous lesions often accompany ulcerative areas, with the erythematous pattern predominant in 37% of cases. Symptoms are least commonly associated with reticulate lesions.
Pustular psoriasis can occur as the primary form of psoriasis in an individual patient or in patients with established plaque-type psoriasis. Pustules usually develop rapidly.

caption: Oral Retinoid Therapy Isotretinoin is approved only for severe cystic acne; however, it is also useful in less severe forms of acne to prevent the need for continuous treatment and the repeated office visits that many patients require. Experts consensus indicates that oral isotretinoin treatment is warranted for severe acne, poorly responsive acne that improves by less than 50% after 6 months of therapy with combined oral and topical antibiotics, acne that relapses off oral treatment, scars, or acne that induces psychological distress. Additionally, agreed indications include gram-negative folliculitis, inflammatory rosacea, pyoderma faciale, acne fulminans, and hidradenitis suppurativa. This retinoid is a reliable remedy in nearly all acne patients. The dose ranges from 0.5 to 1 mg/kg/day in one or two doses. For severe trunkal acne in patients who tolerate higher doses, treatment may be given in doses up to 2 mg/kg/day. In practice, most patients start at a 20- to 40-mg dose to avoid an early flare, and then increase to 40 to 80 mg/day to limit side effects.

image_path: validation_data/(James, Andrew-s Disease of the Skin) William D. James MD, Timothy Berger MD, Dirk Elston MD - Andrews- Diseases of the Skin- Clinical Dermatology -Saunders (2005)-OCR1_00312_04779_0.png
caption: Tinea barbae, also known as ringworm of the beard, tinea sycosis, and barber's itch, is not a common disease. It primarily affects those in agricultural pursuits, particularly those in contact with farm animals. The condition usually presents unilaterally on the neck or face. There are two clinical types identified: deep, nodular, suppurative lesions; and superficial, crusted, partially bald patches with folliculitis. The deep type develops slowly and produces nodular thickenings and kerion-like swellings, typically caused by T. mentagrophytes or T. verrucosum. The swellings are confluent, forming diffuse boggy infiltrations with abscesses. The overlying skin is inflamed, the hairs are loose or absent, and pus may be expressed through the remaining follicular openings. Generally, the lesions are confined to one part of the face or neck in men. The superficial type is characterized by a less inflammatory pustular folliculitis, possibly associated with T. violaceum or T. rubrum. The affected hairs can sometimes be easily extracted. Rarely, E. floccosum may cause widespread verrucous lesions known as verrucous epidermophytosis.

image_path: validation_data/(James, Andrew-s Disease of the Skin) William D. James MD, Timothy Berger MD, Dirk Elston MD - Andrews- Diseases of the Skin- Clinical Dermatology -Saunders (2005)-OCR1_00366_05612_0.png
caption: There is a secondary eruption with no visible chancre, and the glands below Poupart’s ligament are markedly enlarged, suggesting that an anal chancre should be suspected. Atypical chancres are common. Simultaneous infection by a spirochete and another microbial agent may produce an atypical chancre. The mixed chancre caused by infection with Haemophilus ducreyi and T. pallidum will produce a lesion with a course different from either chancroid or primary syphilis alone. Such a sore begins a few days after exposure, since the incubation period for chancroid is short, and later the sore may transform into an indurated syphilitic lesion. A phagedenic chancre results from the combination of a syphilitic chancre and contaminating bacteria that may cause severe tissue destruction and result in scarring. Edema indurativum, or penile venereal edema, is marked solid edema of the labia or the prepuce and glans penis accompanying a chancre. Chancre redux is a relapse of a chancre with insufficient treatment, accompanied by enlarged lymph nodes. Pseudochancre redux is a gumma occurring at the site of a previous chancre, distinguished from relapsing chancre by the absence of lymphadenopathy and a negative darkfield examination. Syphilitic balanitis of Follmann may occur in the absence of a chancre, with lesions that may be exudative, circinate, or erosive.

Of the cutaneous reactions associated with vaccination, delayed large local reactions are reported more often in females and are often associated with more systemic symptoms than other vaccine-associated cutaneous findings. Pigmentosus may also involve the face and nose as brown or greyish black macules. Acanthosis nigricans, often seen in flexural areas, can present with hyperpigmentation on the ala nasi. Severe forms and atypically located lesions can be associated with malignancies. Ephelides (freckles) are common pigmented lesions of the nose and cheeks, often seen in early childhood. The patients are usually fair-skinned and have blue or green eyes. The tan-brown irregular, small macules become prominent in summer. Lentigo can also be located on the nose, which is mostly covered with lentigines and ephelides in xeroderma pigmentosum. The early (in situ) lesions of lentigo malignant melanoma, called lentigo maligna, present with irregular hyperpigmented macules on the nose. Mostly related to chronic sun exposure, the lesions become elevated as the tumor progresses into the invasive stage. Early diagnosis is important for effective treatment. The early lesions of seborrheic keratosis, pigmented actinic keratosis, and lentigo solaris can resemble lentigo maligna. Blue or bluish grey, usually unilateral pigmentation located on the skin and mucosal surfaces supplied by the first and second branches of the trigeminal nerve, is seen in nevus of Ota. Affected areas can include the eyelids, forehead, temple, nasal root, and ala nasi by this dermal melanocytic tumor. Additionally, bluish grey pigmentation of the sclera is observed.

caption: Once all the appropriate node fields have been imaged, the location of the sentinel nodes in each field should be marked on the overlying skin. Our protocol involves finding the surface projection of the node with the help of a surface marker and then marking this location permanently using a pinpoint tattoo of carbon black ink, as well as temporarily marking the location with a small cross of indelible ink. In the past, we also marked the surface pathway of the draining lymphatic collectors to facilitate an in-continuity dissection from the melanoma site to the draining node field. This is no longer done as surgical practice has evolved. It is important that the marking is performed with the patient in exactly the same position as that during imaging.

image_path: validation_data/(James, Andrew-s Disease of the Skin) William D. James MD, Timothy Berger MD, Dirk Elston MD - Andrews- Diseases of the Skin- Clinical Dermatology -Saunders (2005)-OCR1_00093_01479_0.png
caption: Nummular Eczema (Nummular Neurodermatitis) usually begins on the lower legs, dorsa of the hands, or extensor surfaces of the arms. The primary lesions are discrete, coin-shaped, erythematous, edematous, vesicular, and crusted patches. Most lesions are 20 to 40 mm in diameter. Lesions may form after trauma (Koebner’s phenomenon). As new lesions appear, old lesions expand by tiny papulovesicular satellite lesions appearing at the periphery and fusing with the main plaque. In severe cases, the condition may spread into palm-sized or larger patches. Pruritus is usually severe and possesses the paroxysmal, compulsive quality, and nocturnal timing seen in circumscribed neurodermatitis. Mast cells are noted in close approximation to nerves in lesional skin, and the neuropeptides substance P and calcitonin gene-related peptide are increased in lesional skin. Atopic dermatitis frequently has nummular morphology in adolescents, but in atopy the lesions tend to be more chronic and lichenified. Histologically, nummular eczema is characterized by acute or subacute spongiotic dermatitis. Initial treatment consists of simple soaking and greasing with an occlusive ointment, and once to twice a day application.

image_path: validation_data/(James, Andrew-s Disease of the Skin) William D. James MD, Timothy Berger MD, Dirk Elston MD - Andrews- Diseases of the Skin- Clinical Dermatology -Saunders (2005)-OCR1_00265_04061_0.png
caption: Paronychia is an inflammatory reaction involving the folds of the skin surrounding the fingernail. It is characterized by acute or chronic purulent, tender, and painful swellings of the tissues around the nail, caused by an abscess in the nail fold. When the infection becomes chronic, horizontal ridges appear at the base of the nail. With recurrent bouts, new ridges appear. The primary predisposing factor that is identifiable is the separation of the eponychium from the nail plate. This separation is usually caused by trauma as a result of moisture-induced maceration of the nail folds from frequent wetting of the hands. This relationship is close enough to justify treating chronic paronychia as work-related in bartenders, food servers, nurses, and others who often wet their hands. The moist grooves of the nail and nail fold become secondarily invaded by pyogenic cocci and yeasts. The causative bacteria are usually S. aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, Pseudomonas species, Proteus species, or anaerobes. The pathogenic yeast is most frequently Candida albicans. The bacteria usually cause acute abscess formation or erythema and swelling, and C. albicans most frequently causes a chronic swelling. If an abscess is suspected, applying light pressure with the index finger against the distal volar aspect of the affected digit will better demonstrate the extent of the collected pus by inducing a well-demarcated blanching. Smears of purulent material will help confirm the clinical impression. Myremecial warts may at times mimic paronychia. Subungual black macules followed by edema, pain, and swelling have been reported to be a sign of osteomyelitis caused by S. aureus or Streptococcus viridans in children with atopic dermatitis.

caption: These ulcers result from trauma to anesthetic feet and typically occur over bony prominences, such as the first metatarsophalangeal joint, the metatarsal heads, or the heel, or at any other site of injury. They are classically deep, painless, and often covered with thick callus. The diagnosis is confirmed by finding sensory loss and identification of an associated disorder that has caused it, such as diabetes, leprosy, paraplegia, peripheral nerve injury, polyneuropathy, or syringomyelia.

Gynecomastia is a condition in which men's breasts increase in size, giving them the appearance of female breasts. The severity of gynecomastia can vary from mild to severe and may be temporary or permanent.

Richter transformation is characterized by the sudden transformation of chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma into a more aggressive form of large cell lymphoma. Symptoms include progressive weight loss and increasing lymph node size. Differential diagnosis includes diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and Hodgkin's lymphoma. Treatment involves immunochemotherapy and stem cell therapy.

In general, 'lichen' implies a pathology featuring a band-like infiltrate of lymphocytes just beneath the epidermis. Clinically, this can appear "lichenoid," characterized by flat-topped pink-purple papules. One final point: three types are often noted for genital rashes; use this knowledge in examinations! LP: pink papules/plaques; LS: atrophic white plaques; LSC: scaly plaque with hyperaccentuated skin markings. Note: excessive scratching of LP or LS may lead to LSC.

Syphilis is caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum and is usually spread through sexual contact. It has three main stages: the primary infection typically presents as a papule that turns into a painless ulcer known as a chancre. In tertiary syphilis, manifestations may include Argyll Robertson pupil, tabes dorsalis, and aortitis. Remember, neurosyphilis can occur at any stage of the disease. If patients are HIV positive, disease progression can be more severe and rapid.

Histologically, Marjolin's ulcer presents as a squamous carcinoma in most cases, although other histological types, such as adenocarcinoma or basal cell carcinoma, may also occur. Marjolin's ulcer can arise in previous burn scars, chronic wounds, chronic ulcers, or even sites of chronic osteomyelitis. It is characterized by its insidious evolution, with a latency period that can extend up to several years.

A hernia is a condition where an organ or tissue protrudes through an opening or weak spot in the surrounding muscle or connective tissue. It occurs when the inner layers of muscle in the abdominal wall become weakened, allowing a part of the intestine or fatty tissue to push through, forming a bulge or lump. Hernias can occur in various parts of the body, including the groin (inguinal hernia), belly button (umbilical hernia), upper part of the stomach (hiatal hernia), or through a surgical incision (incisional hernia). They can be caused by factors such as muscle weakness, straining, heavy lifting, obesity, or pregnancy. Hernias can cause discomfort, pain, or tenderness, particularly when coughing, bending, or lifting. Treatment usually involves surgical repair to strengthen the weakened area and prevent complications.

Phrynoderma: A characteristic dermatosis associated with vitamin A deficiency, characterized by the presence of follicular hyperkeratosis, located on the limbs, abdomen, and back, often accompanied by xerosis and hyperpigmentation.

Frontonasal dysplasia, also known as median cleft syndrome, presents a varied spectrum of facial malformations including frontonasal hypoplasia and severe median clefts in the nose, lips, palate, and forehead. Diagnosis is based on observing the skull, brain, eyes, and extremities.

Onychotillomania is nail picking, and Onychophagia is nail biting. They can also occur in normal aging.

Chronic intermittent pressure from the friction of the elbows or hands on the thighs can cause the release of hemosiderin from erythrocytes and the proliferation of the stratum corneum of the skin, leading to Dahl's sign. Dahl's sign is a clinical sign characterized by areas of thickened and darkened skin on the lower thighs and/or elbows, often seen in patients with severe chronic respiratory disorders such as COPD, ILD, CHF, and chronic moderate to severe persistent asthma.

Hemorrhagic herpes zoster is a rare and serious condition caused by the Varicella-zoster virus. The lower eyelid is the most common site for linear basal cell carcinoma (BCC). Including our three patients, 14 of 42 reported cases were situated in this anatomical location. Although it appears banal, linear BCC of the lower eyelid is significant.
Dermatoscope of primary localized cutaneous nodular amyloidosis on hallux nail bed.

The typical clinical exam initially shows a pustule with surrounding purple/violet color change. After ulceration, features include violaceous or "gun metal grey" borders, rolled borders with an overhang of tissue, and healing with cribriform (bridge-like) scarring.
Association discovered between pemphigus and the prevalence of malignancies in other organs not increased in these patients.
A dentist presents to the clinic with a lesion on his finger diagnosed as acute paronychia.
Cutaneous findings in ________________.
Hint: The patient is at risk for cerebrovascular accident (CVA) and deep vein thrombosis (DVT) due to Antiphospholipid Antibody Syndrome.

Pemphigus foliaceus (PF) does not present with mucosal lesions and results in a higher split, leading to fragile blisters that often break quickly, leaving only the scale or crust visible. PF is generally more recalcitrant and harder to treat compared to pemphigus vulgaris (PV). This complexity influences treatment decisions, such as the use of rituximab, especially in balancing aggressive treatment against other considerations.
Two patients exhibit different dermatoses: Pityriasis versicolor and Acanthosis nigricans.

Hair can grow on transplanned skin. After an injury where the nose was damaged, very thick hair began to grow on the transplanted area. In plastic surgery, the correct term is not "skin transplant" but "skin graft." However, this case involved nasal tip reconstruction using a forehead flap.

Hydatidiform mole (molar pregnancy) is a subtype of diagnosis under Gestational Trophoblastic Disease (GTD), originating from the placenta and capable of metastasis. This tumor uniquely originates from gestational tissue. Other forms include gestational choriocarcinoma and placental site trophoblastic tumors. In a case of a molar pregnancy, a 20-year-old woman in her second trimester developed vaginal bleeding, prompting a dilation and curettage (D&C) procedure.
Crusted / Norwegian scabies in an immunocompromised patient.
What are the characteristics of this condition?
Conjunctival Chemosis.
Correct! The diagnosis is Angular Cheilitis.

Lower lip > upper lip cheilitis suggests UV exposure, leading to actinic cheilitis which requires specific treatment. The entire lower lip shows signs of actinic keratosis. Angular cheilitis is a symptom; identify the underlying cause through a thorough examination. It is not always due to nutritional deficiency. Addressing skin folds can also be beneficial in diagnosis and treatment.
OBGY in labor room.

Wells' syndrome is an idiopathic, recurrent, inflammatory dermatosis that appears to result from dysregulated tissue eosinophilia. It is mostly benign, with constitutional symptoms present. Histology shows flame figures from the deposition of Major Basic Protein on collagen bundles by eosinophils. Differential diagnosis includes skeeter syndrome.

Porcine parvovirus replicates differently at different temperatures, but it’s not clear if there is a standard preference for lower temperatures, nor do we have this in human models. Similarly, the Coxsackie virus showed better replication in mice at 4°C compared to 36°C, though there's no corresponding human data.
59-year-old male with a medical history significant for hypertension, end-stage renal disease on dialysis, presented to the clinic with intensely itchy lesions over the past 1-2 months.

Leprosy, also known as Hansen's disease, is caused by Mycobacterium leprae or Mycobacterium lepromatosis. It leads to damage of the nerves, respiratory tract, skin, and eyes. This nerve damage can result in loss of pain sensation and subsequent loss of extremities parts due to repeated injuries or infections through unnoticed wounds. Consider a case of an 18-year-old female from Kenya presenting with a three-month history of weight loss, painful ulcers of the hands, feet, chest, and face, and numbness of the extremities; HIV negative. What’s the diagnosis?

Actinic keratosis is a precancerous skin condition caused by prolonged exposure to ultraviolet (UV) rays from the sun or tanning beds. It appears as rough patches or blisters on the skin, especially on areas most exposed to sun damage such as the face, scalp, neck, and arms. This condition results from the cumulative damage to skin cells from UV radiation over time.

70M x 2 yrs. Dx: non-AIDS-related, subtype classic. KS types: AIDS-associated and non-AIDS-related, including classic KS, endemic KS, and iatrogenically-induced KS. All types linked to infection with HHV-8.

Congenital Clubfoot, also known as congenital talipes equinovarus, is a common idiopathic deformity of the foot that presents in neonates. It is often associated with conditions such as arthrogryposis, diastrophic dysplasia, tibial hemimelia, and various syndromes including Pierre Robin, Opitz, Larsen, and prune-belly.

Spot diagnosis! Management: Actinomycetoma responds well and may not require surgery. Generally, it requires long-term therapy with antibiotics or antifungal drugs, with or without surgery. Eumycetoma, caused by fungus, responds poorly to medical treatment alone, so it may require debridement and sinus drainage.

63-year-old female presented with a 2-year history of asymptomatic, slowly progressive cutaneous lesions. Medical history included diabetes mellitus, asthma, and osteoarthritis. She had no systemic symptoms, no family history of a similar skin condition.

Vestigial tail" describes structural remnants found in embryonic life or ancestral forms. During the 5th to 6th week of life in the uterus, the human embryo has a tail comprised of 10 to 12 vertebrae. By the 8th week, the human tail disappears.

Stretch marks (striae) are lines or stripes that appear on the skin due to breaks in the elastic fibers of the dermis. They typically result from rapid stretching or sudden changes in weight, including during pregnancy, teenage growth, or rapid weight gain or loss. Initially, stretch marks may appear purple, red, or pink, eventually fading to silver or white. They commonly develop on the stomach, buttoats, thighs, chest, or upper arms, although they can occur anywhere on the body where the skin is stretched. Stress can potentially contribute to the emergence of stretch marks by increasing cortisol levels, which may impact the skin's elasticity and collagen production, thereby making it more susceptible to stretch marks.
Increasing reports of cutaneous reactions to medical devices, including continuous glucose monitoring devices (CGMDs), primarily involve reactions to the sensor.
Cutaneous leishmaniasis differential diagnosis.
Diagnosis: Physical examination revealed hemorrhagic bullae on the tongue and gingiva, and purpura on the arms and legs.
Perichondritis.

The high lipid content of the Malassezia cell wall provides mechanical stability and osmoresistance, which favors adhesion to the host cells, thus preventing phagocytosis and the inflammatory response. Environmental factors like humidity and heat are important.
33-year-old male developed a 3-month history of painful, crusted ulcer on the right ear. Medical history included 8-month history of reactive arthritis. Medications: methotrexate, sulfasalazine.
Needle decompression treats tension pneumothorax. Arthritis complicated by pneumoconiosis is known as: (A) Goodpasture syndrome (B) Caplan syndrome (C) Cystic Fibrosis (D) Marfan syndrome.
Insulin lipohypertrophy lipodystrophy (LD), a disorder of adipose tissue, is a common complication of subcutaneous insulin injections and may present as either lipohymphony (LH) or lipoatrophy (LA).
15 M with patchy alopecia present since birth, not progressing. Diagnosis: A carpet of vellus hairs.
Toxic Shock Syndrome vs. Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis
Diagnosis: Keloid with an open wound, resembling impetigo.

If there is scale, it's likely a papule or plaque. If you close your eyes and palpate a difference between a rash or lesion and normal skin, it's a plaque or papule. Skin conditions like syphilis and sarcoidosis can present in many different ways, making them important to always consider in differential diagnoses.

One of the most pathognomonic Covid nail findings is the red half-moon – red bands at the distal part of the lunula shortly after symptom onset, thought to be due to microvascular injury of the nail bed capillaries. Next up are Mees' lines - transverse white lines across nails, seen 3-6 weeks after symptoms due to altered growth of the nail plate. These should grow out with time. Not specific to Covid, they are found in acute renal failure, CHF, SLE, ulcerative colitis, cancer, other infections, and chemotherapy.

Congenital hypertrophy of the nail fold describes an overgrowth of the nail fold covering the nail plate; it can be treated with topical steroids if inflamed and generally improves by the first birthday. Trachyonychia, sometimes called "20 nail dystrophy," involves longitudinal ridging and roughness of the nail plate. It can be idiopathic in childhood or a sign of psoriasis or lichen planus; approximately 50% will self-resolve.
Diagnosis: Psoriasis.

Leprosy, also known as Hansen's disease, is a long-term condition caused by Mycobacterium leprae or Mycobacterium lepromatosis. It leads to damage of the nerves, respiratory tract, skin, and eyes. This nerve damage can result in loss of pain sensation and loss of parts of extremities due to repeated injuries or infections from unnoticed wounds. An 18-year-old female from Kenya presents with a three-month history of weight loss, painful ulcers on the hands, feet, chest, and face, and numbness of the extremities. She is HIV negative. What is the diagnosis?

Severe subluxation of the first carpometacarpal (CMC) joint with squaring and rotation of the proximal phalanx on the palmar side. The thumb cannot extend, and she is unable to 'hitch hike'. She has rheumatoid arthritis scleroderma overlap, but the damage to the CMC joint is due to osteoarthritis.

Panniculitis is an inflammation of the subcutaneous fat associated with pancreatic diseases. It presents as tender, red-purple nodules that often drain. This condition can be triggered by any pancreatic disease, such as pancreatitis or tumors. Pancreatic enzymes travel through the bloodstream to the skin, where they break down adipocytes, causing inflammation and liquefaction of fat.

Idiopathic" accounts for 33% of cases, often indicating an unknown cause. A skin biopsy is commonly the first step in diagnosis. Conditions such as pemphigus foliaceus can present similarly. Moving to other diseases, psoriasis and pityriasis rubra pilaris are key causes of erythroderma. Psoriasis benefits from numerous systemic treatments, which can still reveal micaceous scale on salmon-colored plaques.

Secondary syphilis, if untreated, resolves in 3-12 weeks, potentially leading to tertiary syphilis months to years later. Tertiary syphilis can present with skin gummas, which are locally destructive but heal spontaneously, and other manifestations such as Argyll Robertson pupil, tabes dorsalis, and aortitis. Neurosyphilis can occur at any disease stage. Disease progression may be more severe and rapid in HIV-positive patients.

3-month-old infant with ear lesion. Hemangiomas, or infantile hemangiomas, are the most common benign tumor of infancy, often referred to as "strawberry marks" due to their clinical appearance. Endothelial cell proliferation results in hemangiomas. There are types: Congenital are visible at birth, whereas infantile appear later in infancy.

Shawl sign (back) and V sign (upper chest) are poikilodermatous findings typically in photodistributed patterns. Dermatomyositis is a clinical diagnosis where exam accuracy is crucial, though a biopsy can assist in diagnosis. Heliotrope rash, a poikilodermatous erythema, occurs around the eyes.

Orange discoloration at the end of the nail beds has been noted 16 weeks after COVID-19 infection, thought to be related to microvascular damage. Next, Mees’ lines—transverse white lines across nails—appear 3-6 weeks after symptoms, caused by altered growth of the nail plate. These lines should grow out with time and are not specific to COVID-30; they are also found in acute renal failure, congestive heart failure, systemic lupus erythematosus, ulcerative colitis, cancer, other infections, and chemotherapy.
In these patients, Forchheimer's sign is one of the characteristics of Rubella.

A 50-year-old patient diagnosed with Mucormycosis, a serious but rare fungal infection caused by a group of molds known as mucormycetes. These fungi are commonly found in soil and decaying organic matter.
Trichrome sign, leucotrichia, longer duration, and higher age at onset, along with mucosal vitiligo, are associated with a poor prognostic implication in vitiligo.
What's your diagnosis? Suspected acrodermatitis enteropathica due to zinc deficiency.
Dorsal surface involvement with affection of knuckles like this is uncommon, often seen in severe cases of OCD related to cleanliness.
The diagnosis is Dupuytren's contracture. Good job!
DD: Insect bite reaction / Cellulitis.

In challenging cases, we inject 5-fluorouracil into the scar, typically mixed with steroids, to downregulate fibroblasts and decrease collagen production. The mainstay of therapy is usually intralesional steroids. Patients often require repeated injections. A recommended at-home treatment is silicone scar sheets.
Patient presents with an itchy rash on the neck diagnosed as ringworm, an annular cutaneous fungal infection.
Cosmetic disfigurement due to scars and dyspigmentation is a main complication of discoid lupus erythematosus. Another complication is the risk of keratinocyte carcinoma.

Common wart (verruca vulgaris): a benign lesion caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection. It appears as a rough bump on the skin, flesh-colored or slightly darker, with an irregular surface that may have small black spots.

A meticulous examination can aid in diagnosis. Honey-colored crusts suggest a bacterial infection, such as impetigo. Smaller pustules or satellite lesions indicate candida. Irritants like saliva can also cause similar symptoms. Angular cheilitis is a diagnostic sign; however, its cause should be further investigated as it isn't always due to nutritional deficiency. Don't overlook actinic cheilitis. Addressing issues in skin folds is beneficial in management. A thorough examination is key to identifying the underlying cause.

27-year-old male diagnosed with Urbach-Wiethe disease. This autosomal recessive condition typically presents in infancy with a hoarse cry or voice due to infiltration of the laryngeal mucosa. It is caused by loss-of-function mutations in the extracellular matrix protein 1 (ECM1) gene.

28M unilateral progressive swelling of the right ear for 1 year, surface temperature slightly raised/warm, asymptomatic. Color Doppler suggests arteriovenous malformation (AVM). Erythema component may be missed in dark-skinned individuals.

Treatment in dermatology is incomplete without information, education, and communication/counseling of the patient. Skin is the organ system most affected by repeated external environmental factors. Conditions like polymorphous light eruption and contact dermatitis illustrate this impact.
This review examines primary and secondary erythromelalgia.

Patients with darker skin who have erythroderma will exhibit subtler symptoms, as their exam will appear less overtly erythematous. In diagnosing atopic dermatitis, a strong atopic history in the patient and their family is a useful factor. If atopic dermatitis is confirmed, there are many new medications available for treatment.

7-year-old male with tendinous and tuberous xanthomas. Total cholesterol is 730, likely indicating familial hypercholesterolemia type 2a. Correction of the underlying lipid disorder can lead to shrinkage or clearance of xanthomas.
The diagnosis is Darier's Disease.
Cranioplasty involves replacing a missing area of the skull with a specific material. This procedure is commonly performed using cranioplasty implants or meshes.
Muscle tendon rupture due to excessive heavy lifting.

Time from inoculation to chanche typically ranges from 10-90 days; 21 days is most common. A rare variant, Follman balanitis, can cause many smaller ulcerations. In tertiary syphilis, symptoms may include Argyll Robertson pupil, Tabes dorsalis, and aortitis. Note that neurosyphilis can occur at any stage of the disease. Disease progression may be more severe and rapid in HIV+ patients.

A 48-year-old was rushed to the ER by his relatives with worsening abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting for a week, and abnormal sensations in his extremities. He was gradually losing hair. Past medical history includes major depression following the loss of his wife.

A 12-year-old female was admitted to the dermatology department with multiple brown lesions on the trunk and face and a large cerebriform plaque on the right side of her scalp. She was born with multiple brown papules and nodules on the trunk and face and a brown patch on the right side of her scalp. She reported an increase in the size of the lesions on her body and face over time with no associated pain or itching. According to history, the lesion on the scalp was a small brown flat patch at birth, which increased in size and thickness over the last 8 years and became nodular and cerebriform in the past 5 years. The patient complained of intense itching and malodorous discharge from the lesion recently. Physical examination showed normal developmental and nutritional status, normal head circumference, and normal neurologic, cardiovascular, musculoskeletal, and ophthalmic evaluations. The mass on the scalp measured approximately 22 cm × 18 cm × 2.5 cm with a nodular surface. Diagnosis: Pseudocutis gyrata due to congenital cerebriform melanocytic nevus, characterized by skin thickening with folds and ridges resembling the brain surface, caused by infiltration of the connective tissue.
Just got this case yesterday.

This is a case of arsenic poisoning/toxicity. Patients can have arsenical keratoses on the palms and soles, diffuse hyperpigmentation, "raindrop hypopigmentation," and arsenic-induced skin cancers. You can also see Mees' lines on the nails. This serves as a wonderful example of skin revealing underlying pathology that dermatologists are poised to recognize.

This boy has a three-month history of a white lesion on his cheek with no history of trauma. The lesion may be a prickly pear lesion, most likely from glochids pricking the face, displaying a star pattern formed by one or more glochids from the same areole.
Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma is a histologically benign tumor with locally aggressive behavior, characterized by significant epistaxis, frequent local recurrence, and therapeutic complexity.

37-year-old female presents with a 13-year history of vegetating, friable, painful nodules, and fistulas that drained whitish grains on the right foot, with no history of local trauma and no improvement after.

A 66-year-old patient presented with nasal passage obstruction and cervical lymphadenopathy measuring 1 to 2 cm in diameter. Lab results showed normal ESR, CRP, and CBC. Electrophoresis indicated a monoclonal peak in gamma globulins, and immunofixation revealed monoclonal IgG with a kappa light chain in both blood and urine. Biopsy of the cavum and lymph nodes showed a deposit of acellular eosinophilic substance, staining brick red with Congo red. Diagnosis: Lymph node AL amyloidosis.
What are the signs in the images? Palmoplantar keratoderma (keratosis of the palms and soles) is seen in chronic arsenic poisoning too.
A 6-month-old child presented with complaints of diarrhea and mucocutaneous lesions, symptoms that began with the initiation of weaning.
Lesions present since birth, increasing in severity with age.

Marjolin's ulcer: A malignant transformation that can arise in previous burn scars, chronic wounds, chronic ulcers, or even sites of chronic osteomyelitis. It is characterized by an insidious evolution, with a latency period that can extend up to four decades from the initial traumatic event. Histologically, Marjolin's ulcer typically presents as squamous cell carcinoma, though other histological types like adenocarcinoma or basal cell carcinoma may also occur.

Ludwig's angina is a severe soft tissue infection of the neck and floor of the tongue, commonly leading to intensive care admissions due to airway inflammation and complications from septic shock. Dental infections are the most common cause of these abscesses."

Shawl sign (back) and V sign (upper chest) are patterns learned in medical school. These areas often display poikilodermatous changes. Dermatomyositis is a clinical diagnosis, emphasizing the importance of a thorough examination. While a biopsy can aid in diagnosis, it is not essential. A characteristic feature is the heliotrope rash, a poikilodermatous erythema that occurs around the eyes.

Nail melanoma can occur on any fingernail or toenail but is most commonly seen on the thumb, index finger, or big toe. Half of these patients report previous trauma—do not ignore that history! As the cancer grows, it can cause the nail to break down, leading to chipping or splitting. A recent study showed that nail technicians could be an important screening group to detect signs of melanoma in their clients.
Molluscum Contagiosum

A 62-year-old African American male with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus presented with a six-year history of worsening recurrent bumps and open sores on the scalp, back, and axillae. He reported multiple ER/urgent care visits.

Congenital dermal melanocytosis, also known as Mongolian spot, is the most common pigmented lesion in newborns. It typically appears as a blue-grayish macula with indefinite edges, and can also be blue-green or brown. The diameter of the lesion can be 10 cm or even more.

Thyroid hormone and atrial fibrillation connection is interesting, but a lingual thyroid appears very different. An 88-year-old woman with hypertension and hypothyroidism presents with a history of swelling on the right side of her tongue. Treatment: Her tongue looks like this.
Detailed presentation of the facial circulation.

Millium cysts in the nose usually affect the face and, less frequently, the limbs and trunk, and can appear congenitally or during childhood. Differential diagnoses include Gorlin's syndrome, punctate chondrodysplasia, and Rombo's syndrome.

A 48-year-old was rushed to the ER with complaints of worsening abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting for 7 days, and abnormal sensation in his extremities. He was noted to be gradually losing hair. Past medical history includes major depression after the loss of his spouse.

Cough and angioedema: The most common adverse effects in patients treated with an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor. Guidelines for chronic cough recommend an exhaustive interrogation of patients.
Hand of a sickle cell anemia patient, possibly deep vein thrombosis (DVT) of the arm.

Phytophotodermatitis often presents with bizarre geometric shapes on the skin. If triggered by substances like celery or parsley, the appearance of the rash may differ, typically without long dramatic lines. Recognizing these signs can help prevent unnecessary doctor visits.
Another big weekend of racing at Eagle Farm, headlined by the G1 Queensland Oaks.

Psoriasis and pityriasis rubra pilaris (PRP) are quintessential causes of erythroderma. Psoriasis has many systemic treatments available now that are helpful, and one can often still see the micaceous scale on salmon-colored plaques. Erythroderma requires a workup to find an underlying cause. Erythrodermic patients can suffer from severe pruritus and insensible losses of heat and fluids. The workup starts with a skin biopsy, but there are other tests that can help.

Extremely painful lesion that rapidly developed. Herpes Zoster (Shingles) is caused by the Varicella Zoster Virus, due to reactivation of the dormant virus in the dorsal root ganglia. Treatment consists of oral antivirals, NSAIDs, and antibiotics in case of secondary bacterial infection.
Patient presents with joint pain. The skin examination showed psoriasis with psoriatic arthritis, a long-term inflammatory arthritis occurring in those affected by the autoimmune disease psoriasis.

This case report describes a woman in her 20s with painful, red skin lesions present for 6 months that gradually progressed from the groin to other sites. Diagnosis: Acrodermatitis enteropathica. Manifestations include skin lesions, gastrointestinal symptoms, and zinc deficiency. The etiology relates to an autosomal recessive genetic disorder. Presents with symmetrical skin eruptions distributed in acral areas, evolving into vesicles, pustules, and erosions.
Erythema nodosum is a type of panniculitis, an inflammatory disorder affecting subcutaneous fat. It presents as tender red nodules on the anterior shins, and less commonly on the thighs and forearms.

Marjolin's ulcer is a cutaneous malignancy arising in previously injured skin, longstanding scars, or chronic wounds. Initially described as ulcerations with dense villi within a burn scar, these ulcers highlight the complex relationship between chronic skin injury and skin cancer.

70-year-old with a 5-week progressive, painful, pruritic rash unresponsive to topical and systemic steroids. Developed erythroderma; no triggering factor, infection, or drug cause identified. Past medical history includes prostate cancer in complete remission for 13 years.

Eczema herpeticum with ocular involvement features well-delimited monomorphic vesicular lesions with umbilication or erosions, predominantly found on the trunk, head, and neck; staphylococcal superinfection is present, showing large crusted lesions with follicular pustules.
Tapeworm infection types.

A 46-year-old with a 10-week history of wrist and hand stiffness and weight loss, accompanied by an itchy rash characterized by firm, erythematous, non-tender papules. Histology revealed a histiocytic infiltrate and large multinucleated histiocytes with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.
10F, duration: 3 days. Diagnose: Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease.
Ingrown Nail (Onychocryptosis).
A number of different treatments have been used to manage livedoid vasculopathy, a chronic ulcerative condition. We use pentoxifylline with good results.
Lip involvement in lupus erythematosus.

Jaccoud arthropathy is a deforming, non-erosive arthropathy characterized by ulnar deviation of the second to fifth fingers with metacarpophalangeal joint subluxation that is correctable with physical manipulation. It is associated with rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, psoriatic arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and malignancy. Swan neck changes of the fingers are observed.

Cosmetic dermatologists can perform filler injections in the lower lip area to augment the soft tissues, making the skin fold less noticeable. Lower lip cheilitis often results from UV exposure, suggesting the diagnosis of actinic cheilitis. This condition, essentially an actinic keratosis of the lower lip, requires specific treatment.

Sunset Sign: Paresis of the ascending gaze is characterized by the appearance of the eyes being pushed downward, with the sclera visible between the upper eyelid and the iris, and part of the lower pupil potentially covered by the lower eyelid.

Shawl sign (back) and V sign (upper chest) indicate photodistributed poikilodermatous patterns. Dermatomyositis (DM) is primarily a clinical diagnosis, emphasizing the importance of a precise exam. While a biopsy can aid in diagnosis, it is not mandatory. Heliotrope rash, characterized by poikilodermatous erythema, is typically observed around the eyes.

Hand Foot Mouth Disease is followed by Muehrcke’s lines, which are white transverse lines seen in chronic hypoalbuminemia due to conditions like nephrotic syndrome, liver disease, or malnutrition. Terry’s nails appear in patients with cirrhosis, chronic renal failure, diabetes, and congestive heart failure, characterized by leukonychia (white) of the entire nail except for a pink tip, with no lunula visible. These can also appear in normal aging.
This patient has reduced oral opening and scleroderama.
An analysis of over-the-counter tinted sunscreens with the objective of investigating factors that influence consumer choice depending on underlying skin tone.

If it's eroded or ulcerated, especially with a scalloped border, consider Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV). It can present atypically and is a crucial diagnosis not to miss. If HSV remains on the differential after ruling out other conditions, empirically treating with Valacyclovir while waiting for Direct Fluorescent Antibody (DFA) or Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) results from a swab is advisable due to its tolerability.
