At the age of 2 years, this girl developed lesions on her face, which persisted from then onwards. They were asymptomatic apart from occasional slight itching. She was otherwise well. Provide three differential diagnoses.

Pemphigus vulgaris is an autoimmune blistering disorder where the patient's own antibodies target a desmosomal protein, leading to the separation of keratinocytes. This can be described as a brick wall, where the mortar holding it together gets dissolved.

Purplish Skin Lesions on Leg: Small Vessel Vasculitis/Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis. This is an independent risk factor for Lymphoma associated with Primary Sjogren’s Syndrome. Recommended tests include Anti-Ro, Anti-La, C3 & C4, and Type II IgMk cryoglobulins. In Primary Sjogren’s Syndrome, vasculitis may present as Purpura or Neuropathy.
Observe the lesions, indicative of the disease. Note the glazed flexural lesions characteristic of flexural psoriasis.

Tests used to diagnose appendicitis include:  - Physical exam: A member of your health care team may apply gentle pressure on the painful area. - Blood test: This test checks for a high white blood cell count. - Urine test: You may have to take a urine test.

A 67-year-old patient with tender nodules on the posterior thigh and elbow has severe atopic eczema, previously controlled with mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg twice daily. This patient also has a history of type II diabetes and a renal transplant, currently managed with prednisolone and tacrolimus.
Hodgkin's Lymphoma: Cervical lymphadenopathy, fever. Lymph node biopsy shows Reed-Sternberg cells. Staging I of Hodgkin's Lymphoma.

Loeys-Dietz syndrome is characterized by vascular findings including cerebral, thoracic, and abdominal arterial aneurysms and/or dissections; skeletal manifestations; craniofacial features such as widely spaced eyes, strabismus, bifid uvula or cleft palate, and craniosynostosis; and dystrophic scars.

Not all nail thickening is fungus! It's important to confirm the diagnosis before treating. Conditions that can mimic fungal infections include psoriasis or just trauma to the nail. Antifungal pills can be most effective. Alternatively, prescription topicals or treatments such as tea tree oil and Vicks VapoRub applied around the nails have decent data.

28M, unilateral progressive swelling of right ear over 1 year. Surface temperature slightly raised/warm, asymptomatic. Color Doppler suggests arteriovenous malformation (AVM). Erythema component may be missed in dark-skinned individuals.

Pachyonychia congenita typically presents around 2-3 years old with thickened nails. This spectrum of genetic disorders is characterized by thick yellow-brown nails, thick palms and soles, and plantar pain. If patients do not develop plantar keratoderma by age 15, they most likely do not have this condition.

Tongue cancer is a malignant neoplasm that can occur in the tongue. It often develops due to exposure to risk factors such as smoking tobacco and drinking alcohol. Nicotine and chemicals in tobacco can damage tongue cells, and excessive alcohol consumption can increase cancer risk. Early detection is crucial for successful treatment. Symptoms may include sores, painful lumps, bleeding, or unusual changes in the color or texture of the tongue.

Cullen's sign may be useful in the diagnosis of intra-abdominal or retroperitoneal bleeding. Although not a specific sign, it appears days after the onset of bleeding and is not useful as an early indicator. Symptoms can include fever, nausea, vomiting, and loss of appetite.

Many patients seek to reduce signs of aging such as crow's feet, sagging nasolabial folds, and prominent platysmal bands. A combination of noninvasive and minimally invasive procedures can produce appearance-improving results.
Classic case for Sunday morning. Highlighting eccrine vs apocrine with this case.
I'm experiencing hair spewing sores on my calves and have received no care after raising concerns about a previous misdiagnosis by a dermatologist.
Digital ulications, "puffy fingers," and telangiectasia associated with systemic sclerosis.

Distal Lumen: Measure Central Venous Pressure, Fluid Therapy, Vasoactive Drugs, Intermittent Medication. In the Thicker Lumen: Rapid Infusion of Drugs, High Molecular Weight Drugs, Blood/Blood Derivatives, Total Parenteral Nutrition, Measure Total Venous Pressure.
Frank's sign. Coronary Artery Disease.

Skin ulceration, commonly seen from Xylazine use, has a multifactorial and unclear mechanism. The morphology of the ulceration is nonspecific. Biopsy is not helpful in diagnosing; the patient's history is more informative. These wounds often get superinfected and can serve as a portal for deeper infections. Good wound care and potential antibiosis if an infection is suspected, are crucial in managing patients with these skin findings. Superinfection is common; effective wound care is essential.
Effect of smoking for many years: Peripheral arterial disease leading to gangrene. Stop smoking. It is said that patients continue to smoke even with prosthetic legs.

Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (Bochdalek hernia): The frontal radiograph demonstrates cystic lucencies occupying the left hemithorax, appearing continuous with abdominal bowel gas, with displacement of the cardiac silhouette to the right.
Psoriasis above the neck or on the hands is harder on patients because they can’t cover it up.
Custard apple scalp report.
Systemic treatment alters the skin microbiota, with variable recovery across individuals and parallel changes in specific bacteria.

Phrynoderma: characteristic dermatosis associated with vitamin A deficiency, characterized by follicular hyperkeratosis in the extension areas of the limbs, abdomen, and back, often accompanied by xerosis and hyperpigmentation. The patient presents with impaired night vision and a rash.
Actinic Reticuloid: Chronic Actinic Dermatosis.
A child presents with a rash on the cheeks resembling a slap mark, indicative of Parvovirus infection.

Acutely, patients may experience an inflammatory phase that results in blisters. Subsequently, this may resolve but leave redness or hyperpigmentation at the site of the rash. Occasionally, the acute phase may not occur, and only hyperpigmentation is visible. A twist on the diagnosis is "Berloque dermatitis," which has become less common due to industry standards. Older perfumes with high bergapten levels can sensitize the skin to UV light, potentially triggering this condition if an old bottle of perfume is used.
Executive develops cutaneous leishmaniasis a few days after traveling to the Middle East. Giemsa stain of smear will show LD bodies.
A 40-year-old man presented to the dermatology clinic with a 1-year history of painless swelling of his earlobes. He also reported nasal congestion and intermittent nosebleeds.
A new mother brings her 2-day-old baby to the pediatric outpatient department with skin findings on his back. Cause: Mongolian spots. Usually a normal finding, rarely associated with Gangliosidosis.

Heliotrope rash appears subtly on more melanated skin and can affect the entire face. Dermatomyositis is a clinical diagnosis; proper examination is crucial. Although a biopsy can aid in diagnosis, it is not always necessary. Heliotrope rash, a poikilodermatous erythema, typically occurs around the eyes.
What to Do If Sutures Split: Understanding Practical Implications.
Bilateral scales at the base of the eyelids suggest a diagnosis of seborrheic blepharitis.

Syphilis and sarcoidosis can present in many different ways, which makes it important to always consider these diagnoses. Similarly, fungal infections and scabies can be deceptive, especially when a patient is extremely itchy. A dose of humility is crucial in dermatology and all of medicine.

Ixodes tick is the classic vector for Borrelia burgdorferi. The tick usually has to be attached to the patient for more than 36 hours to transmit and cause Lyme disease. Lyme and Syphilis, both spirochetal diseases, exhibit early to late disease stages.
Facial warts.
Scaphoid fracture fixed with hybrid screw and distal radioulnar joint instability fixed with K-wire.
32-year-old male, fever for 2 days, mucosa and scalp affected. Diagnosis: Varicella, characterized by a "dewdrops on a rose petal" appearance.

Onychomadesis, the shedding of the nail plate, has been reported in the months following COVID-19 infection, representing a severe version of Beau's lines. Next, Mees' lines, transverse white lines across the nails, appear 3-6 weeks after symptoms from altered growth of the nail plate. While not specific to COVID-19, they can also be associated with acute renal failure, congestive heart failure, systemic lupus erythematosus, ulcerative colitis, cancer, other infections, and chemotherapy. These conditions should improve with time.

Blue nails, or more formally azure lunula, are characterized by a blue discoloration of the lunulae, seen in argyria and cases of hepatolenticular degeneration (Wilson's disease). These are recurring Mees' lines in a symptomatic Wilson disease carrier, indicating systemic damage that has been undiagnosed and not treated.
Mastoid Abscess requires urgent drainage. A very thin bone separates it from the meninges; spread can be catastrophic.

Pemphigus vulgaris is defined by antibodies targeting Desmoglein 3 (+/- Dsg 1), which is preferentially expressed in the oropharynx, hence involvement of the mouth is expected. Additionally, rituximab significantly immunosuppresses, leading to concerns about hospitalization, even in fully vaccinated and boosted patients. Alternatives like mycophenolate and azathioprine are used to control the disease.
Strychnine poisoning causing trismus.

A 38-year-old female presented with scattered itchy folliculocentric papules on her left upper arm, occurring 11 days after her first and 2 days after her second COVID-19 vaccine (Pfizer-BioNTech). A diagnosis of eosinophilic pustular folliculitis (EPF), likely caused by the COVID-19 vaccine, was made.

40-year-old female felt intense pain and burning on her neck and back after feeling something touch her while snorkeling. The severe pain lasted for 30 minutes and persisted for 24 hours. Diagnosis: jellyfish stings.
36 M presenting with more hair loss on the left than right. Onset of hair loss on left scalp 1 year ago. Diagnosis: Lichen Planopilaris.
A 39-year-old male patient with a history of intellectual disability, hydrocephalus with ventriculoperitoneal shunt since age three, and epilepsy.
Aphthous stomatitis (canker sore).

Bezold's abscess is a rare deep neck abscess and an intratemporal complication of coalescent mastoiditis, wherein the infection erodes through the lateral mastoid cortex medial to the attachment of the sternocleidomastoid muscle. Once the infection reaches this space, it can progress into the infratemporal fossa and travel deep to the investing layer of deep cervical fascia. The spread of infection in this layer can lead to progressive cellulitis or abscess and may also proceed toward the carotid sheath, where thrombosis of the internal jugular vein can occur. Since the introduction of antibiotics, the number of reported cases of Bezold's abscesses has significantly decreased.
Hereditary disorder of keratinization.
The presence of multiple pilomatrixomas is uncommon. If detected early, underlying genetic mutations associated with conditions such as myotonic dystrophy should be considered.
Single ulcers, anal lesions, and mouth sores are all unique symptoms of the current monkeypox outbreak, underscoring the need to broaden case definitions for the disease.

Peutz-Jeghers syndrome and Laugier-Hunziker syndrome share similar cutaneous features, except that the latter is accompanied by melanocytic macules on the fingernails and toenails. Moreover, Laugier-Hunziker syndrome does not associate with polyposis. Laugier-Hunziker syndrome is a benign disorder often acquired in adulthood, characterized by lentiginous hyperpigmentation of the oral mucosa and lips, associated with longitudinal melanonychia and palmoplantar hyperpigmented lesions.

Pityriasis lichenoides chronica (PLC) diagnosis confirmed. Lesions manifest as erythematous scaly papules and heal with hypopigmentation, typical of PLC. Treatment includes phototherapy, topical steroids, doxycycline, and methotrexate. Symptoms: persistent hypopigmentation, recurrent scaly lesions for 1.5 years, mild itchiness, primarily affecting the trunk and proximal parts of extremities. No significant changes on the face, mucosa, or nails.

Bacterial infection (pic 1), benign mole (pic 2), part of someone’s background skin color (pic 3). Amelanotic melanoma (pink, not brown) can challenge even experienced dermatologists. Changes in just one nail should prompt consideration of a biopsy.
The correct diagnosis is: Terry's Nails.

Scarring is a normal part of healing, involving extra collagen to repair skin injuries. Sometimes, this process becomes excessive, resulting in hypertrophic scars or keloids. In certain cases, treatment involves getting steroids directly into the scar. Special instruments like the Dermajet, which uses air to inject the medication without a needle, can be effective.
Case of very painful ulcerative Discoid Lupus Erythematosus. Pigmented fungiform papillae.
Infant Hemangiomas: Non-carcinogenic vascular tumors composed of endothelial cells. They are the most common tumors in childhood, occurring in approximately 1 in 20 babies.
Patient presents with a skin eruption. Possible causes include undercooked shiitake mushrooms, bleomycin, or dermatomyositis.

Persistent into adulthood: new second finger swelling. Noticeable damage-related changes include: 1. Z-shaped thumb 2. Fusiform soft tissue swelling on the lateral and medial aspects of the second proximal interphalangeal joint, associated with synovitis.
Differential Diagnosis: 1. Madura foot 2. Botryomycosis 3. Nocardia infection.
A 6-year-old female patient presents a condition that appears in winter and disappears in summer. No family members are affected. Diagnosis: Ichthyosis vulgaris.
What are the newer treatments for multiple keloids? Example: cryotherapy.
Kills bacteria and prevents mold from returning.
Constrictive posthitis. Effacement of the frenulum. Perianal skin pallor and sclerosis. Meatal stenosis. Zoonoid inflammation affecting glans penis and prepuce.

The patient is a young male with a long duration of symptoms, which exacerbate during winter and dry weather. The symptoms include severe dandruff without itchiness or pain. The discussion suggests a preliminary diagnosis of Ichthyosis, a group of inherited keratinization disorders characterized by dry skin and fish-scale-like flaking. Symptoms often worsen in cold, dry seasons and improve in warm, moist conditions. Diagnosis can be supported by family history, skin lesion appearance, and histopathological changes, including various forms of ichthyosis presenting different skin patterns. Treatment primarily involves symptomatic care, including the use of moisturizers, oral vitamin A, or retinoic acid drugs, and topical corticosteroid or retinoic acid ointments. It is recommended to avoid hot water baths and scratching the affected area.

The poster, whose specific demographics are not mentioned, reports having a red, swollen patch on their arm and is questioning whether this could be insect bite dermatitis. Based on the discussion among forum users, there was speculation that the condition could indeed be insect bite dermatitis. However, further symptoms or discussions on treatment were not specified in the posts.

The forum post is about mucous cysts, with a focus on the symptoms and treatment approaches discussed by various users. The primary medical concern indicated in the post is mucous cysts located near the joints, especially around the thumb. Multiple surgical and minimally invasive approaches, including hormone injections, have been utilized for treatment. The discussion in the forum thread highlights mucous cysts and various treatments that have been applied by users or supported through medical advice. Some users advocate for surgical removal of the cyst, considering the risk of scar contracture affecting joint mobility. Minimally invasive treatments like small needle knife therapy and hormone local injections are also favored. Other users shared experiences with treatments like cryotherapy for children, which initially showed effectiveness but required surgery due to recurrence and the onset of a new cyst in the ipsilateral arm.

The poster is inquiring about which medical specialty handles hemangiomas and the best hospital for treatment, specifically mentioning a sponge-like hemangioma. Other users suggested that hemangiomas fall under the purview of dermatology and laser cryotherapy. There's a mention of an approach to observe first as it might regress as it grows. Shanghai Children's Hospital on Gudai Road was recommended for treatment of hemangiomas.

The poster reports a condition termed '甲带状色素沉着', seeking advice on treatment. The discussion suggests that the condition, diagnosed as '甲母痣' (nail nevus), does not require treatment but should be closely monitored.

Female, 3 years old, presents with scattered papular vesicles on her back, and reports itchiness. Discussion between the original poster and other forum members includes agreement on the initial diagnosis of papular urticaria. Questions were raised about the duration of the condition and whether chickenpox has been ruled out.

Male, 26 years old, overweight. Has had skin lesions for over ten years. He has consulted top-tier hospitals in Beijing and Changchun, who recommended cosmetic surgery plus dermoplasty and radiation therapy, patching, and localized hormone treatment (patient disagrees due to being overweight). Currently experiences occasional infections with pus discharge and sometimes can squeeze out a whitish cheese-like substance. Discussed treatments in the forum included skin soft tissue expander implantation followed by superficial radiotherapy, radiation combined with local steroid injections, the use of Quinocort for keloid-like scars which has shown good results in acne keloidalis nuchae patients, and surgical removal combined with traditional Chinese medicine. Quinocort's formulation and effectiveness were queried for more details.

The poster reports having a patch of red rash between the 5th and 6th ribs on the right chest, extending to the midline and back of the armpit. The poster is seeking information about the type of skin disease this could be and its respective treatment. Several users suggested that the symptoms might be indicative of shingles (herpes zoster), noting that it is typically painful and itchy. However, the poster specifies that the rash is neither painful nor itchy. Differential diagnoses discussed include allergic dermatitis, as it was noted the rash does not feature the typical fine vesicles associated with shingles. One user suggested using acyclovir for a week if it were shingles, while others noted the rash location is typical for shingles but could potentially develop typical symptoms such as pain later on. There is uncertainty due to the unclear image of the rash and the mention of possible stretch marks.

A middle-aged female experiences morning eye-area rashes, which are swollen and painful but not itchy. She is uncertain whether the cause is viral or due to an insect bite. Some users suggested that the condition might be Paederus dermatitis. The proposed treatments include using Lisheng Stone wash and Baidobang ointment. Additionally, one user suggested that it could be an allergy and recommended using anti-allergy medication.

The poster's chief complaint or topic of discussion is about '指节垫', which could refer to either 'joint pads' or 'chewing pads'. There is no specific demographic or detailed symptom information provided by the poster. Through the discussion in the forum, it's identified that there is no clear distinction in pathological changes between joint pads and chewing pads. It is remarked that there is no definitive treatment method for this issue and further guidance is sought by some users. However, no specific symptoms or detailed treatment plans were discussed.

The user is discussing recurrent disseminated infundibulofolliculitis. Specific symptoms, duration or other personal demographics were not detailed in the post. Discussions by forum users suggested differential diagnoses including perifollicular keratinization and flat warts. The quality of pictures posted was mentioned to be too blurry to confirm any diagnosis clearly. There was no consensus or discussion of treatments in the available text.

The user in the Chinese forum is asking for an explanation and analysis of a prescription given by a local renowned rural doctor for skin diseases, from the year 2005. The prescription seems to be confusing and improperly formatted, containing multiple medications including Clobetasol 40mg, Kotimol 2ml * 2 for immediate intramuscular injection, several not clearly identified medications, and topical applications like coal tar shampoo, sulfur ointment, and mixed oil ointments. The discussion in the replies highlights the effectiveness of the prescription but criticizes it for being chaotic and involving the misuse of several drugs with similar properties. Some of the specific medications mentioned in the forum include clotrimazole, antacids, diuretics, and multiple complex topicals. Recommendations from other forum members suggest alternating some of the topicals like coal tar and sulfur ointments and modifying the prescription by replacing some ingredients with alternatives such as black bean distilled oil ointment.

The user complains about a skin condition affecting their hands, seeking identification and advice on the issue. Discussion among forum participants includes suggestions of potential diagnoses such as secondary syphilis (suggested to check TP), common warts, corns, and erythema multiforme. The user has been asked for additional symptoms to aid in further diagnosis.

A patient reported contact dermatitis after their pants got wet with the herbicide glyphosate from weeds while spraying the day before. They did not pay much attention initially, but by the next morning, they experienced changes in their legs. The primary symptom now is localized skin pain with no other manifestations. The discussion suggests that this is a case of contact dermatitis due to exposure to a chemical. Recommended treatments included topical use of burn ointment and internal use of anti-allergy and anti-inflammatory medications. Other advice included cleaning with levonorgestrel wet compresses, applying fusidic acid cream topically, and taking antibiotics and antihistamines internally. There is support for these treatment suggestions among the forum users.

An elderly woman reports having lesions on the right side of her face. Based on user discussions, the condition was identified as herpes zoster, characterized by blistery lesions. It was considered a viral infection. Some users briefly considered other possibilities such as hand-foot-and-mouth disease and erysipelas due to the manifestation as an infection, but the consensus was on herpes zoster.

A male poster reported noticing two red circles appearing subcutaneously on the back of his right hand after being with his wife around noon. These circles were neither painful nor itchy and faded away about an hour later without any associated rash. The poster stated that he had never experienced this phenomenon before. Multiple forum users responded suggesting the possibility that the red circles could be cholinergic urticaria, a condition typically benign and related to nerve excitement. The suggestions were consistent, with multiple affirmations of the diagnosis of cholinergic urticaria. One user raised the possibility of an allergic reaction to a product used during the encounter with his wife, such as a condom. No specific treatment was recommended other than general reassurances about the benign nature of the condition.

The poster, presumably seeking medical advice, reports a history of a skin injury from two years ago which initially healed after debridement but reopened a month later and remained unhealed. Subsequent skin grafting was initially successful but the skin flap necrosed after a month. The necrosis process started with the area turning black, followed by decay and discharge of foul black blood. Current treatment includes microwave therapy, oral medications for blood circulation and anti-inflammation, however, with unsatisfactory results. New granulation tissue forms but necrosis persists around the area. Discussion in the forum suggests the possibility of necrotizing fasciitis, resembling the symptoms of streptococcal gangrene, characterized by rapid spread of inflammation, fever, and necrosis within 4-5 days of onset. Diagnosis in a hospital suggested osteomyelitis with no abnormalities in MRA. Treatments being discussed include using a combination of antibiotics (Azithromycin, Ofloxacin, Ornidazole, Vitamin E, B1, fluconazole previously) with modest outcomes. A current recommendation includes using third-generation cephalosporins. There's also a mention of potentially using integrative medicine with Chinese herbal remedies to enhance overall health and expedite wound healing. No signs of squamous cell carcinoma were evident from the wound morphology.

Female, 20 years old, presents with patchy skin rashes accompanied by desquamation for two months. The rash is scattered across the back and arms, and is itchy. Discussion in the forum suggests a query if the symptoms might indicate pityriasis versicolor. No specific treatments or additional diagnoses discussed.

A 20-year-old female reports having firm, flesh-colored papules on her trunk for 2 months. These papules cause no discomfort. The discussion on the forum involves various differential diagnoses including flat warts, soft warts, xanthoma, tissue hyperplasia, steatocystoma, and post-vesicular scars. Atrophic spots were also mentioned as a potential issue. However, no consensus was reached regarding the correct diagnosis.

The poster requests help identifying a medical issue shown in pictures. The patient has patches distributed over the scalp, neck, and left waist side. These areas have central pigmentation with slightly red periphery and no crusting or scaling. They report sensations of itching and pain. Topical antibacterial and anti-allergy treatments have been ineffective. The patient has no history of insect bites. Multiple forum users agree that the patient's symptoms are consistent with Fixed Drug Eruption (固定性红斑型药疹). They suggest identifying and avoiding the allergenic medication as the most probable cause and solution.

The user reported a skin lesion that has been present for two years and has started bleeding in recent months. The discussion among forum users suggests a consensus that the condition may be a pyogenic granuloma. Other users considered the possibility of hemangioma, especially noting that the bleeding might have been exacerbated by the patient's actions. A suggestion was made to confirm the diagnosis through a skin biopsy.

The poster, a synthetic silk worker, reports experiencing severe itchiness on the back, which intensifies after sleeping in heat. Initially, the affected area was small but has since grown larger. The poster does not consume alcohol and is not on any special medication. Users in the forum suggest the condition might be neurodermatitis. They recommend oral treatment with loratadine, one tablet per day, and topical treatment with halometasone cream, applied twice daily.

Female, 17 years old, experiences excessive sweating on the palms along with vesicles, redness, and associated itching. Condition was identified during an examination as dyshidrosis. The condition is induced by excessive sweating. It is a special type of eczema known as dyshidrotic eczema and should be treated as such. Some users question the diagnosis of dyshidrosis, suggesting treatment as for eczema instead.

The poster reports experiencing symptoms for over a month, primarily characterized by itching and rough skin texture. Users in the forum discussion suggest that the symptoms described by the poster could indicate seborrheic dermatitis. Recommendations for treatment include frequent changing of undergarments, choosing cotton materials, and bathing once or twice a week during winter.

Patient; female, 56, reports no other illnesses, normal blood pressure and blood sugar. She has a skin lesion for several months which is painful upon palpation but not itchy. Further inquiries by users asked about potential triggers, the nature of the lesion (whether it is a papule), the patient's own feelings about the condition, any history of insect bites, and if the lesion is isolated or more widespread. No specific diseases or treatment options were discussed based on the lesion appearances.
The main topic of the post is atopic dermatitis and specifically related to the phenomenon called 'white dermatographism' or 'white scratch sign'.

Male, 34 years old, has had a skin lesion on the left forehead since childhood which has grown as his body grew. The lesion is described as a clearly demarcated, waxy, yellow-colored, wart-like patch with a hard consistency and no hair on the surface. The lesion sometimes presents as a wart-like protrusion after full development. Treatments discussed include laser surgery with two rounds of pixel laser treatment along with the application of a scar patch. Approximately 6 treatments could be required, costing around 7000 RMB in total. Cryotherapy was also mentioned as a treatment option.

Male, 35 years old, reports itching around the right eye with rough skin. Has used antifungal cream (Bifonazole) without effect and steroid cream (Fluocinonide) with temporary relief followed by rapid recurrence. Now has a similar rash under the right earlobe, with normal skin on the left side of the face and no similar lesions on other body parts. Suspects potential pityriasis versicolor but unsure about diagnosis. Based on discussions in the post, other users suggested possibilities like neurodermatitis or eyelid dermatitis. There were no specific details on further findings or a conclusive diagnosis. Treatment discussions emphasized steroid cream as initially effective but with issues of relapse.

The main topic discussed in the post revolves around the 2018 consensus on the diagnosis and treatment of vitiligo, covering various aspects such as strengthening post-color restoration prevention with tacrolimus ointment, changes in severity scoring, separation of facial and limb tip type vitiligo, usage of systemic steroids during the progressive phase, phototherapy strategy, and an expansion of calcineurin inhibitor use to infants. It mentions that standard treatments should not be replaced by alternative or not evidence-based methods such as blue oxygen, self-blood, and targeted gene therapy, which have been popular but possibly misrepresented in some commercial promotions. Follow-up comments reflect a general approval of the shared guidelines and clarify differences in treatment strategies for vitiligo during the stable and progressive stages, recommending tailored approaches for each. The discussion elaborates on the advisable use of phototherapy and its limitations regarding the maximum dosage to avoid the risks exceeding benefits. New discussions point out the relevance of integrating multiple restoration methods, especially emphasizing the benefits of autologous epidermal graft transplantation during the stable phase of vitiligo. Lack of details in the consensus and the absence of addressing potential hepatorenal damage due to medication are noted deficiencies.

A third-year medical student reports that a classmate has developed a boil on their leg. The student is seeking recommendations for appropriate medications to treat this condition. Suggestions from forum members included using topical Baidubang for its antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, applying iodine for a few days to reduce inflammation, using fish oil ointment and oral antibiotics for treatment, and applying erythromycin ointment. Additionally, methods like topical ozone treatment with infrared light were mentioned. A development of possible contact dermatitis around the boil was noted, with recommendations for treating it with Mupirocin ointment and taking Clean Heat Disperse Capsules orally.

The poster reports scattered granular growths around the anal area, alternating diarrhea and constipation, chronic perianal ulcers, and itching. The issue has worsened recently with new growths appearing after the initial ones were not taken seriously and burst. There is mention of frequent washing and the intermittent use of zinc ointment due to the itching. Other forum users suggested the possibility of perianal eczema and external hemorrhoids, with one comment questioning these suggestions as the patient reported that the conditions were not itchy. Another user raised the possibility of condyloma.

The poster reported a case of a one-month-old baby who developed a skin issue when the baby was half a month old. The symptoms include a rash distributed on both cheeks. The baby was diagnosed with allergic eczema at a hospital and was prescribed hydrocortisone butyrate and Phellodendron wash for external use. After two days of treatment, the condition seemed to worsen. The symptoms described by the poster were further discussed in the forum. Suggestions from other users included adjusting the diet when breastfeeding, avoiding spicy foods, beef, mutton, alcohol, and seafood, and considering herbal remedies like chrysanthemum and honeysuckle dew. Another user mentioned conditions like millet rash and newborn acne, suggesting that newborn acne does not require treatment.

The user reported experiencing symptoms of itching and redness, requesting help to identify the disease and suitable treatment. Based on the discussion, the participants suggested the condition could be acute urticaria. They recommended treatments such as cetirizine, loratadine, intravenous calcium gluconate, and detailed that a full medical history review, allergy identification, and current medications must be considered. The use of steroids was also mentioned but advised to be administered under proper guidelines.

A 26-year-old female is experiencing acute urticaria for 3 days. Despite using antihistamines, she is suffering from recurrent episodes. Allergen testing (sensitivity screening) was performed, and the results indicated allergies to house dust mites, dust mites, and cockroaches. The patient had a complete blood count (CBC) performed on the day of symptom onset, which was normal. There were questions raised regarding the equipment used for the allergen testing, specifically asking whether 'Baikang' or 'Mola' devices were used, as well as the accuracy of allergen testing during an acute episode. The discussion included suggestions to recheck the complete blood count.

The user has been experiencing a medical condition for 10 years, for which they are seeking identification and treatment suggestions. Based on the forum discussion, it is suggested that the user might be suffering from chronic eczema, which could have led to lichenification due to excessive scratching. A suggested treatment includes bandage therapy, though this is only offered as a suggestion and not a definitive solution.
The poster reports having had a hemangioma on their hand characterized by red patches for many years without any special sensations.

Two young children under the age of two from the same department developed widespread rashes, predominantly on the distal parts of the limbs, merging into patches. Initially presented as small papules, which gradually evolved into clear, full vesicles. The rash is non-itchy. One child has had the rash for three days with a bright red color, while in the other child, the rash has been present for nearly six days with many lesions having disappeared. The discussion reveals a tentative diagnosis for both cases as Kaposi's varicelliform eruption. The progression of the rash starts with papules turning into large vesicles. For the second child, the rash is mostly in the dissipating stage, with very few rashes visible during the photographing. No other symptoms were reported besides the rashes. Other discussed possibilities included hand-foot-and-mouth disease and linear IgA bullous dermatosis.

The poster is querying whether their symptoms are indicative of Rosacea or Seborrheic Dermatitis. The mentioned symptoms include itching, erythema, and dilated follicles predominantly around the nose, which could be consistent with rosacea. Additionally, consideration of Seborrheic Dermatitis is suggested due to the presence of red base covered by sticky scales, which spread beyond the nose to other parts of the face. From the discussion, it was highlighted that the predominant enlargement of the follicular pores and a clear demarcation of affected areas suggest Rosacea more strongly than Seborrheic Dermatitis. There is a mention of a mild presentation, which was suggested might not require treatment. Treatment suggestions include the use of moxibustion.

The user is concerned about a skin condition on their hands, suggesting potential diagnoses such as erythroderma or progressive palmoplantar keratoderma. There is a discussion regarding the exact diagnosis with possibilities including erythroderma or progressive palmoplantar keratoderma. The conversation suggests the need for historical medical data to confirm the diagnosis.

The poster, suffering for about two years, describes experiences of developing itchy and severe reddish rash over the whole body when performing activities that raise body temperature, such as walking or eating. The rash usually subsides naturally within an hour if left untreated. The poster suspects that this condition is not just a regular allergy and has attached a photo taken during a flare-up. The major diagnosis discussed among the forum users is cholinergic urticaria. Suggestions for managing this condition include avoiding potential dietary triggers such as seafood and certain food additives, maintaining personal and environmental hygiene, avoiding irritants like scented soaps and alcohol, and ceasing smoking. Treatment suggestions include using antihistamines regularly, with occasional use of atropine, propranolol, and oxtriphylline to achieve satisfactory results. Some users also recommend using traditional Chinese medicines, like ginseng and goji berries, as preventive measures.

A 30-year-old individual reported a recurring redness of the glans penis after sexual intercourse, persisting for four months. Initial symptoms started three months ago and included pain during intercourse, followed by the appearance of redness few days after intercourse. The condition has been treated with multiple medications including Baidubang, Parison, Eloson, Funo, Lushan Stone, and Tacrolimus without cure. Past medical history includes similar episodes of redness dating back to 2008, with various levels of temporary relief following the use of different medications. Discussion in the forum involved differential diagnoses and treatment suggestions. Users debated the patient's condition ranging from performing a biopsy, considering psoriasis, and suggestions of using Dan Shen tablets to improve blood circulation. Topical treatments like Dakin's solution and Kuh-sen washing liquid were also mentioned. A skin CT was performed, showing normal epidermal thickness, basal layer liquefaction degeneration, and infiltration of mononuclear inflammatory and melanin-laden cells in the dermis. Suggestions included avoiding masturbation and seeing a dermatologist for conditions like psoriasis which could not be confirmed even after evaluations in tertiary care hospitals.

A five-year-old child has had an unusual birthmark since birth. The birthmark described is irregular, uneven, and located at the neck where friction occurs. Users suggested a preventive excision would be wise, recommending a visit to dermatology or cosmetic surgery departments for removal.

The poster reported being bitten by an unknown bug during sleep 7 days ago. Initially, only itchiness was felt, which led to scratching. Post-scratch, reddening occurred on the right arm's elbow area. Two days following the bite, the symptoms evolved to surrounding skin redness and swelling with a central black area and a few blisters. Discussion in the forum led to a consensus that the symptoms are indicative of Paederus dermatitis (also known as blister beetle dermatitis). Users also noted that the affected area resembles a burn and now produces a stinging sensation. Treatment suggestions included avoiding scratching, primary disinfection to prevent infection, and local application of steroid creams or lotions such as halometasone (sold under the brand name Halobetasol) or dermatop ointments. Additionally, oral antihistamines were suggested to aid in symptom management. Some users indicated that the condition could heal gradually without specific treatment, although it might take around four to five weeks based on a similar personal experience. There was a suggestion to use Talquistina (calamine lotion) for symptomatic relief and suggestions of potential complications like secondary infection.

The poster reported experiencing peeling skin on their foot for one month, and noted no enlargement of the lower limb lymph nodes. The discussion in the forum suggested that the use of a deodorant insole might be causing the skin peeling. It was mentioned that the insole contains salicylic acid and benzoic acid, which may lead to peeling due to their effects on the skin. Furthermore, a user suggested that the poster might be a physical laborer experiencing higher foot perspiration, especially at the toes which have thinner skin. Another user pointed out the possibility of an inadequate quality of the deodorant insole with excessive salicylic acid content. Formaldehyde solution was also mentioned as a common ingredient in such insoles, potentially contributing to skin corrosion.

34-year-old male patient noted growths in the perineal area and lower genitals three months ago, described as multiple grayish growths about the size of mung beans in the perineal area and smaller grain-sized growths on the lower side of the genitals. The patient has a history of unprotected sexual exposure. Initially, a negative result for HPV was reported, and a biopsy confirmed the presence of Condyloma Acuminatum (CA). Diagnostic contradictions were highlighted in the discussion, specifically regarding the HPV test which failed to confirm the virus, implying potential issues with the previous diagnosis and need for further review, including additional tests and biopsy. It was also noted that the clinical course of Condyloma Acuminatum can be divided into overt and covert phases, the latter of which requires auxiliary tests like the 5% acetic acid white test and typification of the HPV virus.

A 35-year-old female has been experiencing repeated itching at the back of her head and dandruff-like flaking for 2 years. Users in the forum suggested that the symptoms might indicate Asbestos Scab or Tinea Capitis given the description of hair breakage and the corresponding look of black dots on the scalp. The user also mentioned using an ointment for half a month which somewhat improved the condition, but the erythema persisted.

The poster reports skin lesions around both eye sockets and requests a diagnosis. The consensus among the forum members is a diagnosis of Milia (粟丘疹). It is suggested by multiple users that treatment typically involves either direct microwave therapy or using a sterilized needle to puncture the lesions. Discussions also highlighted concerns about scarring post-treatment.

The user has posted a query regarding certain symptoms, asking for a diagnosis and inquiring about the specific symptoms and their onset. Multiple users discussed potential diagnoses including tinea versicolor, erythema annulare centrifugum, granuloma annulare, and pityriasis rosea. Antifungal treatment was suggested for conditions like tinea versicolor. Further history and symptom clarification were recommended for more accurate diagnosis.

The poster, a 9-year-old girl, has been experiencing itching multiple places on her body for one day. Physical examination found multiple rashes on the body, with the ones on high areas being rough to the touch. A specific rash on the back of her left thigh, as large as the child's palm, felt relatively hard and was warmer than other areas. It was considered that the itching could have led to a secondary skin infection. Various users discussed the potential diagnoses including insect bite dermatitis and allergic reactions. There was a debate on whether antibiotics are necessary, with some suggesting that antibiotics should be used if there's a sign of infection, while others recommended against antibiotics unless there is clear evidence of infection. Several mentioned using antihistamines for allergy treatment. A few suggested further examination and lab tests such as a complete blood count to investigate the possibility of a high white cell count indicative of infection. Topical treatments like Baidubang ointment were also recommended.

40-year-old male experiencing chest rashes measuring 2-3mm for the past three months without pain or itchiness. Despite multiple treatments including oral and topical medications and infusions, no improvement was observed. The specific medications used are unknown. The discussion among forum users primarily supports the diagnosis of Malassezia folliculitis. Treatment suggestions included the use of sulfur ointment, oral and topical antifungal medications, and UVB phototherapy as potentially effective.

The poster reports experiencing recurring foot rashes without any subjective symptoms. The rashes have gradually increased and are characterized by collar-like scaling. Other forum users suggest that the rash appears similar to tinea pedis based on the photos shared. There is a question about whether similar symptoms appear on the torso, but no information was provided about this.

The poster is working in a plastic factory and is frequently exposed to chemicals. They are concerned about the condition of their fingernails, specifically mentioning 'spoon nails' (koilonychia). Discussion in the forum suggests the presence of spoon nails which might be associated with frequent chemical exposure. One user requested further explanation on the relationship between koilonychia and chemical exposure.

The poster reports concerns about certain physical findings on the right side of his frenulum. He describes three pinpoint-sized grains, which slightly protrude and are visible when the foreskin is retracted. Initially, these granules were itchy and caused a sharp pain when touched. Upon examining the foresword during a shower, the grains appeared to align into a single line.

Female, 20 years old, has been experiencing facial papules for three years, which are neither painful nor itchy. Occasionally, small pustules appear. The symptoms reportedly worsen before the onset of menstruation. The user has discussed the condition with other forum members. Based on user feedback and image observation, several potential diagnoses and recommendations were mentioned including: Seborrheic dermatitis, potential rosacea, and mite infection. It is suggested that the user avoids spicy and greasy food, maintains facial cleanliness, and possibly checks for mite infection or hormonal levels (estrogen and testosterone).

The poster reports experiencing repeated occurrences of hand tinea with 2-3 episodes of recurrence per year. The condition is described as not very itchy, and sometimes one or two blisters also appear on other fingers. One respondent suggested performing a pathogen culture to confirm a fungal infection and mentioned that proper treatment based on confirmed diagnosis can be very effective and potentially lead to a complete cure.

A relative experienced a reddish patch on their face without any apparent cause about a week ago. This patch gradually increased in size and was accompanied by pain and itching. The patient has a medical history of neurodermatitis and urticaria. A dermatologist diagnosed the condition as folliculitis. Based on the discussion, users suggested that the symptoms might resemble those of insect bite dermatitis.

The poster reports having soft subcutaneous nodules that are painful when pressed. The nodules have been present for a long time and have recently started to grow. Users in the forum speculate various potential diagnoses including sharp wet wart (condyloma acuminatum), enlarged lymph nodes especially if located in the inguinal region, subcutaneous cysts, viral infections suggesting removal, inflamed subcutaneous sebaceous gland cyst causing pain, and possibilities of lipoma. They recommend seeking timely medical examination and treatment to prevent possible worsening of the condition.

Female, 7 years old, experiencing recurrent facial erythema without clear cause. The condition has been treated as allergic dermatitis with medications including Ketotifen (开瑞坦) and Vitamin C. While the erythema significantly subsides the day after treatment, it reappears once the medication is stopped. Other users in the forum suggested that the symptoms might resemble infectious erythema. There are inquiries about treatment methods for infectious erythema.

The post discusses issues related to the left eye and left side of the face, and specifically mentions a condition known as 'Ota nevus' or commonly called 'nevus of Ota'. It is suggested that this condition is present at birth or soon after, potentially growing or darkening around ages 4-5 and 12-14. It is also mentioned that the nevus can differ in color intensity with seasons or emotional changes. The forum discussion reveals various treatment options historically used for Ota nevus, including cryotherapy, traditional laser therapy, and surgical approaches such as dermabrasion or skin grafting, which often resulted in scars. Modern treatment has advanced to using Q-switched laser, which offers a significant improvement by effectively treating the nevus without leaving scars.

The poster describes having a rash on the abdomen that has persisted for months without any associated pain or itching. The discussion identifies the rash as typical Lichen Nitidus. Characteristics include large as a pinhead, round, flat-topped small papules with a smooth, wax-like sheen. It is mentioned that Lichen Nitidus tends to resolve on its own and generally does not require treatment. However, if treatment is considered, options discussed include taking vitamins A and D, applying topical corticosteroid creams, and using cryotherapy for isolated or persistent lesions.

The poster describes a case of a leg ulcer (referred to as '臁疮腿') that is difficult to heal after an injury. The poster is seeking advice on treatment options, particularly topical medications. Multiple users have suggested various treatment methods for difficult-to-heal ulcers. These include using a mixture of glucose and insulin solution for wound cleaning, internal and external use of traditional Chinese medicine to promote blood circulation and healing, checking for underlying conditions like diabetes, vasculitis, varicose veins, or venous thrombosis, and conservative treatments like meticulous wound cleaning, dressing changes, or surgery like skin grafting if conservative treatments fail. Some users highlighted the use of homemade ointments, hot needle therapy, and commercial products like Sheng Ji San and Meibao, while others recommended laser assistance or antibiotic sensitivity tests to treat the ulcer effectively.

A 51-year-old robust male with no diabetes and no harmful habits except smoking, presents with a 2-year history of a red, well-defined rash covering extensive areas such as the back, buttocks, and thighs. The rash is level with the skin, smooth surfaced with sporadic white scales resembling those seen in psoriasis but minimal. Initially started on the scalp and later spread to the back and arms, especially after alcohol consumption. Previous treatments including steroids (triamcinolone acetonide) led to rapid improvement that relapsed within a month, causing more severe outbreaks. The patient is currently disheartened and seeking help through an acquaintance after multiple ineffective hospital treatments. After multiple discussions in the forum, it was clarified and repeatedly indicated by multiple participants that the patient's condition is likely psoriasis and not 'red skin disease' as initially suspected. Various treatments were attempted, including a mix of infusions, oral antifungal medication, and topical steroid and antibiotic treatments with no apparent effect. Suggestions shifted towards Chinese herbal medicine which showed a slight reduction in redness. The general consensus among the forum participants suggested a reassessment of the medical approach since the initial treatments were ineffective and proposed common treatments for psoriasis such as topical steroids, vitamin A and other vitamins. However, there remains no follow-up confirmation on the effectiveness of these new recommendations.

The poster reported having unexplained red patches on their back, which are not painful and have a barely noticeable itchiness. Approximately one week before noticing the back red patches, similar skin conditions appeared on the elbows, also without pain or significant itchiness. The skin changes were also observed on the inside of the thighs near the knees. Discussion among forum users suggested several potential diagnoses including insect bite dermatitis and contact dermatitis. One user mentioned the possibility of fixed drug eruption considering the clear boundaries of the large rash and recommended investigating the poster's medical and drug history to prevent recurrence. Treatment suggestions included oral chlorphenamine three times a day and topical application of an anti-inflammatory cream twice a day, which reportedly led to a gradual reduction in the redness.
A 74-year-old female with nodules on the left hand and forearm for two months, biopsy performed. The condition discussed is identified as Sporotrichosis, specifically the lymphocutaneous type.

Female, 54 years old, reports having red papules on the buttocks that are itchy. This condition occurs in the summer and improves in the winter, and it has been ongoing for 6 years. Other forum users suggest the condition might be sweat-related. Summer dermatitis is suspected, and a treatment mentioned is the application of aluminum acetate ointment.

The poster, a patient, reported having a small pus-filled bump on the neck area which started six months ago. Initially diagnosed as folliculitis by a local hospital, the condition progressed to ulceration and exudation. The patient mentioned that wherever the exudate flows, complications occur, and the affected area has been increasing. Despite visiting multiple hospitals, the condition has not improved, with some suggesting a fungal infection. Currently, the specific disease remains unidentified. Several potential diagnoses were discussed by forum users, including necrotizing pyoderma and pemphigus. A shift from Western medicine to Traditional Chinese Medicine was suggested, specifically applying herbal poultices. Users have noted the lack of improvement with Western treatment and recommended an urgent biopsy for pathological examination.

The poster is concerned about their vaginal discharge, which appears slightly yellow in the photo but is actually slightly white in reality. The user is questioning if the amount of discharge is excessive or normal, and also mentioning the presence of tiny clumpy or flaky textures similar to those in the photo. Through discussion in the forum, other users suggested that the symptoms could indicate a yeast infection. It was recommended to use clotrimazole pessaries for three days, which was effective. However, after cessation of the treatment and subsequent menstruation, the user reported that excessive discharge continued without odor, was white, and intensified with noticeable white debris after drying. It was suggested that a second course of antifungal treatment should be pursued following menstruation to further address the issue.

The poster shared images of their condition, prominently showing a cluster resembling large grapes and a smaller swelling underneath. The consensus among forum users appears to be that the condition might be soft fibromas, often referred to as skin tags. Several users suggested removal and pathological examination. There were mentions of various potential diagnoses including skin tags, soft fibromas, neurofibromas, and achromic nevus. Treatments discussed include surgical removal and laser treatment.

The user is experiencing papules at the root of the thigh and is inquiring about the type of skin disease. Based on the discussion in the forum, the possible diagnosis discussed is Tinea Corporis. Other users suggested performing a fungal microscopic examination for further verification.

The post concerns a young child without itching or pain. The condition discussed is 窝状角质松解症, or punctate keratolysis. This is typically recognized by its characteristic appearance and the lack of pain and itching symptoms in the patient.

The poster has been experiencing issues with their foot for over six months, characterized by intermittent small bumps that later burst, leading to a messy and painful appearance especially noticeable during a specific period. The condition seems to recur every few days, accompanied by significant itching. Other forum users speculated that the problem might be located on the shin area of the leg, possibly caused by skin dryness. However, the poster confirmed the issue is isolated to a small area on the upper surface of the foot, distinct from other skin areas which are in good condition. The term '胫前' (shin area) was mentioned, and when researched, the poster found the corresponding images distressing, seeking advice on how to manage the condition given its localized nature.

Female, 28 years old, has been experiencing skin symptoms for 2 years. Initially, it was a small spot with peeling skin resembling dandruff. The condition temporarily improved with the use of Pikangwang but deteriorated again later. She has also tried Youzuoer with no success. Recommendations from other users based on shared pictures suggest a high possibility of seborrheic dermatitis and advise visiting a dermatologist. There are concerns about potential side effects from steroids and suggestions that the condition might be steroid-dependent dermatitis. Tacrolimus ointment was mentioned as a possible treatment.

The original poster's demographics, specific symptoms, nature, duration, and medical reasons for posting are not described. The discussion involves potential diagnoses which include sebaceous gland displacement syndrome or multiple sebaceous cysts. No further symptom descriptions, treatments, or other details are provided.

A 65-year-old male reports having a black scabbed patch at the inner corner of the left eye for six months. The discussion in the forum led to a diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma. Treatment and further descriptions of symptoms are not available in the provided excerpts as they were hidden and required higher privilege access to view.

The poster reported a purple swelling on the tongue, which is painless and not itchy, and there is no bleeding. Discussion in the forum suggested that the condition might be a venous lake. Another user suggested it could be a hemangioma and recommended surgical treatment.

The patient is a 27-year-old female experiencing thinning of the skin on the left temporal area and above, which presents as slashed-shaped, with prominent blood vessels and sparse hair. She has a sensation of tightness in the area and has been experiencing these symptoms for over 10 years. A biopsy has confirmed a diagnosis of localized scleroderferma. The discussions in the forum suggest that localized scleroderma is a rare condition, which is not commonly seen in clinical practice. There is an appreciation and gratitude expressed for sharing the case, indicating educational value for other users, but no additional symptoms, diagnosis details, or treatment approaches are discussed.

The user is discussing a skin condition referred to as '增殖性皮炎' or proliferative dermatitis, which is associated with a bacterial infection. The user hopes to use fluorescent immunology and bacterial culture for further diagnosis. The exact nature and duration of symptoms are not specified in the post. Following the discussion, another user suggests that the condition might be hidradenoma and recommends diagnostic medication for confirmation. Specific details on further symptom descriptions, potential diagnoses beyond proliferative dermatitis and hidradenoma, or discussed treatments were not thoroughly expanded upon in the post.

A user posted about their case of Tinea Versicolor (花斑癣), describing typical characteristics of the condition. The discussion in the replies suggests it is a typical case of Tinea Versicolor. Treatments discussed include external application of Western Show Dew (a medicinal product), maintaining a light diet, timely disinfection of clothes and personal items, keeping the skin dry, and avoiding excessive sweating. A question was raised regarding treatment during pregnancy, but no specific answer for this scenario was provided.

The poster is discussing their experience with common warts (寻常疣), including their location on the face and the appearance, which was captured in a photo appreciated by another user for its clarity. Based on the discussions, two treatment options for the common warts were discussed. The first option is freezing (cold treatment), which might require repeated sessions but does not leave scars. The second is electrocautery (burning), which can potentially resolve the issue in one go but might leave scars. The need for patient consent and a clear explanation of these treatments was emphasized.

The poster has been suffering from recurrent penile inflammation for several years. They reported no history of sexually transmitted diseases. Previously, the inflammation would subsume after applying Desonide cream within a few days. Recently, the frequency of episodes has increased, characterized by severe itching. Based on the discussion, the possibility of candidal balanitis was considered by a user, suggesting that the sexual partner should also be treated if this diagnosis is confirmed. The use of question marks indicated uncertainty about the diagnosis, highlighting that it was a suggestion based on visible symptoms rather than a confirmed diagnosis. It is noted that a definitive diagnosis might require laboratory tests or microscopic examination.

The poster reports observing red spots at the urethral opening. They mention no pain or itching associated with these spots. Various users suggested that the red spots might be a mild local inflammation and reassured that it is likely normal. Dietary advice was given to avoid 'heaty' foods to alleviate symptoms. Hygiene recommendations included frequent washing and using specific cleansers. There was a question regarding whether the condition could be a sexually transmitted disease, but no conclusive opinion or evidence was provided in the discussion.

The poster is seeking a diagnosis and treatment suggestions for a toenail condition. They provided a description and an image of the affected toenail, highlighting an abnormal color. The discussion among forum users suggests that the condition is likely onychomycosis (nail fungus). Users recommended various treatments including surgical removal of the severely infected nail, reducing fungal keratinized nail parts, applying terbinafine cream topically, and taking fluconazole orally once a week at a dose of 150 mg until new nail growth is observed. Regular liver function tests were advised to monitor for adverse effects, with discontinuation of medication if abnormalities are detected. Traditional Chinese Medicine perspectives attribute the condition to a deficiency in liver and kidney function, suggesting that inadequate blood flow fails to nourish tendons, leading to dryness.

The poster reports experiencing reddish patches, pustules, pain, and slight itching on the left shoulder and back for two days. The discussion primarily revolves around whether the condition is herpes zoster (shingles) or paederus dermatitis. Several users suggested herpes zoster due to the characteristic sharp, pin-like pain, and the appearance of the rash which is typical of a viral infection affecting nerve pathways. However, paederus dermatitis was also considered by some due to its symptom description and pattern of skin injury which can mimic a scrape-like injury with obvious pus and surrounding inflammation often seen during the summer from insect secretions. There is a disagreement in the diagnosis, but there's a tendency towards herpes zoster based on the distribution and sensation described. Recommendations include topical medication for relief and serological testing for viruses if available.

The user inquired about symptoms they are experiencing, questioning if they might have either pearly penile papules or genital warts. They emphasized having no unsafe sexual contacts. The consensus among the forum users appears to be that the symptoms described by the poster are indicative of pearly penile papules, which are benign and do not require treatment. Multiple users reassured that this condition is normal and does not adversely affect health.

A 30-year-old female, working in a garment factory, reports experiencing small blisters on the radial side of the skin of both index fingers for the past two to three years. Blisters rupture, followed by dry cracking and itching, which worsens during rainy weather, at night, and after washing clothes or dishes. She has tried 'Confucius' ointment and other 'anti-itch' creams without cure. Another user suggested that the condition might be herpes, mentioning a similar experience where the blisters eventually disappeared on their own.

The user reported a patch of light white discoloration on the right anterior chest wall persisting for over twenty days, with episodes of fading and recurrences of new patches. The patches are described as neither painful nor itchy. Discussion on the forum suggests a diagnosis of Tinea Versicolor, also known as Pityriasis Versicolor. For treatment, the use of antifungal creams such as Clotrimazole or steroid creams like Hydrocortisone was recommended.

The poster reports an issue of 血管角皮瘤 (angiokeratoma) located unilaterally. Discussion within the forum suggests that angiokeratoma was the diagnosis. However, there are no further details on symptom description, potential disease diagnoses beyond angiokeratoma, or discussed treatment provided in the interaction.

The poster is a young male who has noticed an issue for over a year with no significant symptoms except for bleeding during times of constipation, which he originally thought was hemorrhoids. Discussions between the poster and other users suggest that the condition is likely to be anal condyloma acuminatum, commonly known as genital warts. Various users have commented on the typical appearance and nature of the condition, recommending diagnosis through acetic acid test and histopathology after excision. Treatment suggestions include laser cauterization and interferon therapy.

The post is about a lesion in the right armpit. The poster does not specify their demographics or detailed symptoms besides the location of the lesion. The discussion among the forum users suggests the lesion might be a common wart or a soft fibroma. Most replies leaned towards it being a soft fibroma, with multiple suggestions mentioning it. The proposed treatments for these conditions included laser treatment and surgical removal.

34-year-old female experiencing itching, erythema, and white patches on the left side of her neck and head for four months. Several possible diagnoses were discussed including fungal infection, ringworm, Sweet's syndrome, contact dermatitis, and possibly tinea corporis. There is a mention of recurrent episodes of erythema. The use of steroids or other localized treatments was denied by the poster. The condition has persisted, suggesting it is likely not an insect-related dermatitis.

A 16-month-old child initially without any skin problems except Mongolian spots at birth developed a coffee-colored spot near the left thigh close to the knee at around 40 days old. Over two months later, further similar spots appeared, especially around the umbilicus after using a coin for treating umbilical hernia. Currently, there are 8-9 such spots, with the larger ones being around 5-6 pieces. The spots are flat, non-painful, and with no symptoms of itching. After research, the parent suspects these spots could be 'cafe-au-lait' spots, which are indicative of neurofibromatosis if six or more spots of at least 15mm are present. There is no family history of similar conditions. The parent saw a dermatologist who suggested observation due to the insufficiently deep color of the spots. A neurologist consulted suggested the spots were not 'cafe-au-lait' spots and recommended watching for any size increase, multiplication, or darkening of the spots. Users in the forum discussed the difficulty in diagnosing these spots from pictures alone and shared personal anecdotes related to similar skin marks, suggesting ongoing observation as these may just be pigmentation. One user shared their own experience with thigh spots diagnosed as pigment deposition that remained unchanged over years after consultation at a hospital.

A patient diagnosed with tinea versicolor, initially asymptomatic, was treated with Pikaung cream (topical) and intravenous fluconazole. The patient had requested infusion therapy and was unsure if the condition was sweat stain or papule-type urticaria, leading the practitioner to use both approaches. Further discussion revealed that intravenous fluconazole was deemed unnecessary, and oral administration was recommended. Additional treatment suggestions included using ketoconazole, either orally or topically, and non-steroidal antifungal creams or sprays. The effectiveness of topical 2% ketoconazole wash or selenium sulfide wash was highlighted. It was advised that topical 2% ketoconazole wash be used once daily for five days or selenium sulfide wash be used at bedtime for a month. Oral itraconazole was recommended at 200mg daily for one week. Diverse opinions emphasized the recurrent and challenging nature of tinea versicolor. Recommendations from the book 'Dermatological Drug Therapy' by Jin Peiying suggested a specific regimen for treating tinea versicolor, including daily use of ketoconazole shampoo for 7-10 days, topical imidazole treatment twice daily for 2-4 weeks, and oral medication with either spironolactone or fluconazole.

Female, 8 years old, without any history of medication, reports a skin lesion that is neither painful nor itchy. The lesion was discussed and analyzed by different users who hypothesized it could be viral herpes or erysipelas based on the appearance in a photograph originally taken during hot weather. There was mention of potential related symptoms such as pain, bitter taste in the mouth, or constipation. The consensus seemed to lean towards erysipelas, and users suggested a prompt visit to the hospital for treatment. One user also mentioned the possibility of lymphangitis.

The poster, a 15-year-old male, reports suffering from a skin condition for over 10 years. He has undergone allergen testing in Beijing, which indicated an allergy to benzo[a]pyrene, mainly sourced from car exhaust and smoke. The diagnosis received from a traditional Chinese medicine hospital in Beijing was dry eczema. He mentions that multiple treatments have been ineffective and seeks advice for better treatment options. Users on the forum suggest that the condition could be specific dermatitis. Recommendations for treatment include using moisturizers to repair the skin barrier function, taking oral antihistamines and immunomodulators. Prescription treatments mentioned include intramuscular injection of Schizonepeta, oral administration of Chinese patent medicine (Itchy Granules), and topical use of ethoxyphenyl bismuth or dandiphenol.

Male, 40 years old, without diabetes, reports an infection following a finger scratch from cutting bamboo. The affected area was itchy leading the patient to frequently squeeze it for over 10 days. Currently, the wound neither hurts nor itches. Discussion in the forum suggests the problem might be a pyogenic granuloma. Multiple users recommend surgical intervention, primarily to explore and possibly remove foreign bodies and to ensure proper healing. Options such as laser treatment or electrosurgery were mentioned. Additionally, users recommended cleaning and debridement of the wound, alongside anti-inflammatory treatments.

A young female experiencing persistent skin itching without any apparent reason, ineffective use of 'Piyenping' cream. Symptoms specifically occur on the front chest, back, arms, and neck. Treatment discussed focuses on reducing swelling, promoting blood circulation, and relieving itchiness. It was also cautioned to avoid arbitrary use of steroid creams and to try traditional Chinese medicine.

A 28-year-old male has been experiencing symptoms for over two years, including itchiness that periodically improves but worsens after consuming seafood or eggs. The patient has tried various ointments with little effect and has been suggested to have eczema or fungal infections. Medical consultations included a pathological examination at a provincial hospital where the patient received an injection costing hundreds, which temporarily alleviated the symptoms for a few months before they returned. Users in the forum suggested the condition might be athlete's foot (tinea pedis) caused by fungal infections, potentially exacerbated by prolonged use of steroid creams. Recommendations include anti-fungal ointments such as Natural Miao ointment or ketoconazole solution for 2-4 weeks, along with systemic antifungals like ketoconazole or griseofulvin for a similar duration. Adjunctive anti-inflammatory treatment was advised, alongside dietary adjustments and avoiding tight non-breathable footwear. Additional suggestions included maintaining hygiene and avoiding local allergens or irritants. Some participants recommended re-testing for fungal cultures to confirm the presence of a fungal infection and ensure appropriate treatment.

The user reports a round lesion on the inner side of the upper arm. The lesion is painful upon touching, with the pain radiating to the elbow. It measures approximately 2 millimeters in diameter. Participants in the forum suggested that the lesion might be a blue nevus, based on its appearance. Other possibilities such as a hemangioma were also mentioned but less likely. Some users recommended biopsy and histopathological examination if there is uncertainty about the diagnosis. Concerns about potential malignant transformation were also discussed.

The user reports a skin condition affecting their upper body, which is neither itchy nor painful. Other forum users suggest the possibility of 'Tinea Versicolor' and recommend seeking a professional diagnosis at a hospital.

The post discusses a user's personal experience or observation of Alopecia Areata (spot baldness) combined with Vitiligo. These health conditions are implied as the main topic given the thread title mentioning both disorders specifically. The discussion in the forum includes a user highlighting that having Vitiligo in the areas of Alopecia Areata might be preferable as it prevents the growth of white hairs in those spots. There is no additional specific discussion about the symptoms, potential disease diagnoses, or discussed treatments other than this opinion.

The poster reports the presence of white spots in the perineum area. Various users suggested different potential diagnoses including genital warts and lichen sclerosus, but there was disagreement and a recommendation for a professional medical examination to obtain a precise diagnosis. Some users also recommended doing the acetic acid white test for further investigation.

The user posted about having small grain-like bumps on their face for over a year. These bumps cause no pain or itching, and some can be squeezed out. Other forum users suggested these could be either milia or miliaria. The consensus tilts towards a diagnosis of syringoma, particularly because the bumps have been present for an extended period without causing pain or itching.

The poster is discussing the topic of pigmented basal cell carcinoma, referred to as '基底细胞癌（色素型）', indicating it's likely their medical condition and reason for the post. The discussion appears to focus on confirming a diagnosis of pigmented basal cell carcinoma. Various users in the thread seem to agree or query about this specific type of cancer, with mentions of basal cell carcinoma and epithelioma. No further symptoms or treatments are specifically detailed in the post.

The user's primary concern revolves around a skin condition related to 斑痣 (nevi or moles), specifically identifying and addressing their appearance. Through discussions in the forum, it was suggested that the skin markings might be 雀斑样痣 (lentigines).

The post is titled 'Erythematous Pemphigoid' and details provided by the poster include a confirmed diagnosis through pathology and Direct Immunofluorescence (DIF). The discussion in the forum included a thank you message for sharing, indicating appreciation or acknowledgment of the information provided. There was an inquiry about photos from other locations, suggesting interest in further visual documentation of the condition.

The post concerns a young girl experiencing an itchy, spreading rash that has lasted for about half a month. Topical application of 'Fubao' has been ineffective in treating the symptoms. The discussion among forum users suggests a consensus that the condition could be tinea cruris (jock itch), a fungal infection. Several users recommend checking for fungal presence and initiating antifungal treatment. There was also a mention that the typicality of the lesions might be altered due to prior use of steroids. Some recommended treatment includes topical antifungals such as Daktarin and formulations containing fluocinolone.

67-year-old male reports partial loss of right eyebrow hair over the past two months, without any associated symptoms, redness, or scaling. The scalp and other hair are normal. Other users discussed and suggested the possibility of alopecia areata as the diagnosis. The absence of other skin lesions seems to support this. Treatments like corticosteroid injections were mentioned. Some replies advised excluding leprosy.

The user posts about experiencing Pityrosporum Folliculitis, seeking advice on how to treat the condition. They mention undergoing microscopy, revealing a high spore count.
