The diagnoses suggested by users include sporotrichosis ('孢子丝菌病') and sebaceous cyst ('皮脂腺囊肿'). Additionally, symptoms indicating 'purulent inflammation' and a 'tendency for infection' led to a recommendation for a routine blood test ('查血常规') to further investigate the condition.

The poster reports a condition of the nails without external injury, which has persisted for several years. The nails grow back in the same condition after being cut. Users in the forum discuss the possibility of the condition being a nail pigmented nevus, with recommendations to observe it regularly to monitor for any changes. Suggestions were made that the poster should consider potential diagnoses of nail matrix nevus or common longitudinal melanonychia. There is concern about the severity of the condition and inquiries about necessary tests, with advice on continued observation.

An 8-year-old boy has been experiencing rashes for over 7 years, which are particularly itchy, occurring day and night and throughout all seasons. The boy has no history of rhinitis or asthma and no family history of these conditions. The initial consideration is atopic dermatitis, with possibilities for other conditions yet to be ruled out. Other users suggested different diagnoses including large vesicular dermatitis, generalized eczema, psoriasis, and chronic eczema, although atopic dermatitis is most frequently mentioned. Treatments discussed include enhancing immune system through exercise, sunbathing, taking traditional Chinese medicine both internally and as external washes, improving dietary habits, and using topical medications for eczema.

The poster, a 30-year-old female, has been experiencing a non-painful, non-itchy growth obstructing her hand while washing her head for over two years. She is concerned whether it might be melanoma. Discussion on the forum suggests that the growth is possibly a sebaceous nevus or a hemangioma, based on its soft texture. There was a suggestion for surgical removal and biopsy to confirm diagnosis. Non-surgical, minimally invasive treatment for hemangioma was also discussed, indicating that typically two treatment sessions could potentially resolve it.

A 20-year-old male manual worker has been experiencing itchy skin on his face for two months. Initially, he did not pay attention to it, but later noticed that the affected area began small and has been gradually expanding. Based on the discussion, several users suggested that the condition resembles tinea (fungal infection) such as facial ringworm and advised checking for fungal presence. Consensus supports the diagnosis of ringworm with recommendations for antifungal treatment specifically mentioning the application of antifungal creams such as Ciclopirox (Loprox) ointment and Clotrimazole ointment. Continuous treatment beyond symptom resolution, extending two to three weeks, is advised to ensure complete cure.

A 60-year-old male reports suffering from severe itching for 2 years, associated with chronic eczema on his lower legs. Discussion in the forum suggests the possibility of 'verrucous eczema,' though one user mentioned never having heard of it. Chronic eczema is confirmed, with complications of impaired venous return in the lower limbs.

The poster describes symptoms that started around the age of 10 after swimming in a river, which gradually spread throughout the body and increased in size, persisting for over 20 years. Several users have suggested potential diagnoses such as nodular prurigo, scabies, and predominantly neurofibromas. Many users have specifically mentioned neurofibromas, referring to its appearance in the shared images along with café-au-lait spots. Neurofibromas have been discussed with different medical terms, reflecting various views on their origin. Despite suggestions, users emphasized the need for further medical examinations and puncture tests to confirm the diagnosis.

The male patient, 53 years old, has been experiencing a skin condition for five years. He has used many topical ointments with poor results and refuses oral medications. No specific tests or treatments for skin diseases have been administered. The patient might be diagnosed with psoriasis. Various users on the forum suggested conditions like chronic eczema and congestion dermatitis related to venous reflux. Treatments discussed include homemade herbal medicine concoctions and potentially using psoriasis-specific creams. Some questioned if underlying conditions such as arteritis, diabetes, or varicose veins are present, but noted that the absence of certain lifestyle details (like smoking and alcohol consumption) and other symptoms makes a definite diagnosis challenging.

The poster mentioned that there are small, water blister-like bumps around the navel area that are not itchy and are increasing in size. Initially, there was one bump observed in the latter half of the previous year, as reported by the child. The child does not feel pain, but scratches them during sleep. Other users in the forum suggest that the symptoms might be consistent with conditions like Glossy Lichen or contact dermatitis, although contact dermatitis was ruled out based on further description. Molluscum contagiosum was also considered but not ruled out. No medications have been used for this condition. Discussions also involved treatment options with external medications, but specific recommendations were not provided.

The user reports small pimple-like spots on their arms and neck that have been present for over four months. These spots remain unchanged over time, are painless, and not itchy, although they slightly redden and itch when sweating. Further discussions indicate difficulty in diagnosing due to unclear photos. One user suggested the possibility of Pityriasis Versicolor.

The user posted asking if spots on their skin could be vitiligo. They specifically mentioned having white spots on both arms. Following the discussion, the consensus among respondents is that the condition is unlikely to be vitiligo. Several users suggested it might be idiopathic guttate hypomelanosis or post-inflammatory hypopigmentation. Due to insufficient medical history provided by the poster, a definitive diagnosis was not reached, and further detailed medical history was advised for accurate diagnosis.

The user posted asking for diagnosis of a skin condition present on their child's face. Multiple users responded confirming that the skin condition is infant eczema, characterizing it as a clear and typical case.

The user reports having an itchy condition on their neck. They have tried using the medications Dermatophagoides, Wujigao, and Yuzhuoer without significant effect. Users in the forum suggested that the condition could be eczema or neurodermatitis. Treatment suggestions included anti-allergy treatment, using a traditional Chinese medicine decoction from Portulaca oleracea for cool compresses, combined with Mometasone cream application, and dietary restrictions such as avoiding fish, shrimp, lamb, and spicy or raw food. Some users recommended using Lumisone cream specifically for neurodermatitis.

The poster is an 11-year-old boy who initially presented with a cold, a decrease in appetite a few days before the onset of the cold, and occasional coughing after recovery from the cold. He developed multiple vesicular rashes concurrent with the cold. There was no mention of pain, but these rashes were itchy. The discussion among the forum members suggests various possible diagnoses including multiform erythema, eczema, shingles (herpes zoster), chickenpox, Malassezia folliculitis, insect bite dermatitis, urticaria, viral rash, autologous sensitivity dermatitis, and drug rash. The dominant consensus points towards shingles, especially because of the morphology and distribution of the rash and the absence of pain but presence of itching. The boy had taken antivirals and initially one dosage of Niuhuang Jiedu tablets, and is currently taking Chlorpheniramine (Pulilin).

The user did not specify their demographic details or personal symptoms. The post title suggests it is a sharing of a typical case of hyphomycosis. The user comments confirm the condition as a typical case of hyphomycosis, which is supported by pathology and culture.
The poster reports noticing symptoms with the onset of hotter weather. Discussion among forum users suggests the possible diagnosis of Tinea Versicolor with recommendations for antifungal treatment.

The poster is new to skincare and believes to have damaged and dehydrated their skin due to rapid commencement of AHA/BHA/retinoids usage. The poster is experiencing very dehydrated skin and mentions concerns about small bumps on their face.

User reports a longstanding raised mole which has recently developed black scabbing. Expresses significant health anxiety and concerns of possible melanoma. User has a dermatology appointment scheduled. Discussion suggests that similar symptoms have been assessed by other users' dermatologists as benign. Suggestions in replies emphasize the importance of a professional skin check for diagnosis and peace of mind. Immunotherapy and potential misdiagnosis concerns due to health anxiety are mentioned.

The poster, over the past 3-4 months, observed changes in a mole which was smaller and without a dark spot initially but has altered significantly, acquiring a darker spot. Additionally, they reported an involuntary weight loss, transitioning from a size 8-10 in jeans to a size 2, and experiencing days of extreme fatigue where they feel incapable of engaging in usual activity. These manifestations are raising concerns about potential melanoma.

The user woke up with itchy rashes located on their stomach and side. The symptoms have lasted over 12 hours without improvement. The discussion in the forum suggests a potential diagnosis of Cholinergic urticaria. However, due to the rash lasting more than 12 hours, users speculated it might be something else as the typical duration for each rash element in urticaria is less than 24 hours.
Red spots bug bites.

The individual reported a 2mm raised skin lesion with no color difference from surrounding skin, containing clear fluid, and slight pain upon contact with other fingers. The lesions are located on each side of a knuckle. A user suggested that the condition might be dyshidrotic eczema; however, the original poster disagreed, noting the lesion does not itch and is only a single bubble, differing from typical presentations of dyshidrotic eczema.

The poster reports that their friend sent a picture of a painful lesion on the hand asking if it was a blister. The lesion developed suddenly and is accompanied by pain. Other users added that the friend also suffers from eczema, suggesting that the lesion might be related to this existing condition. There was a discussion around whether the painful lesion could indeed be eczema or another issue.

The poster is seeking help identifying a skin condition that started 1-2 years ago. They have already consulted one dermatologist and are waiting to see another. Users suggested that the condition may be mild Becker's Nevus or Acanthosis nigricans. Another user mentioned it could be confluent and reticulated papillomatosis. Advice was given to consult a family medicine doctor to check blood sugar levels, cortisol, and other hormones.

The user reports experiencing a painful underarm rash that has persisted for several days. The pain has intensified particularly after sun exposure and the most recent shower. The user suspects the rash may be linked to the use of deodorant or recent shaving activities.

User reports a red and sensitive spot around the lips that has been present for an unknown duration. Condition worsens when eating salty or spicy foods. The user's dermatologist suggested the condition might be related to 'rose acne' but was uncertain. The dermatologist prescribed a cream containing allantoin and dexpanthenol, which has not effectively alleviated the symptoms.

The poster, an individual of unspecified demographics, reports having a rash on their hand characterized by extremely itchy bumps with fluid, appearing in the mornings and slightly visible by night. Additionally, similar smaller bumps appeared on the ring finger and upper lip, suggesting a recurring issue. The poster is worried this might be related to a known allergy to poison ivy, but suspects that if it were, the reaction would be more severe. The discussion between the poster and other forum users suggests the rash might be dyshidrotic eczema. One user with similar symptoms and a diagnosis of dyshidrotic eczema reports a reduction in itching due to daily antihistamine use. The poster recognizes they have eczema primarily on their face and neck, which aligns with the suggested diagnosis. Treatments like over-the-counter Allegra and hydrocortisone creams, along with stress management techniques such as yoga, were recommended by peers. The poster plans to try these treatments.

The patient reports having red itchy bumps on their feet, which started on the previous Saturday. This condition had also appeared in 2019. The patient initially speculated bed bugs as a cause due to travel history; however, this cause was ruled out as the patient's spouse, sharing the same bed, did not exhibit symptoms.

The patient reports having lesions for over a month on the inner thigh, initially suspected to be Molluscum Contagiosum (MC). Upon attempting to pop one lesion, it behaved differently from a typical spot, leaving open skin without fluid leakage. The lesions are neither itchy nor sore.

The poster reports observing a darkening spot on their toenail which was noticed last week and has progressively become darker. The individual does not recall any trauma to the toe but acknowledges the possibility of an unnoticed injury. Users in the forum suggest that the darkening could be due to a blood clot, potentially caused by either trauma like kicking something hard during physical activities such as playing football, or wearing tight shoes. It is noted that the clot tends to darken over time and eventually becomes hard, potentially requiring removal. Furthermore, others advise that changes in nail color and texture can also be indicative of more serious conditions, such as cancer, and recommend consulting a doctor for a professional assessment.
The poster, a user of unspecified demographics, reports having blisters in a line with another line starting below. The blisters do not itch but produce a sensation that 'kinda burns.'
